# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

### Q1. Define Food Security. List its four pillars as given by the FAO. **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Food security, as defined at the **World Food Summit, Rome (1996)**, exists when all people, at all times, have physical, social and economic access to sufficient, safe and nutritious food that meets their dietary needs and food preferences for an active and healthy life. The four pillars are:

1. **Physical Availability** of food
2. **Economic and Physical Access** to food
3. **Food Utilization** (proper diet, clean water, health)
4. **Stability** of the above three dimensions over time.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between Food Security and Nutrition Security. Give two examples of government schemes that address each. **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Food security concerns the **quantity and continuity** of food supply (caloric sufficiency), whereas nutrition security concerns the **quality of diet and the body's ability to use the nutrients** (micronutrient adequacy, dietary diversity).

- **Food Security** schemes: Public Distribution System (PDS) under the **National Food Security Act, 2013**; **Pradhan Mantri Garib Kalyan Anna Yojana (PMGKY)**.
- **Nutrition Security** schemes: **POSHAN Abhiyaan (PM's Overarching Scheme for Holistic Nutrition, 2018)**; **Mid-Day Meal Scheme**; **ICDS Supplementary Nutrition Programme**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Module 1, 14 Mark Pattern]**

---

### Question A — Q1(a) (7 Marks)

**Explain in detail the four pillars of food security as defined by the FAO. With suitable Indian examples, illustrate how each pillar is operationalised in Indian policy.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The FAO 1996 framework defines food security along four dimensions. A country or household is "food secure" only when all four are simultaneously satisfied.

**1. Physical Availability of Food:**
This refers to the physical presence of food in adequate quantity, of appropriate quality, and from domestic production, imports, or food aid. In India, the **Food Corporation of India (FCI)** procures foodgrains at the **Minimum Support Price (MSP)** from farmers, maintains central and state buffer stocks, and ensures that approximately **600+ lakh tonnes of foodgrains** are physically available for distribution. The Public Distribution System (PDS) ensures that food is *physically* moved to over **5.4 lakh Fair Price Shops** across the country.

**2. Economic and Physical Access to Food:**
Even if food is available, people must be able to *reach* it. This depends on purchasing power, market infrastructure, and social protection. In India, the **National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013** covers up to **75 % of the rural population and 50 % of the urban population** under the Targeted PDS, providing 5 kg of foodgrains per person per month at ₹ 1–3/kg. The **MGNREGA** supplements rural income, and the **Antyodaya Anna Yojana** provides 35 kg per family per month to the poorest at ₹ 2–3/kg.

**3. Food Utilization:**
Utilization requires dietary diversity, clean drinking water, sanitation, and primary health care. India's response includes the **Mid-Day Meal Scheme** (hot cooked meals to ~12 crore children), the **ICDS Supplementary Nutrition Programme**, the **Swachh Bharat Mission** for sanitation, and the **POSHAN Abhiyaan** (2018), which targets **stunting, undernutrition, anaemia, and low birth weight** through convergence of 9 ministries.

**4. Stability:**
Stability means the household must not lose food access due to sudden shocks. The **FCI maintains strategic reserves** of wheat and rice, and the **National Food Security Act, 2013** guarantees uninterrupted supply even during droughts. **Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY)** insures crops against climate shocks, indirectly stabilizing farmer incomes.

**Conclusion:** The four pillars function as a chain — the weakest link defines the household's food security. A village may have excellent availability (PDS shop present) but poor utilization (no clean water, no toilet) — such a village is still food insecure.

---

### Question A — Q1(b) (7 Marks)

**Define the Global Hunger Index (GHI). A country reports the following data: Proportion of Undernourished = 18 %, Under-5 Underweight = 22 %, Under-5 Mortality = 5 %. Calculate the GHI and interpret the severity.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
The **Global Hunger Index (GHI)** is a composite statistical tool developed by the **International Food Policy Research Institute (IFPRI)** that measures and tracks hunger at global, regional, and national levels. Since 2015, it combines three equally weighted indicators:

1. **Inadequate food supply** (proportion of undernourished population).
2. **Child undernutrition** (under-5 underweight prevalence).
3. **Child mortality** (under-5 mortality rate).

**Formula (post-2015):**

$$
\text{GHI} = \frac{1}{3}\left(\text{PUN} + \text{CU} + \text{CM}\right)
$$

All three values are expressed on a 0–100 scale (higher = worse).

**Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GHI} &= \frac{1}{3}\left(18 + 22 + 5\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{3}\left(45\right) \\
&= 15.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Severity Interpretation:**

| GHI Range | Severity |
|:---------:|:---------|
| $\leq 9.9$ | Low |
| 10.0 – 19.9 | **Moderate** ← our country |
| 20.0 – 34.9 | Serious |
| 35.0 – 49.9 | Alarming |
| $\geq 50.0$ | Extremely Alarming |

**Conclusion:** The country scores **15.0 → Moderate Hunger**, which is comparable to India's 2023 score of **28.7 (Serious)**. This indicates that despite moderate food availability, significant gaps remain in **child nutrition and mortality reduction**.

---

### Question B — Q2(a) — Alternative Choice (7 Marks)

**Discuss the concept of "Hidden Hunger" in India. How does it differ from the classical concept of hunger (calorie deficiency)? List four micronutrient deficiencies of public-health importance in India and the FSSAI-mandated fortification strategies for each.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Hidden Hunger (Micronutrient Deficiency):**
Hidden hunger refers to a state in which a person consumes sufficient **calories** (energy) but lacks essential **micronutrients** — vitamins and minerals. The hunger is "hidden" because the person may appear physically normal, but the body is functionally deficient.

**Difference from Classical Hunger:**

| Parameter | Classical Hunger | Hidden Hunger |
|:----------|:-----------------|:--------------|
| **Cause** | Insufficient total calories | Insufficient micronutrients |
| **Body weight** | Visibly underweight | Often normal or overweight |
| **Detection** | Visible | Requires blood test |
| **Affected population** | Poor, famine-affected | All income groups |
| **Indian prevalence** | 14.96 % (FAO 2022) | 50–80 % (NFHS-5, varied) |

**Four Micronutrient Deficiencies of Public-Health Importance in India:**

1. **Iron Deficiency → Anaemia:** 67.1 % of children and 57 % of women (NFHS-5).  
   *FSSAI Strategy:* **Iron and Folic Acid (IFA) fortification** of wheat flour, rice, double-fortified salt (iron + iodine), NDEAR IFA tablets under Anemia Mukt Bharat.

2. **Vitamin A Deficiency:** Affects ~2 % of children under 5; leads to night blindness.  
   *FSSAI Strategy:* **Vitamin A fortification of edible oil and milk**, and biannual Vitamin A supplementation for children aged 9–59 months.

3. **Iodine Deficiency → Goitre, Cretinism:** 76 % of households use adequately iodised salt (NFHS-5).  
   *FSSAI Strategy:* Mandatory **iodisation of edible salt** under the **Food Safety and Standards (Fortification of Foods) Regulations, 2018**.

4. **Vitamin B12 and Folic Acid Deficiency:** Neural tube defects, anaemia.  
   *FSSAI Strategy:* **Fortified rice with B12, Iron, and Folic Acid** (POSHAN Scheme), and the **F+ logo** to identify fortified staples.

**Conclusion:** Hidden hunger is a "silent epidemic" requiring fortification, dietary diversity, and supplementation, alongside FSSAI's mandatory standards.

---

### Question B — Q2(b) — Alternative Choice (7 Marks)

**"Food Security and Nutrition Security are necessary but not sufficient for each other." Critically analyse this statement with at least four supporting arguments and two Indian policy illustrations.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:**
The statement captures the **causal-but-non-identical** relationship between the two concepts. The FAO recognizes that a person can be food-secure (calories met) but nutrition-insecure (micronutrients deficient), and vice versa.

**Argument 1 — Food Secure but Nutrition Insecure:**
The classic Indian case is the **rural poor who survive on polished white rice** (e.g., in coastal Andhra Pradesh, Odisha, Assam). They are calorically adequate (2000–2200 kcal/day) but suffer from **Beriberi (B1 deficiency)**, **Pellagra (B3 deficiency)**, and **Iron deficiency anaemia**. They are **food secure** but **nutrition insecure**.

**Argument 2 — Nutrition Secure but Not Food Secure:**
A tribal family in the Western Ghats may consume a **highly diverse diet of millets, forest greens, and pulses** (nutrition secure), but in a bad monsoon year the *quantity* of food available drops below 2100 kcal/person/day, making them **food insecure** (under-nourished).

**Argument 3 — The "Triple Burden of Malnutrition":**
India simultaneously faces **undernutrition (stunting 35.5 %, wasting 19.3 % per NFHS-5)**, **micronutrient deficiency**, and **overnutrition (23.9 % of women overweight/obese)**. The triple burden demonstrates that calorie-focused food security alone cannot solve the problem — targeted nutrition interventions are essential.

**Argument 4 — Different Lead Ministries:**
Food security in India is primarily handled by the **Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food and Public Distribution (FCI, PDS)**, while nutrition security is handled by the **Ministry of Women and Child Development (ICDS, POSHAN)** and **Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (NRHM)**. This institutional separation itself proves that the two are not the same.

**Indian Policy Illustration 1:**
The **National Food Security Act, 2013** provides grain but does not address micronutrient quality. The **Food Safety and Standards (Fortification) Regulations, 2018** mandate fortification of rice, wheat flour, oil, and salt with Iron, Folic Acid, Vitamin B12, and Vitamin A — a nutrition-specific response.

**Indian Policy Illustration 2:**
The **POSHAN Abhiyaan (2018)** uses **Anganwadi Centres (AWCs)** to provide **Take-Home Ration (THR) and hot cooked meals** rich in protein, vitamins, and iron, specifically addressing nutritional quality beyond mere calories.

**Conclusion:**
The two concepts must be pursued **in tandem**. Pure food security without nutrition focus leads to **hidden hunger**; pure nutrition programmes without food security (calories) fail to address under-nourishment. Modern Indian policy, especially after the 2013 NFSA, deliberately integrates both.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Where students lose marks in this topic — KTU Examiner's Pitfall List:**  
> 
> 1. **Quoting the OLD 1974 definition of Food Security** ("food availability at the national level"). The 1996 FAO definition has FOUR pillars; quoting a pre-1996 definition costs 2 marks outright.  
> 2. **Confusing Food Security with Nutrition Security.** Even bright students swap "utilization" and "absorption" — remember: **Food security = quantity; Nutrition security = quality + utilization + behaviour.**  
> 3. **Forgetting the difference between PUN (Proportion Undernourished) and CU (Child Underweight).** PUN is population-wide; CU is specifically for under-5 children.  
> 4. **Unit mistakes in GHI calculation:** not converting percentages to a 0–100 scale before averaging, or using the old formula with the new thresholds.  
> 5. **Writing the BMI formula as $w \times h^{2}$ instead of $w / h^{2}$.** This is the single most common calculation error in KTU answer sheets.  
> 6. **Forgetting to convert kcal to grams** in the protein-requirement problem (energy density of protein is 4 kcal/g, not 9 — that is fat).  
> 7. **Ignoring the Asia-Pacific BMI threshold** in the Indian context. WHO Western thresholds over-classify Asians as "Normal" when they are metabolically obese.  
> 8. **Vague answers to "list four micronutrient deficiencies"** — KTU expects **specific names**: Iron, Iodine, Vitamin A, Vitamin B12, Zinc, Folic Acid.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before ESE):**
> 
> - **Food Security** = FAO, 1996 World Food Summit, Rome.  
> - **Four Pillars:** Availability, Access, Utilization, Stability.  
> - **Nutrition Security** = ICN, 1992. Three A's: Availability, Access, Absorption.  
> - **Hidden Hunger** = micronutrient deficiency despite calorie adequacy.  
> - **Triple Burden of Malnutrition** = undernutrition + micronutrient deficiency + overnutrition.  
> - **GHI = (PUN + CU + CM) / 3** (post-2015 IFPRI).  
> - **GHI Severity:** ≤ 9.9 Low, 10–19.9 Moderate, 20–34.9 Serious, 35–49.9 Alarming, ≥ 50 Extremely Alarming.  
> - **India GHI 2023 = 28.7, Rank 111/125** ("Serious").  
> - **BMI = w / h²** (kg / m²). Asia-Pacific: 18.5–22.9 Normal.  
> - **Energy density:** Protein = 4 kcal/g, Carbs = 4 kcal/g, Fat = 9 kcal/g.  
> - **RDA Calorie (ICMR 2020):** Sedentary Male = 2400 kcal/day, Female = 1900 kcal/day.  
> - **RDA Iron (ICMR 2020):** Adult Female = 21 mg/day; Pregnancy = 35 mg/day.  
> - **NFSA 2013:** 5 kg/person/month, 75 % rural, 50 % urban coverage, ₹ 1–3/kg.  
> - **Antyodaya Anna Yojana (AAY):** 35 kg/family/month, poorest of the poor.  
> - **POSHAN Abhiyaan (2018):** PM's Overarching Scheme for Holistic Nutrition; reduces stunting, undernutrition, anaemia, low birth weight.  
> - **FSSAI (Fortification of Foods) Regulations, 2018:** mandatory +15 + F logo norms.  
> - **Triple-Fortified Salt** = Iodine + Iron + Vitamin A.  
> - **F+ Logo:** Indian symbol for fortified foods (rice, wheat flour, oil, salt, milk).  
> - **Mid-Day Meal Scheme** is a **legal entitlement** under Section 4 of NFSA, 2013.  
> - **Stunting** = Height-for-Age < -2 SD (chronic undernutrition).  
> - **Wasting** = Weight-for-Height < -2 SD (acute undernutrition).  
> - **India NFHS-5:** Stunting 35.5 %, Wasting 19.3 %, Anaemia (children) 67.1 %, Anaemia (women) 57 %.  
> - **Food Security ≠ Food Self-Sufficiency** — a country can be food secure through trade even without domestic production.  
> - **Hunger is fundamentally a political, economic, and distributive problem** — not an aggregate supply problem.  
> - **The 4 A's (Availability, Access, Absorption, Agency)** — recent UN-CSA frameworks add "Agency" as a 4th A for women's empowerment.  
> - **Key UN body:** FAO, IFPRI, WFP, WHO, UNICEF, CFS (Committee on World Food Security).  
> - **SDG 2:** Zero Hunger by 2030 — India's progress tracked via GHI and SOFI.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indian Food Regulatory Regime (Existing & Old)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT723 - FOOD LAWS AND LEGISLATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Indian Food Regulatory Regime (Existing & Old)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Indian Food Regulatory Regime** refers to the comprehensive, statutory, and administrative framework of laws, rules, regulations, standards, and enforcement bodies established by the Government of India to ensure the **safety, hygiene, wholesomeness, authenticity, and fair trade practices** of food manufactured, processed, stored, distributed, and sold within the country. In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, this regime is studied under two broad temporal classifications: the **Old Regime** (a fragmented, multi-ministry, multi-act framework pre-2006) and the **Existing Regime** (a unified, single-window, integrated framework introduced via the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, administered by the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India – **FSSAI**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish between the *Old Regime Acts* (PFA, 1954; FPO, 1955; etc.) and the *Existing Regime Act* (FSS Act, 2006). Examiners frequently frame questions asking for "comparative analysis" or "evolution" — both are high-weightage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **traffic system** in a city:
- The **Old Regime** was like a city where traffic rules were split among **multiple disconnected traffic policemen** standing at different intersections — one for trucks, one for cars, one for buses — each following his own rulebook. A food company had to satisfy **eight different ministries** to launch a single product. This created duplication, confusion, and regulatory chaos.
- The **Existing Regime** is like installing a **single smart traffic control system** — one unified command center (FSSAI) that monitors all vehicles (food products) using one rulebook. The product now needs only **one license**, with everything — labelling, additives, contaminants, packaging — governed under **one integrated statute**.

In short, the Indian food regulatory regime evolved from a **multi-agency, fragmented, punitive structure** to a **single-window, science-based, preventive, and consumer-centric structure**.

### 1.3 Key Statutory Constants and Metrics

The regime operates on a few foundational numerical benchmarks that recur in KTU examinations:

- **FSS Act, 2006**: Section **63** prescribes a maximum fine of **₹10 lakh** for selling substandard food.
- **FSS Act, 2006**: Section **16(3)** mandates a maximum limit of **15** notified food additives without specific clearance.
- **FSSAI Headquarters**: Located at **FDA Bhawan, Kotla Road, New Delhi**.
- **FSSAI CEO Term**: **5 years** (or until the age of **65**, whichever is earlier).
- **PFA, 1954** was operational for **52 years** (1954 to August 5, 2011).
- **Number of central acts subsumed under FSS Act, 2006**: **8 principal acts** and **1 regulation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Definition:** *"The Indian Food Regulatory Regime is the statutory and administrative superstructure comprising the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, its rules and regulations, and FSSAI, designed to protect consumer health and ensure fair trade in food."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Old Regulatory Regime (Pre-FSS Act, 2006)

Before 2006, food safety in India was governed by a **patchwork of multiple central enactments** administered by **different ministries and departments**. This led to overlapping jurisdictions, contradictory standards, and limited consumer protection.

#### 2.1.1 Principal Acts of the Old Regime

- **Prevention of Food Adulteration Act (PFA), 1954**
  - Administered by the **Ministry of Health & Family Welfare**.
  - Primary objective: prevention of adulteration of food articles.
  - Central focus: **PUNISHMENT** of offenders rather than prevention of unsafe food.
  - Defined "adulterated food" under Section 2(i) and "misbranded food" under Section 2(ix).
  - Standards were framed by the **Central Committee for Food Standards (CCFS)** under the Director General of Health Services (DGHS).
  - Limited scope: did not cover food standards comprehensively, food labelling in detail, food fortification, GM foods, functional foods, or nutraceuticals.

- **Fruit Products Order (FPO), 1955**
  - Administered under the **Essential Commodities Act, 1955** by the **Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI)**.
  - Regulated processed fruits, vegetables, juices, jams, jellies, pickles, and similar products.
  - Mandated **compulsory registration** of manufacturing units.
  - Specified quality standards, packaging requirements, and labelling rules for fruit-based products.

- **Meat Food Products Order (MFPO), 1973**
  - Administered by **MoFPI** under the **Essential Commodities Act**.
  - Regulated manufacture, quality control, and distribution of meat food products.
  - Required licensing and hygienic processing conditions for meat handling.

- **Milk and Milk Products Order (MMPO), 1992**
  - Administered by the **Department of Animal Husbandry & Dairying, Ministry of Agriculture**.
  - Regulated the production, supply, and distribution of milk and milk products.
  - Categorized milk plants based on handling capacity:
    - Category I: handling **up to 10,000 litres per day**.
    - Category II: handling **10,000 to 50,000 litres per day**.
    - Category III: handling **more than 50,000 litres per day**.
  - Required registration for units handling more than **10,000 litres/day**.

- **Vegetable Oil Products (Regulation) Order (VOPO), 1998**
  - Regulated manufacture, distribution, and sale of vegetable oil products.
  - Prescribed standards for refined oils, hydrogenated oils, and processed oil products.

- **Edible Oils Packaging (Regulation) Order, 1998**
  - Made it **mandatory** to pack edible oils in **sealed containers** of specific capacities (commonly **15 kg** for retail; bulk permitted for institutional buyers).
  - Prohibited the sale of loose edible oils to prevent adulteration.

- **Solvent Extracted Oil, De-oiled Meal and Edible Flour (Control) Order, 1967**
  - Regulated the solvent extraction industry and ensured the quality of solvent-extracted oils.

- **Standards of Weights and Measures (Packaged Commodities) Rules, 1977** (under the Standards of Weights and Measures Act, 1976)
  - Governed the **labelling of pre-packaged food** with respect to net weight, volume, name of the commodity, and manufacturer details.

- **Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 1986 (BIS Act)**
  - Established the **Bureau of Indian Standards** (BIS, formerly ISI) for standardization and certification.
  - Issued the **ISI Mark** for voluntary and certain mandatory food product categories.

- **Export (Quality Control and Inspection) Act, 1963**
  - Governed quality control and inspection of food and other goods meant for **export**.
  - Empowered agencies to inspect export consignments.

- **Essential Commodities Act, 1955**
  - The **parent act** under which orders like FPO, MFPO, MMPO, VOPO, and the Solvent Extracted Oil Order were framed.
  - Used primarily for **price control and supply regulation** of essential commodities including food.

#### 2.1.2 Critical Limitations of the Old Regime

- **Fragmentation**: Multiple ministries (Health, Food Processing, Agriculture, Civil Supplies) regulated different food categories, causing jurisdictional overlap.
- **Punitive Approach**: The PFA was designed to **punish adulteration** rather than prevent the entry of unsafe food into the market.
- **Limited Scope**: Did not cover GM foods, functional foods, organic foods, health supplements, nutraceuticals, irradiation, or food fortification in a comprehensive manner.
- **Slow Standards Updating**: Standards under the PFA were revised infrequently and could not keep pace with scientific developments.
- **Low Penalties**: Maximum imprisonment under PFA was **3 years** (and 6 years for repeat offenders causing grievous injury), with a fine of **₹1,000** — generally considered too lenient.
- **No Recall Mechanism**: The PFA had no provision for a mandatory food recall.
- **Weak Consumer Voice**: No formal mechanism for consumer representation in standard-setting.
- **No Single Licensing**: Manufacturers had to obtain **multiple licenses** from different authorities (FPO licence from MoFPI, BIS certification, MMPO registration, etc.).

### 2.2 The Existing Regulatory Regime (Post-FSS Act, 2006)

#### 2.2.1 The Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006

The FSS Act, 2006 was enacted by the Indian Parliament and received Presidential assent on **August 23, 2006**. It was brought into force on **August 5, 2011** through a notification, simultaneously repealing the **PFA, 1954** and the **Essential Commodities Act provisions** relating to food.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Statistic:** The FSS Act, 2006 **subsumed 8 central acts and 1 regulation** to create a single, unified framework.

The 8 acts subsumed were:
1. Prevention of Food Adulteration Act, 1954
2. Fruit Products Order, 1955
3. Meat Food Products Order, 1973
4. Milk and Milk Products Order, 1992
5. Vegetable Oil Products (Regulation) Order, 1998
6. Edible Oils Packaging (Regulation) Order, 1998
7. Solvent Extracted Oil, De-oiled Meal and Edible Flour (Control) Order, 1967
8. Standards of Weights and Measures (Enforcement) Act, 1985 (partially)
9. Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 1986 (partially)

#### 2.2.2 The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)

FSSAI is the **principal regulatory body** established under **Section 4** of the FSS Act, 2006. It is a **statutory autonomous body** under the **Ministry of Health & Family Welfare**, Government of India.

**Key Features of FSSAI:**
- Headquartered at **FDA Bhawan, Kotla Road, New Delhi**.
- Has **regional offices** across the country.
- Chaired by the **Chairperson** and headed by the **Chief Executive Officer (CEO)**.
- The **Scientific Committee** and **Panels** advise on scientific matters.
- The **Scientific Panel** consists of a maximum of **11 members** (including a chairperson) as per FSSAI regulations.
- There are **21 scientific panels** covering different food categories.

**Functions of FSSAI (Section 16):**
- Framing of regulations and standards (Food Safety and Standards Regulations, **FSSR 2011**).
- Granting **FSSAI Licenses** and **Registration** to food business operators (FBOs).
- Laying down **procedure and guidelines** for accreditation of laboratories.
- Ensuring **food recall** procedures.
- Promoting **food safety awareness** and training.
- Setting up **training programs** for FBOs and food safety officers.
- Maintaining a **national information network** for food safety.
- Coordinating with **international bodies** like Codex Alimentarius Commission, WHO, FAO.

**Two-Tier Licensing System:**
- **Registration**: Mandatory for **petty food manufacturers** with turnover up to **₹12 lakhs per annum** (now revised to **₹25 lakhs per annum** as per recent FSSAI guidelines for certain categories). This is done with the **local authority**.
- **License**: Mandatory for all other FBOs. **State License** is issued by the State Food Safety Authority; **Central License** is issued by the **Central Licensing Authority (CEO of FSSAI)** for FBOs having turnover **above ₹20 crore per annum** or operating in multiple states or engaged in specific high-risk activities.

#### 2.2.3 Salient Features of the FSS Act, 2006

- **Single Regulatory Window**: All food safety laws under one statute.
- **Shift from Punitive to Preventive**: Focuses on **preventing unsafe food** from entering the market through licensing, audits, and testing.
- **Risk-Based Approach**: Food is regulated based on its **risk classification**.
- **Defined Penalties**:
  - Selling substandard food: fine up to **₹5 lakh** (Section 63).
  - Misbranded food: fine up to **₹10 lakh** (Section 63).
  - Adulterated food (causing injury): imprisonment **6 months to life**, fine up to **₹10 lakh** (Section 59).
  - Adulterated food (causing death): minimum imprisonment **7 years**, may extend to **life**, fine not less than **₹10 lakh** (Section 59).
- **Food Recall**: Section 28 empowers the Food Safety Officer to order **recall** of food articles.
- **Consumer Representation**: Two members of the **Scientific Committee and Panels** are nominated to represent **consumer interests**.
- **Special Courts**: Designated under **Section 42** for speedy trial of food safety offences.
- **Food Safety and Standards Regulations, 2011 (FSSR 2011)**: Framed under Section 92, covering 15+ schedules relating to:
  - **Schedule I**: General principles of food safety and hygiene.
  - **Schedule II**: Limits for contaminants, toxins, and naturally occurring toxic substances.
  - **Schedule III**: List of food additives and their permitted limits.
  - **Schedule IV**: Microbiological requirements.
  - **Schedule V**: Packaging and labelling requirements.
  - **Schedule VI**: Standards for proprietary foods.
  - **Schedule VII**: Standards for supplements, nutraceuticals, health supplements, etc.
  - **Schedule VIII**: Standards for irradiated food.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Old Regime (Pre-2006) | Existing Regime (FSS Act, 2006) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Anchor Act** | Prevention of Food Adulteration Act, 1954 | Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 |
| **Regulator** | Multiple ministries (Health, FPI, Agri) | Single body — FSSAI |
| **Approach** | Punitive (post-incident) | Preventive & science-based |
| **Coverage** | Limited (adulteration focus) | Comprehensive (safety, standards, labelling, recall) |
| **Licensing** | Multiple licenses from different departments | Single FSSAI License/Registration |
| **Penalties** | Up to ₹1,000 fine, 3 years imprisonment | Up to ₹10 lakh fine, life imprisonment |
| **Standards Body** | CCFS, BIS, AGMARK (fragmented) | FSSAI (unified) |
| **Consumer Voice** | Negligible | Represented in Scientific Committee |
| **Scope of Food** | Conventional foods only | Includes GM food, nutraceuticals, functional food, organics |
| **Recall Provision** | Absent | Section 28 (mandatory recall) |
| **Number of Acts** | 8+ separate enactments | 1 unified act subsuming 8 acts |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Regulatory Regime

- **Public Health Protection**: Prevents foodborne illnesses — **600 million** people fall sick annually due to contaminated food globally (WHO data).
- **Trade Facilitation**: Harmonization with **Codex Alimentarius** standards supports India's food export industry (which crossed **USD 50 billion** in recent years).
- **Consumer Confidence**: FSSAI certification and the **green dot** (vegetarian) and **brown dot** (non-vegetarian) labelling symbols have become trusted consumer cues.
- **Industry Compliance**: Single-window licensing has reduced the compliance burden on FBOs and improved the ease of doing business.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked about the "transition from old to existing regime," always structure your answer along **three axes**: *Philosophical shift* (punitive → preventive), *Structural shift* (fragmented → unified), and *Scope expansion* (conventional foods → modern food categories including GM and functional foods).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: Evolution & Analytical Decomposition

### 3.1 Analytical Framework: Transition from Old to Existing Regime

Since this is a law and policy subject, the "derivation" here takes the form of a **step-by-step analytical framework** that explains *why* and *how* the Indian food regulatory regime evolved from the old fragmented structure to the existing unified structure. This analytical scaffolding is precisely the kind of structured reasoning that KTU examiners reward with full marks in Part B (14-mark) questions.

**Step 1: Identifying the Problem in the Old Regime**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Problem} &= \text{Fragmented Legislation} \oplus \text{Punitive Approach} \oplus \text{Outdated Standards} \\
&\quad \oplus \text{Limited Coverage} \oplus \text{Multiple Licensing Authorities} \\
&\quad \oplus \text{Weak Penalties} \oplus \text{No Recall Mechanism}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation*: The Old Regime (PFA 1954 + FPO 1955 + MFPO 1973 + MMPO 1992 + others) suffered from systemic fragmentation. Food businesses fell under overlapping jurisdictions, and the PFA's primary focus was on punishing adulteration after the harmful food had already entered the market. This reactive posture was scientifically inadequate for a rapidly modernizing food industry.

**Step 2: Recognition of Need for Reform**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Drivers of Reform} &= \text{Globalization} \oplus \text{Codex Harmonization Pressure} \oplus \text{WTO Obligations} \\
&\quad \oplus \text{Rise of Modern Food Categories (GM, Functional, Organic)} \\
&\quad \oplus \text{Consumer Rights Movement} \oplus \text{Supreme Court Directives}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation*: In the post-liberalization era (post-1991), India's food sector expanded rapidly. International trade under the WTO framework required harmonization with Codex standards. The rise of GM foods, functional foods, and nutraceuticals demanded a regulatory architecture the PFA could not provide. The Joint Parliamentary Committee and various expert committees advocated for a single integrated statute.

**Step 3: Drafting and Enactment**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Process} &= \text{Expert Committee Recommendations} \rightarrow \text{Cabinet Approval} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Parliamentary Debate} \rightarrow \text{Presidential Assent (Aug 23, 2006)} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Notification for Enforcement (Aug 5, 2011)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation*: The FSS Bill was introduced in the Lok Sabha, debated, and the FSS Act, 2006 received Presidential assent on **23 August 2006**. However, the Act was only brought into force on **5 August 2011**, after FSSAI was constituted and the FSSR 2011 regulations were framed. This 5-year transition period was essential for setting up institutions, training officers, and drafting regulations.

**Step 4: Institutional Setup (FSSAI)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSSAI Composition (Section 5)} &= \text{Chairperson} + \text{CEO} + \text{7 ex-officio members} \\
&\quad + \text{2 non-official members from consumer organizations} \\
&\quad + \text{2 food industry representatives} \\
&\quad + \text{2 subject matter experts} \\
&\quad + \text{1 representative from a farmers' organization} \\
&\quad + \text{1 representative from a retailer organization}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation*: The statutory composition of FSSAI deliberately balances **government, scientific, industry, consumer, farmer, and retailer** voices — a marked departure from the PFA regime where the **Central Committee for Food Standards (CCFS)** was an almost exclusively bureaucratic body.

**Step 5: Framing Regulations and Implementation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSSR 2011} &= \text{15+ Schedules} \oplus \text{Licensing & Registration Regulations} \\
&\quad \oplus \text{Labelling & Packaging Regulations} \oplus \text{Additive Regulations} \\
&\quad \oplus \text{Contaminant Regulations} \oplus \text{Recall Regulations}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation*: Section 92 of the FSS Act empowers the FSSAI to frame regulations, leading to the **Food Safety and Standards Regulations, 2011 (FSSR 2011)** — a comprehensive regulatory document covering all 15+ schedules. This is the **operational backbone** of the existing regime.

**Step 6: Repeal of Old Acts**

$$
\text{Repeal} = \text{Section 97(1)} \rightarrow \text{8 Central Acts automatically repealed upon FSS Act enforcement}
$$

*Explanation*: Section 97(1) of the FSS Act explicitly repeals the eight enumerated central acts, ensuring there is no overlapping jurisdiction. Anything done under the old acts, however, continues to be valid as if done under the FSS Act (savings clause under Section 97(3)).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Penalties Under Both Regimes

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Offence} &\quad | \quad \textbf{Old Regime (PFA)} \quad | \quad \textbf{Existing Regime (FSS Act, 2006)} \\
\hline
\text{Selling adulterated food (no injury)} &\quad | \quad \text{Up to 6 months jail + ₹1,000 fine} \quad | \quad \text{Up to 3 years jail + ₹2 lakh fine (Sec 59)} \\
\text{Selling adulterated food (causing injury)} &\quad | \quad \text{Up to 3 years jail + ₹1,000 fine} \quad | \quad \text{6 months to life + ₹10 lakh fine (Sec 59)} \\
\text{Selling adulterated food (causing death)} &\quad | \quad \text{Up to 6 years jail (repeat)} \quad | \quad \text{Min 7 years to life + min ₹10 lakh (Sec 59)} \\
\text{Selling substandard food} &\quad | \quad \text{Not distinctly defined} \quad | \quad \text{Up to 6 months jail + ₹5 lakh fine (Sec 63)} \\
\text{Misbranded food} &\quad | \quad \text{Up to 3 years jail + ₹1,000 fine} \quad | \quad \text{Up to 1 year jail + ₹10 lakh fine (Sec 63)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Note*: The above table has been rendered with `\mid` separators to prevent markdown table breakage.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Pattern:** A 14-mark question can be asked as: *"Discuss the major changes brought about by the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, in comparison with the earlier regulatory regime."* The analytical matrix above gives you the ready structure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

**Question 1: Define the Indian Food Regulatory Regime. Name the principal Act governing food safety in India today.**
*Source: [KTU University Exam - Model Question, OEFTT723]*

**Model Answer:**
The Indian Food Regulatory Regime is the integrated statutory and administrative framework established to ensure food safety, hygiene, fair trade, and consumer protection in India. The principal Act governing food safety in India today is the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006**, administered by the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)**, headquartered in New Delhi. The Act was brought into force on **5 August 2011** and subsumed eight earlier central acts to create a unified, single-window regulatory system.

**Question 2: List any four central acts that were subsumed under the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006.**
*Source: [KTU University Exam - Model Question, OEFTT723]*

**Model Answer:**
Four central acts subsumed under the FSS Act, 2006 are:
1. **Prevention of Food Adulteration Act, 1954**
2. **Fruit Products Order, 1955**
3. **Meat Food Products Order, 1973**
4. **Milk and Milk Products Order, 1992**

(Other valid answers: Vegetable Oil Products (Regulation) Order, 1998; Edible Oils Packaging (Regulation) Order, 1998; Solvent Extracted Oil, De-oiled Meal and Edible Flour (Control) Order, 1967; Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 1986; Standards of Weights and Measures Act, 1976.)

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 1 Internal Choice

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
*Source: [KTU University Exam - Sessional Model Paper, OEFTT723]*

Discuss the structure and functions of the Indian Food Regulatory Regime under the old framework (pre-2006), highlighting its key limitations.

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the key old-regime statutes and the ministries that administered them.**

**Model Solution:**

The old Indian food regulatory regime, prior to the enactment of the FSS Act, 2006, was characterized by a **fragmented, multi-agency, multi-ministry** structure. The following were the principal statutes:

1. **Prevention of Food Adulteration Act (PFA), 1954** — Administered by the **Ministry of Health and Family Welfare**. It defined "adulterated food" and "misbranded food" and prescribed penalties. Standards were framed by the **Central Committee for Food Standards (CCFS)** under the Director General of Health Services (DGHS).

2. **Fruit Products Order (FPO), 1955** — Framed under the **Essential Commodities Act, 1955** and administered by the **Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI)**. It regulated the manufacture, quality, and labelling of processed fruit and vegetable products.

3. **Meat Food Products Order (MFPO), 1973** — Administered by MoFPI under the **Essential Commodities Act**. It regulated quality, hygiene, and licensing of meat food products.

4. **Milk and Milk Products Order (MMPO), 1992** — Administered by the **Department of Animal Husbandry & Dairying, Ministry of Agriculture**. It categorized milk plants into three categories (up to 10,000 litres/day; 10,000–50,000 litres/day; above 50,000 litres/day) and required registration for larger plants.

5. **Vegetable Oil Products (Regulation) Order (VOPO), 1998** — Regulated vegetable oil products, including quality and packaging standards.

6. **Edible Oils Packaging (Regulation) Order, 1998** — Mandated packaging of edible oils in **sealed containers of prescribed capacities** to prevent adulteration.

7. **Solvent Extracted Oil, De-oiled Meal and Edible Flour (Control) Order, 1967** — Governed solvent extraction processes and product quality.

8. **Standards of Weights and Measures (Packaged Commodities) Rules, 1977** — Under the Standards of Weights and Measures Act, 1976, administered by the **Ministry of Consumer Affairs**, governing labelling of pre-packaged food.

9. **Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 1986 (BIS Act)** — Established the BIS for standardization and certification (ISI Mark).

10. **Export (Quality Control and Inspection) Act, 1963** — Governed quality of food for export, administered by the **Ministry of Commerce**.

**(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the key limitations of the old regulatory regime that necessitated reform.**

**Model Solution:**

The old regime suffered from several structural and substantive limitations:

1. **Fragmentation and Jurisdictional Overlap**: Multiple ministries (Health, FPI, Agriculture, Consumer Affairs) regulated different food categories, often issuing conflicting or duplicating standards. A single FBO could be governed by 4–6 different ministries simultaneously.

2. **Punitive Rather Than Preventive Approach**: The PFA's primary objective was to *punish* adulteration after it occurred, rather than *prevent* unsafe food from entering the market. There were no mandatory preventive controls like HACCP, GHP, or GMP.

3. **Outdated and Inflexible Standards**: Standards under the PFA were revised infrequently, failing to keep pace with rapid advances in food science, processing technology, and international standards (Codex Alimentarius).

4. **Limited Scope of Coverage**: The PFA and the related orders did not adequately cover **GM foods, functional foods, nutraceuticals, health supplements, organic foods, food irradiation, and food fortification**.

5. **Weak Penalties**: The maximum fine under the PFA was only **₹1,000** with imprisonment up to 3 years. These penalties were widely considered too lenient to act as effective deterrents.

6. **No Recall Mechanism**: The PFA had no provision for a **mandatory food recall**, which meant unsafe food products could not be efficiently removed from the market.

7. **No Consumer Representation**: The CCFS was a largely bureaucratic body with negligible representation of consumers, scientists, or industry stakeholders.

8. **Multiple Licensing Burden**: FBOs had to obtain **multiple licenses** from different authorities, increasing compliance cost, time, and corruption risk.

9. **No Single Definition of "Unsafe Food"**: The PFA focused on "adulteration" as the trigger for prosecution, but did not clearly define "unsafe food" in a science-based, risk-based manner.

These limitations collectively necessitated the enactment of a unified, modern, science-based statute — the **FSS Act, 2006**.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
*Source: [KTU University Exam - Sessional Model Paper, OEFTT723]*

Explain the salient features and structure of the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) under the existing regulatory regime.

**(a) [7 Marks] Discuss the salient features of the FSS Act, 2006, that distinguish it from the old regime.**

**Model Solution:**

The FSS Act, 2006 introduced several salient features that fundamentally transformed the Indian food regulatory landscape:

1. **Single, Integrated Statute**: The Act subsumed **8 central acts and 1 regulation** to create a single, unified law governing all food safety matters in India.

2. **Establishment of FSSAI**: A dedicated, autonomous statutory body — the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** — was created under **Section 4** to function as the single regulatory authority.

3. **Shift from Punitive to Preventive Approach**: The Act emphasizes the **prevention of unsafe food** through licensing, registration, audits, mandatory compliance with FSSR 2011, and risk-based inspection.

4. **Science-Based Standard Setting**: Standards are framed by the **Scientific Committee** and **21 Scientific Panels** composed of independent scientists, ensuring evidence-based regulation.

5. **Comprehensive Coverage**: The Act covers **GM foods, functional foods, nutraceuticals, health supplements, organic foods, irradiated food, and food fortification**, which were largely uncovered under the PFA regime.

6. **Stricter Penalties**: Penalties have been significantly enhanced — up to **₹10 lakh fine** and **life imprisonment** for causing death by adulterated food (Section 59). This acts as a strong deterrent.

7. **Food Recall Mechanism**: **Section 28** empowers the Food Safety Officer to order **recall of food articles** found unsafe, enabling rapid removal of harmful products from the market.

8. **Consumer Representation**: The Act mandates **representation of consumer organizations** in the Scientific Committee and Panels, ensuring that consumer voices inform standard-setting.

9. **Special Courts**: **Section 42** provides for **designated Special Courts** for speedy trial of food safety offences, replacing the slow, overburdened regular courts used under the PFA.

10. **Food Safety and Standards Regulations, 2011 (FSSR 2011)**: Operationalized through **15+ schedules** covering general principles, contaminants, additives, microbiological standards, labelling, packaging, and proprietary food standards.

11. **Two-Tier Licensing System**: A simplified **Registration** (for petty FBOs) and **Licensing** (Central and State) regime, replacing the multi-license burden of the old regime.

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe the composition, functions, and licensing architecture of FSSAI.**

**Model Solution:**

**Composition of FSSAI (Section 5):**

FSSAI consists of a **Chairperson**, a **Chief Executive Officer (CEO)**, and the following members:
- **7 ex-officio members** representing various central ministries (Health, Agriculture, Food Processing, Commerce, etc.).
- **2 non-official members from consumer organizations** or recognized consumer rights bodies.
- **2 representatives of the food industry**.
- **2 subject matter experts** from relevant scientific fields.
- **1 representative from a farmers' organization**.
- **1 representative from a retailers' organization**.

**Functions of FSSAI (Section 16):**
- Frame **regulations and standards** for food safety.
- Grant **FSSAI Licenses** and approve **Registration** of FBOs.
- Specify **licensing conditions** and procedures.
- **Accredit food testing laboratories**.
- Develop **food recall procedures**.
- Conduct **food safety audits** and inspections.
- Promote **consumer awareness** and training.
- Coordinate with international bodies such as **Codex Alimentarius Commission, WHO, FAO**.
- Maintain a **national information network** for rapid dissemination of food safety data.

**Licensing Architecture:**

FSSAI operates a **two-tier licensing system**:
- **Registration**: Mandatory for **petty food manufacturers** with turnover up to **₹12 lakhs per annum** (or as revised by FSSAI for specific categories). Obtained from the **local authority** (e.g., municipality).
- **State License**: Issued by the **State Food Safety Authority** for FBOs with turnover above the petty threshold but below **₹20 crore per annum**, or for units operating within a single state.
- **Central License**: Issued by the **Central Licensing Authority (the CEO of FSSAI)** for FBOs with turnover **above ₹20 crore per annum**, or those operating in **multiple states**, or engaged in specific high-risk activities (e.g., dairy processing, food exports, large-scale manufacturing).

This single-window licensing architecture has been a major relief for FBOs compared to the fragmented system of the old regime.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1**: Students often state that "FSS Act 2006 repealed 10 acts." The correct number is **8 central acts** (and 1 regulation). Do not overstate the number.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2**: Confusing the dates. The FSS Act received **Presidential assent on 23 August 2006** but was **enforced on 5 August 2011**. Both dates are important.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3**: Stating that FSSAI is a department under the Ministry. FSSAI is an **autonomous statutory body** under the administrative supervision of the Ministry of Health.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4**: Forgetting the **food recall** provision under **Section 28** — this is a frequently asked, high-scoring point.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5**: In Part B answers, students describe but fail to **compare** the two regimes. Always end with a **comparative table or summary** for full marks.
> 6. **Common Mistake 6**: Misquoting the maximum penalty. It is **₹10 lakh and life imprisonment** (not 1 lakh) under the FSS Act for causing death by adulterated food.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist: Indian Food Regulatory Regime (Old & Existing)**

- **Old Regime Anchor**: **Prevention of Food Adulteration (PFA) Act, 1954** — punitive, fragmented, multiple ministries.
- **Existing Regime Anchor**: **Food Safety and Standards Act (FSS Act), 2006** — preventive, integrated, FSSAI as single regulator.
- **FSS Act Assent Date**: **23 August 2006**; **Enforcement Date**: **5 August 2011**.
- **Acts Subsumed under FSS Act**: **8 central acts** + **1 regulation** (total 9).
- **Key Old Acts to Remember**: PFA 1954, FPO 1955, MFPO 1973, MMPO 1992, VOPO 1998, Edible Oils Packaging Order 1998, Solvent Extracted Oil Order 1967, BIS Act 1986.
- **FSSAI Composition**: Chairperson + CEO + 7 ex-officio + 2 consumer + 2 industry + 2 experts + 1 farmer + 1 retailer.
- **FSSAI Function**: Frame standards, license, accredit labs, recall, train, coordinate with international bodies.
- **FSSR 2011 Schedules**: 15+ schedules covering additives, contaminants, microbiological standards, labelling, packaging, proprietary food, nutraceuticals, irradiated food.
- **Two-Tier Licensing**: Registration (petty FBOs, turnover up to ₹12 lakhs) and License (State or Central, based on turnover and operations).
- **Central License**: Issued by CEO of FSSAI for FBOs with turnover **> ₹20 crore** or multi-state operations.
- **State License**: Issued by State Food Safety Authority for FBOs operating within one state.
- **Section 28 of FSS Act**: Powers of Food Safety Officer — includes **food recall** and seizure.
- **Section 59 (FSS Act)**: Penalty for adulterated food causing injury or death — **up to life imprisonment** and **fine of ₹10 lakh or more**.
- **Section 63 (FSS Act)**: Penalty for substandard or misbranded food — fine up to **₹5–10 lakh** and imprisonment up to **6–12 months**.
- **Section 42 (FSS Act)**: Provision for **Special Courts** for speedy trial of food safety offences.
- **PFA 1954 Penalty (Old Regime)**: Max fine ₹1,000, imprisonment up to **3 years** (6 years for repeat offenders causing grievous injury).
- **Maximum Fine under FSS Act**: **₹10 lakh** (for adulterated food causing death, minimum ₹10 lakh).
- **Special Symbols**: FSSAI mandates **Green Dot** (vegetarian) and **Brown Dot** (non-vegetarian) on all packaged food labels.
- **Three Philosophical Shifts**: (i) Punitive → Preventive; (ii) Fragmented → Unified; (iii) Conventional food only → Modern food categories included.
- **International Coordination**: FSSAI represents India at the **Codex Alimentarius Commission**.
- **FSSAI Head Office**: **FDA Bhawan, Kotla Road, New Delhi**.
- **Number of Scientific Panels under FSSAI**: **21 panels** covering different food categories and risk areas.

**One-Line Memory Mnemonics:**
- *"**PFA 1954 was Punitive, Fragmented, and Past; FSSAI 2006 is Preventive, Unified, and Present.**"*
- *"**Eight acts entered; one FSSAI emerged.**"*
- *"**5 August 2011 — The Date Indian Food Law Became One.**"*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FSSAI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT723 - FOOD LAWS AND LEGISLATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** FSSAI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** is the apex statutory body established under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (FSS Act, 2006)** for laying down science-based standards for articles of food and to regulate their manufacture, storage, distribution, sale and import to ensure availability of safe and wholesome food for human consumption.

The Authority was **constituted on 5th September 2008** and its operational headquarters is located at **FDA Bhawan, Kotla Road, New Delhi – 110002**. FSSAI functions under the administrative control of the **Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statutory Reference:** FSSAI derives its legal mandate from Section 4 of the FSS Act, 2006, which mandates the Central Government to establish the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India to discharge the functions assigned to it under the Act.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine FSSAI as the **"Traffic Police of the Indian Food Highway."** Just as traffic police:

- **Set the rules** (speed limits = food safety standards)
- **Issue licenses** (driving licenses = FSSAI licenses)
- **Monitor violations** (checking drunk driving = checking adulteration)
- **Impose penalties** (challans = fines and imprisonment)
- **Ensure public safety** (accident prevention = food-borne illness prevention)

Similarly, FSSAI acts as the **central regulatory authority** ensuring every food item reaching a consumer's plate — from the farmer's field to the restaurant table — is **safe, hygienic, and truthfully labeled**.

## 1.3 Genesis of FSSAI – Historical Evolution

Before FSSAI, India had **multiple fragmented food laws** administered by different authorities. The major pre-FSSAI laws included:

| Pre-FSSAI Act | Year | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| Prevention of Food Adulteration Act (PFA) | 1954 | Adulteration & misbranding |
| Fruit Products Order (FPO) | 1955 | Processed fruits & vegetables |
| Meat Food Products Order (MFPO) | 1973 | Meat products |
| Milk and Milk Products Order (MMPO) | 1992 | Dairy industry regulation |
| Vegetable Oil Products Order (VOPO) | 1998 | Edible oils & fats |
| Edible Oils Packaging Order | 1998 | Bulk edible oil packaging |
| Solvent Extracted Oil Order | 1967 | Solvent extracted oils |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All the above eight laws were repealed** when the FSS Act, 2006 came into force, consolidating them into a single integrated statute. This was a watershed moment in Indian food regulatory history.

## 1.4 Vision and Mission

- **Vision:** To inspire trust and confidence in food safety for all consumers.
- **Mission:** To engage a diverse group of stakeholders in the development of food safety standards, and to ensure their effective implementation through scientifically robust and transparent regulatory frameworks.

## 1.5 FSSAI Logo and Its Symbolism

The FSSAI logo features a **circular emblem** with the inscription "FSSAI" and a **stylized "F"** that symbolizes the food pyramid. It represents:
- **Green** – Natural & safe food
- **Blue** – Trust and regulatory integrity
- **The circular form** – Continuous vigilance

> [!NOTE]
> Every food business operator (FBO) in India is required to display the **FSSAI logo and license/registration number (14-digit)** on the label of all packaged food products, providing traceability to the source manufacturer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Organizational Structure of FSSAI

FSSAI operates through a **three-tier governance architecture**:

### 2.1.1 Apex Level – The Authority (Central Body)

The Authority is the **principal governing body** comprising:

1. **Chairperson** – Appointed by the Central Government
2. **Members (total 22)** – Including:
   - Seven ex-officio members representing various Central Ministries
   - Two representatives from the food industry
   - Three representatives from consumer organizations
   - Three representatives from relevant research bodies
   - Seven independent members

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Chief Executive Officer (CEO)** of FSSAI is appointed by the Central Government and reports directly to the Chairperson. The CEO is the principal executive authority responsible for day-to-day administration.

### 2.1.2 Functional Level – Scientific Panels and Committees

- **Scientific Panels (21 panels)** – Domain experts in specialized areas like:
  - Cereals, pulses & bakery products
  - Fruits & vegetables
  - Milk & milk products
  - Meat & fish products
  - Beverages
  - Food additives, contaminants & toxins
  - Pesticide residues
  - Biological hazards
  - Labelling & claims
  - Nutraceuticals & functional foods
  - Genetically modified organisms
  - Water & beverages

- **Scientific Committee** – Coordinates and harmonizes the work of all 21 Scientific Panels.

### 2.1.3 Implementation Level – State Authorities and Food Safety Officers

- **State Food Safety Authorities** – Established by each State Government under Section 30 of the FSS Act, 2006.
- **Food Safety Officer (FSO)** – Designated officer under Section 37 empowered to inspect, seize, and draw samples.
- **Designated Officer (DO)** – Empowered to issue licenses, cancel licenses, and receive reports of food safety incidents.

## 2.2 Key Functions of FSSAI (Section 16 of FSS Act, 2006)

FSSAI discharges the following statutory functions:

1. **Framing of Regulations** – Laying down food safety standards and guidelines.
2. **Licensing & Registration** – Granting licenses to FBOs and registering them as per the licensing regime.
3. **Enforcement** – Ensuring compliance with food safety standards across the supply chain.
4. **Testing & Laboratory Network** – Establishing and accrediting food testing laboratories.
5. **Capacity Building** – Training food safety officers, food analysts, and FBOs.
6. **Public Awareness** – Conducting campaigns to promote food safety awareness among consumers.
7. **Risk Assessment** – Conducting studies and surveys on food safety risks.
8. **International Coordination** – Liaising with international bodies like Codex Alimentarius Commission, WHO, FAO, WTO, and FSSAI's counterparts globally.
9. **Compilation of Data** – Maintaining a database of food businesses, contaminants, and standards.
10. **Special Functions** – Encouraging research, providing technical assistance, and promoting safe food practices.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes all key numerical thresholds, eligibility criteria, and statutory provisions that frequently appear in KTU examinations.

| Parameter | Threshold / Value | Governing Section |
|---|---|---|
| **Establishment of FSSAI** | 5th September 2008 | Section 4 |
| **Number of Members in Authority** | 22 (1 Chairperson + 22 Members) | Section 5 |
| **Tenure of Chairperson** | 5 Years (or until age 65) | Section 6 |
| **Number of Scientific Panels** | 21 Panels | Section 13 |
| **License fee – Manufacturer (₹)** | ₹7,500 (1 Year) / ₹15,000 (3 Years) / ₹30,000 (5 Years) | FSS (Licensing) Reg. |
| **License fee – Re-labeler (₹)** | ₹7,500 / ₹15,000 / ₹30,000 | FSS (Licensing) Reg. |
| **Registration fee – Petty FBO (₹)** | ₹100 | FSS (Licensing) Reg. |
| **License – Turnover ≤ ₹12 lakh/yr** | Registration only (Petty FBO) | Section 31 |
| **License – Turnover > ₹12 lakh/yr** | State License | Section 31 |
| **License – Turnover > ₹20 crore/yr** | Central License | Section 31 |
| **Number of FSSAI digits** | 14-digit unique ID | FSS (Packaging) Reg. |
| **Penalty for unhygienic processing** | Up to ₹5 lakh | Section 49 |
| **Penalty for misbranded food** | Up to ₹3 lakh | Section 49 |
| **Penalty for sub-standard food** | Up to ₹5 lakh | Section 49 |
| **Adjudication by whom** | Designated Officer (DO) | Section 68 |
| **Appeal against DO's order** | First Class Judicial Magistrate | Section 70 |
| **Appellate Tribunal** | Food Safety Appellate Tribunal (FSAT) | Section 70 |
| **Final Appellate Authority** | High Court | Section 100 |
| **Special Court** | Court of Session designated under Section 80 | Section 80 |

## 2.4 Licensing and Registration Regime

### 2.4.1 The Three-Tier Licensing System

The FSSAI licensing regime is structured on a **dual-pillar model** based on **turnover and business activity**:

$$\text{Classification} = f(\text{Turnover}, \text{Business Activity})$$

Where the classification determines whether a Food Business Operator (FBO) needs **Registration** or a **State/Central License**.

| Category | Turnover Criteria | Authority Issuing | Document |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Petty Food Manufacturer** | Up to ₹12 lakh/yr OR Annual production ≤ 50,000 L (milk) OR ≤ 2,500 MT (others) | Registering Authority | **Registration Certificate** |
| **State License** | Turnover > ₹12 lakh but ≤ ₹20 crore | Designated Officer (State) | **State License** |
| **Central License** | Turnover > ₹20 crore OR 100% EOU OR specific categories | Designated Officer (Central) | **Central License** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific Categories mandatorily requiring Central License** (regardless of turnover): Milk collection/chilling centers with capacity > 50,000 L/day, slaughterhouses with > 50 large animals/day, meat processing plants with capacity > 150 MT/day, 100% export-oriented units, importers, and food business operators in SEZs.

### 2.4.2 Validity Periods

$$\text{Validity}_{\text{Registration}} = 1 \text{ to } 5 \text{ years (renewable)}$$

$$\text{Validity}_{\text{License}} = 1, 2, 3, 4, \text{ or } 5 \text{ years (renewable)}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Utility and Industry Applications

FSSAI's framework has revolutionized India's food sector in several measurable ways:

1. **Consumer Protection** – Ensures ~140 crore Indians have access to safe food.
2. **Export Facilitation** – Harmonization with Codex standards boosts international trade.
3. **Single Window Compliance** – Replaced 8 laws with 1 integrated statute.
4. **Traceability** – 14-digit FSSAI number enables recall management.
5. **Innovation Enablement** – Regulatory pathways for novel foods, GM foods, nutraceuticals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Backing:** All standards, regulations, and orders issued by FSSAI are notified under the parent FSS Act, 2006, and have the force of law. Non-compliance is punishable under Sections 48 to 67 of the Act.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Classification of Food Business Operators (FBOs)

Since the FSSAI licensing framework is **deterministic and rule-based**, it can be derived logically. The following step-by-step decision tree illustrates how an FBO is classified:

### Step 1: Identify the Type of FBO Activity

The FSS (Licensing and Registration of Food Business) Regulations, 2011 classify FBOs into the following categories:

- **Petty Food Manufacturer**
- **Food Manufacturer / Processor**
- **Re-labeler / Re-packer**
- **Food Retailer / Wholesaler**
- **Food Distributor / Supplier**
- **Storage (Cold/Non-Cold)**
- **Transporter**
- **Caterer / Hotel / Restaurant**
- **Marketer / Exporter / Importer**
- **E-Commerce Food Business Operator**

### Step 2: Apply the Turnover Threshold

For manufacturers and processors, the following conditional logic is applied:

$$\text{If Turnover} \leq 12 \text{ lakh AND Volume} \leq \text{Limit} \Rightarrow \text{Registration (Petty FBO)}$$

$$\text{If Turnover} > 12 \text{ lakh AND Turnover} \leq 20 \text{ crore} \Rightarrow \text{State License}$$

$$\text{If Turnover} > 20 \text{ crore OR Mandated Category} \Rightarrow \text{Central License}$$

### Step 3: Determine Document Submission

| Document Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Form A** | Application for Registration |
| **Form B** | Application for State License |
| **Form C** | Application for Central License |

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Penalties under FSS Act, 2006

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivation step shows the **graduated penalty structure** for various food safety violations. Understanding this matrix is critical for KTU questions on enforcement and adjudication.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of Penalty Categories

The FSS Act, 2006 categorizes food safety violations into **six distinct offence classes**, each attracting differential penalties.

$$\text{Offence Class} = \begin{cases} \text{Class 1: Unhygienic / Unsanitary processing} \\ \text{Class 2: Sub-standard food} \\ \text{Class 3: Misbranded food} \\ \text{Class 4: Misleading advertisement} \\ \text{Class 5: Food containing extraneous matter} \\ \text{Class 6: Adulterated food} \end{cases}$$

The penalty computation follows:

$$P_{\text{offence}} = P_{\text{base}} \times (1 + n_{\text{repeat}})$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{base}}$ = Base penalty for the offence class
- $n_{\text{repeat}}$ = Number of repeat convictions (integer ≥ 0)

### 3.2.2 Penalty Schedule (Section 49, FSS Act 2006)

| Offence Class | First Offender (₹) | Repeat Offender (₹) | Imprisonment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unhygienic / Unsanitary** | Up to **₹5 lakh** | Up to **₹10 lakh** | Up to 6 months |
| **Sub-standard food** | Up to **₹5 lakh** | Up to **₹10 lakh** | Up to 6 months |
| **Misbranded food** | Up to **₹3 lakh** | Up to **₹5 lakh** | Up to 6 months |
| **Misleading advertisement** | Up to **₹10 lakh** | Up to **₹50 lakh** | Up to 3 years |
| **Food with extraneous matter** | Up to **₹1 lakh** | Up to **₹3 lakh** | Up to 6 months |
| **Adulterated food** | Min **₹1 lakh** – Max **₹10 lakh** | Min **₹2 lakh** – Max **₹15 lakh** | Min 6 months – Max 7 years + life (if death) |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Procedural Flow for Prosecution

The FSS Act establishes a **three-tier quasi-judicial mechanism** for handling violations:

$$
\text{Violation Detected} \rightarrow \text{FSO Inspection} \rightarrow \text{Sample Lifted} \rightarrow \text{Lab Analysis}
$$

$$
\text{Sub-standard} \rightarrow \text{Designated Officer} \rightarrow \text{Improvement Notice}
$$

$$
\text{Adulterated} \rightarrow \text{Designated Officer} \rightarrow \text{Adjudication} \rightarrow \text{FSAT Appeal} \rightarrow \text{High Court}
$$

### 3.4 Derivative: The 14-Digit FSSAI Number Encoding

The unique 14-digit FSSAI license/registration number follows a **structured encoding scheme**:

$$\text{FSSAI Number} = \underbrace{D_1 D_2}_{State Code} + \underbrace{D_3 D_4 D_5 D_6 D_7 D_8 D_9 D_10 D_11 D_12 D_13 D_14}_{Unique Business Identifier + Checksum}$$

Where:
- $D_1 D_2$ = State/UT code (2 digits)
- $D_3$ to $D_{12}$ = Unique FBO identification
- $D_{13} D_{14}$ = Category code (01 for Registration, 02–15 for various license types)

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Example:** A Kerala-based petty FBO might have the number "10XXXXXXXXXXXX" where "10" is the code for Kerala. The full 14-digit number is mandatory on all food labels under the FSS (Labelling & Display) Regulations, 2020.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Organizational Hierarchy Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the complete governance hierarchy of FSSAI from the Central Government down to the local Food Safety Officer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ministry of Health and Family Welfare] --> B[Food Safety and Standards Authority of India]
    B --> C1[Chairperson]
    B --> C2[Chief Executive Officer CEO]
    B --> C3[Authority Members 22]
    C2 --> D1[Scientific Committee]
    C2 --> D2[21 Scientific Panels]
    C2 --> D3[Central Advisory Committee]
    C2 --> E1[State Food Safety Authorities]
    E1 --> E2[Designated Officer]
    E1 --> E3[Food Safety Officer]
    E1 --> E4[Food Analyst]
    C2 --> F1[Reference Laboratories]
    C2 --> F2[Accredited Private Labs]
    C2 --> F3[FSSAI Regional Offices]
    F3 --> G1[Food Business Operators FBOs]
```

## 4.2 Three-Tier Licensing Decision Flow

The following block diagram illustrates the **deterministic decision tree** for classifying an FBO under the FSSAI licensing regime.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[FBO Commences Operation] --> q1{Is Annual Turnover less than or equal to 12 lakh rupees}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Is Production Volume within petty limits}
    q1 -- No --> q3{Is Annual Turnover greater than 20 crore rupees}
    q2 -- Yes --> reg[Registration Certificate Form A Validity 1 to 5 years]
    q2 -- No --> state
    q3 -- Yes --> q4{Is FBO a mandated category for Central License}
    q3 -- No --> state
    q4 -- Yes --> central[Central License Form C Issued by Central DO]
    q4 -- No --> state[State License Form B Issued by State DO]
    reg --> display[Display 14 digit FSSAI Number on Premises and Labels]
    state --> display
    central --> display
```

## 4.3 Statutory Prosecution Flow Block Diagram

The following diagram depicts the **adjudication and appellate structure** under the FSS Act, 2006.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inspect[Food Safety Officer Inspects Premises] --> lift[FSO Lifts Sample under Section 47]
    lift --> analyze[Food Analyst Tests Sample in NABL Lab]
    analyze --> decision{Is Sample Sub Standard or Adulterated}
    decision -- Sub Standard --> notice[Designated Officer Issues Improvement Notice]
    decision -- Adulterated --> adjud[Adjudication by Designated Officer under Section 68]
    notice --> comply[FBO Complies within Stipulated Time]
    comply --> close[Case Closed]
    adjud --> penalty[Penalty Imposed under Section 49]
    penalty --> appeal1[Appeal to First Class Magistrate Section 70]
    appeal1 --> appeal2[Further Appeal to FSAT Food Safety Appellate Tribunal]
    appeal2 --> final[Final Appeal to High Court under Section 100]
```

## 4.4 FSSAI Function Architecture

The following block-level functional architecture maps the **eight primary functional domains** of FSSAI.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FSSAI[FSSAI Apex Body] --> F1[Standards Framing Function]
    FSSAI --> F2[Licensing and Registration Function]
    FSSAI --> F3[Enforcement and Inspection Function]
    FSSAI --> F4[Testing and Laboratory Network Function]
    FSSAI --> F5[Capacity Building and Training Function]
    FSSAI --> F6[Public Awareness and Consumer Education Function]
    FSSAI --> F7[International Cooperation Function]
    FSSAI --> F8[Risk Assessment and Surveillance Function]
    F1 --> O1[Food Safety and Standards FSS Regulations]
    F2 --> O2[FSS Licensing and Registration Regulations 2011]
    F3 --> O3[State Authorities and FSOs]
    F4 --> O4[NABL Accredited Lab Network]
    F5 --> O5[FoSTaC Training and Certification]
    F6 --> O6[Eat Right India Movement]
    F7 --> O7[Codex Alimentarius WHO FAO WTO Engagement]
    F8 --> O8[INFoSS NET Network for Surveillance]
```

## 4.5 Evolution Schematic: Pre-FSSAI to Post-FSSAI

The following schematic captures the **consolidation journey** from multiple legacy laws to the unified FSSAI regime.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    legacy1[Prevention of Food Adulteration Act 1954] --> merger
    legacy2[Fruit Products Order 1955] --> merger
    legacy3[Meat Food Products Order 1973] --> merger
    legacy4[Milk and Milk Products Order 1992] --> merger
    legacy5[Vegetable Oil Products Order 1998] --> merger
    legacy6[Edible Oils Packaging Order 1998] --> merger
    legacy7[Solvent Extracted Oil Order 1967] --> merger
    legacy8[Standards of Weights and Measures Act 1976] --> merger
    merger[All Legacy Laws Repealed 5th September 2008] --> act[Food Safety and Standards Act 2006]
    act --> fssai[Food Safety and Standards Authority of India FSSAI]
    fssai --> unified[Single Unified Regulatory Framework]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define FSSAI and state its establishment date and headquarters.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

FSSAI stands for the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India**. It is the apex statutory body established under **Section 4 of the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006**, for laying down science-based standards for articles of food and to regulate their manufacture, storage, distribution, sale, and import.

- **Date of Constitution:** 5th September 2008
- **Operational Headquarters:** FDA Bhawan, Kotla Road, New Delhi – 110002
- **Administrative Ministry:** Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India

> [!NOTE]
> The FSSAI was constituted to consolidate **eight pre-existing food laws** into a single integrated regulatory framework, ensuring availability of safe and wholesome food for human consumption.

---

### Question 2: State the three-tier licensing structure of FSSAI with turnover thresholds.

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

FSSAI's licensing regime operates on a **three-tier structure** based on annual turnover of the Food Business Operator:

$$\text{Tier 1: Registration} \Rightarrow \text{Turnover} \leq 12 \text{ lakh/yr (Petty FBO)}$$

$$\text{Tier 2: State License} \Rightarrow 12 \text{ lakh} < \text{Turnover} \leq 20 \text{ crore/yr}$$

$$\text{Tier 3: Central License} \Rightarrow \text{Turnover} > 20 \text{ crore/yr OR Mandated Categories}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Specific categories (milk collection centers > 50,000 L/day, slaughterhouses > 50 large animals/day, 100% EOUs, importers) mandatorily require a **Central License regardless of turnover**.

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A: (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**"Discuss in detail the genesis, organizational structure, functions, and powers of the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) under the FSS Act, 2006."**

#### Part (a) – Genesis and Organizational Structure of FSSAI (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Genesis:**

Prior to FSSAI, the food regulatory landscape in India was fragmented across **multiple overlapping laws** administered by different authorities. The **Prevention of Food Adulteration Act, 1954 (PFA Act)** was the principal law but proved inadequate for modern food safety challenges. The increasing complexity of the food supply chain, globalization, and the need for science-based standards necessitated a unified approach.

The **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006** was enacted by the Indian Parliament and notified on **24th August 2006**. The Act consolidated **eight pre-existing food laws** and provided for the constitution of FSSAI, which was **operationalized on 5th September 2008**.

**Organizational Structure:**

The FSSAI structure is a **three-tier system**:

**Tier 1 – Apex Authority (Section 5):**

- **Chairperson** (appointed by Central Government)
- **CEO** (appointed by Central Government)
- **22 Members** comprising:
  - 7 ex-officio members (representing various Ministries)
  - 7 independent members
  - 2 from food industry
  - 3 from consumer organizations
  - 3 from research/academic bodies

**Tier 2 – Scientific Committees and Panels (Sections 13 & 14):**

- 1 **Scientific Committee** for coordination
- 21 **Scientific Panels** for domain-specific standard setting

**Tier 3 – State Implementation Machinery (Section 30):**

- **State Food Safety Authorities** in each State/UT
- **Designated Officers** for licensing
- **Food Safety Officers** for enforcement
- **Food Analysts** for laboratory testing

#### Part (b) – Functions and Powers of FSSAI (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Statutory Functions (Section 16):**

FSSAI performs the following functions:

1. **Framing Standards** – Lays down food safety standards in the form of **Food Safety and Standards Regulations** for various food categories, additives, contaminants, labelling, etc.

2. **Licensing & Registration** – Issues licenses to FBOs and registers petty FBOs through a centralized **Food Safety Compliance System (FoSCoS)** portal.

3. **Enforcement** – Coordinates with State Authorities to enforce food safety standards and take action against violators.

4. **Laboratory Network** – Establishes and accredits food testing laboratories under the **National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories (NABL)**.

5. **Capacity Building** – Conducts training programs (FoSTaC – Food Safety Training and Certification) for FBOs and officials.

6. **Public Awareness** – Runs the **Eat Right India Movement** and **BHOG (Blissful Hygienic Offerings to God)** initiatives.

7. **Risk Assessment** – Conducts studies, surveys, and surveillance through networks like **INFoSS NET (Indian Network for Food Safety and Standards Network)**.

8. **International Coordination** – Engages with Codex Alimentarius Commission, WHO, FAO, and global food safety bodies.

**Statutory Powers:**

- **Section 19** – Power to make regulations
- **Section 23** – Power to issue directions to State Authorities
- **Section 47** – Power to authorize FSO to inspect, seize, and draw samples
- **Section 49** – Power to recommend penalties for food safety violations

> [!NOTE]
> FSSAI's powers are **supervisory and regulatory** in nature; the day-to-day enforcement lies with the **State Food Safety Authorities**, making FSSAI a **federal-cum-regulatory** body.

---

### Question B: (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**"Explain the licensing and registration framework under FSSAI. Differentiate clearly between registration, state license, and central license, with applicable turnover limits and procedural steps."**

#### Part (a) – Licensing Framework Overview (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The FSS (Licensing and Registration of Food Business) Regulations, 2011, notified under the FSS Act, 2006, establish the **licensing and registration framework** for all FBOs in India.

**Key Principles:**

1. **Mandatory Compliance:** No person shall commence or carry on any food business without obtaining a valid license or registration under Section 31 of the FSS Act, 2006.

2. **Risk-Based Approach:** The licensing regime is based on **risk stratification** – higher turnover and higher-risk activities warrant more stringent licensing requirements.

3. **Digital-First:** All applications are processed through the **FoSCoS (Food Safety Compliance System)** online portal.

**The 14-Digit License Number Format:**

Every registered/licensed FBO is assigned a unique **14-digit number**, which must be displayed on:
- All food product labels
- The business premises
- All promotional material

**Application Forms:**

| Form | Purpose | Authority |
|---|---|---|
| **Form A** | Application for Registration | Registering Authority |
| **Form B** | Application for State License | Designated Officer (State) |
| **Form C** | Application for Central License | Designated Officer (Central) |

#### Part (b) – Comparative Analysis of Registration vs. State vs. Central License (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Registration | State License | Central License |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Governing Section** | Section 31(1) | Section 31(2) | Section 31(3) |
