# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete HACCP Implementation Flowchart (12 Stages)

The following **Mermaid flowchart** illustrates the complete 12-stage HACCP implementation sequence, including the five preliminary steps and the seven principles.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start HACCP Implementation]) --> stage1[Stage 1 Assemble HACCP Team]
    stage1 --> stage2[Stage 2 Describe Product and Intended Use]
    stage2 --> stage3[Stage 3 Construct Process Flow Diagram]
    stage3 --> stage4[Stage 4 On Site Verification of Flow Diagram]
    stage4 --> stage5[Stage 5 Principle 1 Conduct Hazard Analysis]
    stage5 --> stage6[Stage 6 Principle 2 Determine CCPs]
    stage6 --> stage7[Stage 7 Principle 3 Establish Critical Limits]
    stage7 --> stage8[Stage 8 Principle 4 Establish Monitoring Procedures]
    stage8 --> stage9[Stage 9 Principle 5 Establish Corrective Actions]
    stage9 --> stage10[Stage 10 Principle 6 Establish Verification Procedures]
    stage10 --> stage11[Stage 11 Principle 7 Establish Record Keeping]
    stage11 --> stage12[Stage 12 Management Review and Continuous Improvement]
    stage12 --> feedback{Deviation or Process Change?}
    feedback -->|Yes| stage1
    feedback -->|No| endNode([HACCP Plan Active and Maintained])

    style start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style endNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stage5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stage6 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stage7 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stage8 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stage9 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stage10 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stage11 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 CCP Decision Tree (Codex Alimentarius)

The following **Mermaid diagram** reproduces the classical Codex four-question decision tree used to classify a process step as a CCP or non-CCP.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Q1 Do preventive control measures exist for the hazard?}
    q1 -->|No| mod[Modify step process or product Reassess later]
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Q2 Is this step designed to eliminate or reduce the hazard to acceptable level?}
    q2 -->|Yes| ccp1[This Step IS a CCP]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Q3 Could contamination occur in excess of acceptable level or increase to unacceptable level?}
    q3 -->|No| notccp1[Not a CCP]
    q3 -->|Yes| q4{Q4 Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the hazard to acceptable level?}
    q4 -->|No| ccp2[This Step IS a CCP]
    q4 -->|Yes| notccp2[Not a CCP Downstream step controls it]

    mod -.Reassess.-> q1
    style ccp1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ccp2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style notccp1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style notccp2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of HACCP in a Food Premise

The following **Mermaid block diagram** depicts how the seven HACCP principles interlink with the operational components of a food business — from raw materials to consumer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Raw Material Supply] --> prereq[Prerequisite Programmes GHP SSOP etc]
    prereq --> p1[Principle 1 Hazard Analysis]
    p1 --> p2[Principle 2 Identify CCPs]
    p2 --> p3[Principle 3 Critical Limits]
    p3 --> p4[Principle 4 Monitoring]
    p4 --> dev{Limit Breached?}
    dev -->|Yes| p5[Principle 5 Corrective Action]
    dev -->|No| p6[Principle 6 Verification]
    p5 --> p6
    p6 --> p7[Principle 7 Documentation]
    p7 --> product[Safe Finished Product]
    product --> consumer[Consumer Protection]
    p7 --> review[Management Review Annual]
    review -.Feedback loop.-> p1

    style p1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each — Short Answer)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Distribution Note (Part A):** Part A consists of short-answer questions worth **2 marks each**. As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, two questions will be asked from this module and students must answer any specified number. Each answer is expected in **30 - 50 words** with a precise definition or list.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]

**Define HACCP. List the seven principles of HACCP.**

**Model Answer:**

HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point) is a **systematic, science-based preventive food safety management system** that identifies, evaluates, and controls hazards significant to food safety throughout the production chain.

**Seven Principles:**
1. Conduct hazard analysis.
2. Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs).
3. Establish critical limits.
4. Establish monitoring procedures.
5. Establish corrective actions.
6. Establish verification procedures.
7. Establish record-keeping and documentation.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**What is a Critical Control Point (CCP)? Differentiate between a CCP and a Control Point (CP).**

**Model Answer:**

A **Critical Control Point (CCP)** is a step in the food process where control can be applied and is **essential to prevent, eliminate, or reduce a food safety hazard to an acceptable level.**

| Aspect | CCP | Control Point (CP) |
|---|---|---|
| Significance | Controls a **food safety** hazard | Controls a **quality** attribute |
| Failure consequence | Direct risk to consumer health | Economic loss only |
| HACCP coverage | Mandatorily covered | Covered under PRP/GMP |
| Monitoring | Continuous / frequent | Periodic |
| Corrective action | Immediate + documented | Routine |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Long Answer with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> *(1) Students often write only the **list** of seven principles but **forget to describe each principle in 2 - 3 lines**. You lose 7 - 8 marks for this. *(2) When the question asks for "**implementation in a food premise**", do NOT give a generic textbook answer — give a **specific product example** (milk, meat, bakery) with **named CCPs and critical limits**.* *(3) Always mention the **Codex Alimentarius (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020)** at least once; examiners reward regulatory referencing.*

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Set A]

**(a) Explain the concept of HACCP. Describe the five preliminary steps required before the seven principles are applied. [7 Marks]**

**(b) Discuss in detail the implementation of HACCP in a pasteurized milk processing plant. Identify at least two CCPs with their critical limits and monitoring procedures. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Concept of HACCP and Preliminary Steps [7 Marks]:**

HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point) is a **preventive, science-based food safety system** developed in the 1960s by the Pillsbury Company in collaboration with NASA to ensure **zero-defect food** for space missions. It is now codified under **Codex Alimentarius CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020** and is mandatory under **FSSAI Schedule 4, Part II** for licensed food premises in India.

**Five Preliminary Steps:**

1. **Assemble the HACCP Team** — A multi-disciplinary team including QA manager, food technologist, microbiologist, production engineer, and sanitation supervisor is constituted. A team leader is appointed.

2. **Describe the Product and Intended Use** — A comprehensive product description is prepared covering formulation, physical/chemical properties (pH, $a_w$), shelf life, packaging, storage conditions, and target consumer group.

3. **Construct the Process Flow Diagram** — A block diagram covering every unit operation from raw material reception to finished product dispatch is drawn.

4. **Verify the Flow Diagram On-Site** — The HACCP team physically walks the plant to confirm the diagram matches the actual production line. Discrepancies are corrected.

5. **Conduct Hazard Analysis (Principle 1)** — All potential biological, chemical, and physical hazards are identified at each step. Their **severity (S)** and **likelihood (L)** are scored to compute $RPN = S \times L$. Hazards with high RPN are classified as **significant** and must be controlled by the HACCP plan.

**(b) HACCP Implementation in a Pasteurized Milk Plant [7 Marks]:**

**Process Flow:** Raw milk reception → Pre-heating → Standardization → Homogenization → HTST Pasteurization → Cooling → Storage → Packing → Cold Storage → Dispatch.

**Hazard Analysis:**

| Step | Significant Hazard | Type |
|---|---|---|
| Raw milk reception | *Salmonella*, *E. coli*, *Listeria* | Biological |
| HTST Pasteurization | Under-pasteurization → pathogen survival | Biological |
| Cold storage | *Listeria monocytogenes* growth | Biological |
| Packing | Cross-contamination, foreign matter | Biological + Physical |

**Identified CCPs:**

**CCP-1: HTST Pasteurization**
- **Critical Limit:** $T \geq 72\,^\circ\text{C}$ for $t \geq 15\,\text{s}$
- **Monitoring:** Continuous temperature recording via calibrated digital probe + chart recorder; flow rate verified by timing the milk transit.
- **Frequency:** Continuous, every batch.
- **Corrective Action:** Divert flow back to raw milk silo; repasteurize the affected batch; investigate and repair instrument.

**CCP-2: Cold Storage of Finished Product**
- **Critical Limit:** $T \leq 4\,^\circ\text{C}$
- **Monitoring:** Digital data logger recording every **15 minutes**.
- **Frequency:** Continuous, 24/7.
- **Corrective Action:** Quarantine product; investigate refrigeration failure; if temperature exceeded **6 °C** for more than **4 hours**, microbiological retest before release.

**Verification:** Daily review of chart records; weekly Total Plate Count and Coliform testing; monthly audit; annual re-validation.

**Records Maintained:** HACCP Plan, Hazard Analysis Worksheet, Monitoring Logs, Corrective Action Register, Calibration Records, Training Records.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Set B]

**(a) Explain the Codex Alimentarius CCP Decision Tree with a suitable example. List the documents required for HACCP record-keeping. [7 Marks]**

**(b) "Prerequisite Programmes are the foundation upon which HACCP is built." Justify this statement and describe any five prerequisite programmes for a meat processing plant. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) CCP Decision Tree and Documentation [7 Marks]:**

The **CCP Decision Tree** (Codex Alimentarius) is a logical four-question tool used to determine whether a process step qualifies as a CCP.

**The Four Questions:**

**Q1:** Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard?
- **No** → Modify the step/process/product (not a CCP).
- **Yes** → Go to Q2.

**Q2:** Is this step *specifically designed* to eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level?
- **Yes** → **CCP**.
- **No** → Go to Q3.

**Q3:** Could contamination with the hazard occur in excess of acceptable level(s) or increase to unacceptable levels?
- **No** → Not a CCP.
- **Yes** → Go to Q4.

**Q4:** Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level?
- **No** → **CCP**.
- **Yes** → Not a CCP.

**Example (Cooked Meat Plant):** Applying the tree to the "cooking" step for pathogen control:
- Q1: Yes (time-temperature control exists).
- Q2: **Yes** → therefore the cooking step is a **CCP** (no need to proceed further).

**HACCP Documentation Required:**

1. HACCP Plan and Hazard Analysis Worksheet.
2. CCP Monitoring Logs.
3. Corrective Action Records.
4. Verification Records (calibration, audits, test reports).
5. Training Records.
6. Product Description and Process Flow Diagrams.
7. Supplier Certificates of Analysis.
8. Recall and Traceability Records.

**(b) Prerequisite Programmes (PRPs) in a Meat Plant [7 Marks]:**

**Justification:**

PRPs are the **Good Hygienic Practices (GHP)** and **Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP)** that must be in place *before* HACCP can be meaningful. Without PRPs, a HACCP plan would have **too many CCPs**, making it unmanageable. PRPs reduce the background level of contamination, allowing HACCP to focus on truly critical safety points.

The **Codex Alimentarius** formally states that **"PRPs are the foundation upon which HACCP is built."**

**Five PRPs for a Meat Processing Plant:**

1. **Premises Design and Layout** — Floors, walls, and ceilings must be smooth, non-absorbent, and easy to clean. Separation of **clean** and **dirty** areas via unidirectional flow prevents cross-contamination.

2. **Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures (SSOPs)** — Detailed cleaning schedules for equipment (e.g., saws, grinders, slicers) using approved sanitizers at defined concentrations and contact times.

3. **Personal Hygiene** — Hand-washing stations at every entry, protective clothing (aprons, gloves, head covers), prohibition of jewellery, and medical fitness certificates for handlers.

4. **Pest Control Programme** — Bait stations, insectocutors, and rodent traps placed at strategic locations with monthly inspection logs.

5. **Supplier Control and Raw Material Receiving** — Only FSSAI-licensed suppliers are approved; every batch of raw meat is inspected for temperature ($\leq 4\,^\circ\text{C}$), odor, color, and accompanied by a veterinary certificate.

6. **(Additional PRP) Equipment Calibration and Maintenance** — Thermometers, metal detectors, and weighing scales calibrated at defined frequencies using traceable standards.

7. **(Additional PRP) Waste Management** — Liquid waste drained via closed systems; solid waste stored in leak-proof bins and removed daily to prevent attraction of pests.

**Conclusion:** Once these PRPs are firmly established, the HACCP team can confidently identify a **manageable set of CCPs** (typically 3 - 5 in a meat plant) — such as cooking, chilling, and metal detection — without being overwhelmed by routine hygiene controls.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Bulletins — Read Twice Before Exam**

- **HACCP** = *Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point*. **Preventive**, not reactive. **Science-based**. **Systematic**.
- Developed by **Pillsbury + NASA (1960s)** for space food. Now **Codex Alimentarius CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020**.
- **5 Preliminary Steps** → Assemble team → Describe product → Construct flow diagram → Verify on-site → Hazard analysis.
- **7 Principles** → HA → CCP → Critical limits → Monitoring → Corrective action → Verification → Documentation.
- **Hazards** = Biological (pathogens, toxins), Chemical (pesticides, allergens), Physical (glass, metal).
- **CCP** = *Step where control prevents or eliminates a significant food safety hazard.*
- **CP** = Step that controls a **quality** parameter, not safety.
- **Critical Limits** must be **measurable** (temperature, time, pH, $a_w$, metal fragment size).
- **HTST Pasteurization** = $\geq 72\,^\circ\text{C}$ for $\geq 15\,\text{s}$; **LTLT** = $\geq 63\,^\circ\text{C}$ for $\geq 30\,\text{min}$.
- **Cold Storage CL** = $\leq 4\,^\circ\text{C}$; **Frozen Storage CL** = $\leq -18\,^\circ\text{C}$.
- **CCP Decision Tree** = 4 sequential Yes/No questions (Q1 → Q2 → Q3 → Q4).
- **Risk Priority Number** $RPN = S \times L$; a value $\geq 9$ generally warrants a CCP.
- **PRPs** (GHP, GMP, SSOP) are the **foundation** before HACCP.
- **FSSAI** mandates HACCP under **Schedule 4, Part II** of the Licensing Regulations, 2011.
- **Records** = Monitoring logs, corrective actions, calibration, audits, training, supplier COAs, recall records.
- **Verification** ≠ Monitoring — verification *confirms* the HACCP plan works (audits, end-product testing, calibration).
- **Mock recalls** must be conducted at least **once per year** to test traceability.
- Always quote **Codex Alimentarius** and **FSSAI** in exam answers; it is a high-value citation.
- For KTU 14-mark answers, always pair **principle explanation** with a **product-specific example** (milk, meat, bakery, seafood) and **state numerical critical limits**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Permitted Additives Like Colours, Preservatives, Emulsifiers, Stabilisers, Antioxidants, etc. and Their Regulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT723 - FOOD LAWS AND LEGISLATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of Permitted Additives Like Colours, Preservatives, Emulsifiers, Stabilisers, Antioxidants, etc. and Their Regulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Food Additive? (FSSAI Legal Definition)

According to the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011** (FSSR, 2011) under FSSAI, a **Food Additive** is defined as:

> "Any substance not normally consumed as a food by itself, and not normally used as a typical ingredient of the food, whether or not it has nutritive value, the intentional addition of which to food for a technological (including organoleptic) purpose in the manufacture, processing, preparation, treatment, packing, packaging, transport or holding of such food results, or may be reasonably expected to result (directly or indirectly) in it or its by-products becoming a component of or otherwise affecting the characteristics of such food."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> A food additive is **NOT** a food itself. It is added **intentionally** for a **technological** function. It must be **permitted** by the regulator (FSSAI / Codex) and used **within prescribed limits (GMP or numerical Maximum Levels)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Makeup of a Food"

Think of a food product like a **building constructed by a civil engineer**:

- The **basic ingredients** (flour, milk, sugar) are the **bricks and cement** — the main structure.
- The **additives** are the **paint, waterproofing chemicals, anti-rust coatings, and sealants** — they are not the building, but they make it look better, last longer, and perform better.

Just like a civil engineer cannot use any random chemical on a building (it must be approved by BIS), a food technologist cannot add any random chemical to food (it must be approved by **FSSAI** and listed in the regulations).

**Key Insight:** The "Do-It-Yourself" (DIY) test for an additive:
1. Is it consumed as food by itself? → If **NO**, it qualifies.
2. Is the addition **intentional** and for a **technological** purpose? → If **YES**, it qualifies.
3. Will it end up in the final product? → If **YES** (or reasonably expected), it qualifies.

---

## 1.3 The Five Major Classes of Additives — Quick Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Five (KTU Module 2 Focus):**

| S.No | Class | Primary Function | Common Examples | INS Number |
|------|-------|-----------------|-----------------|------------|
| 1 | **Colours** | Restore / add colour lost in processing | Tartrazine, Sunset Yellow, Caramel | INS 102, 110, 150 |
| 2 | **Preservatives** | Inhibit microbial growth, extend shelf-life | Sodium benzoate, Sodium nitrite, SO₂ | INS 211, 250, 220 |
| 3 | **Emulsifiers** | Help mix oil & water (stabilize emulsions) | Lecithin, Mono-diglycerides, Polysorbate 80 | INS 322, 471, 433 |
| 4 | **Stabilisers** | Maintain texture, prevent phase separation | Pectin, Carrageenan, Xanthan gum, CMC | INS 440, 407, 415, 466 |
| 5 | **Antioxidants** | Prevent fat oxidation (rancidity) | BHA, BHT, TBHQ, Ascorbic acid | INS 320, 321, 319, 300 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **INS = International Numbering System** — a global coding system developed by the **Codex Alimentarius Committee on Food Additives (CCFA)**. Every permitted additive has a unique 3 or 4-digit number used on labels worldwide (e.g., INS 102 = Tartrazine).

---

## 1.4 Why Additives Are Needed — The "Why" of Module 2

Foods degrade due to four enemies:
1. **Microbes** (bacteria, moulds, yeast) → need **Preservatives**
2. **Oxygen** (lipid oxidation → rancidity) → need **Antioxidants**
3. **Time & Heat** (texture collapse, phase separation) → need **Stabilisers & Emulsifiers**
4. **Loss of visual appeal** (faded natural colour) → need **Colours**

Without additives, modern processed food would spoil within days. Additives **enable food security, trade, and convenience**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do NOT confuse **additives** with **contaminants** or **adulterants**. Additives are *intentional, legal, and regulated*. Contaminants (pesticide residues, heavy metals) are *unintentional* and must be minimized, not added!

---

## 1.5 Governing Regulatory Framework in India

| Regulation | Scope |
|-----------|-------|
| **FSS Act, 2006** | Parent legislation — defines "food", "adulterant", "contaminant" |
| **FSSR 2011 — Chapter 2 (Food Additives)** | Rules for use, limits, labelling |
| **FSSR 2011 — Schedule I, II, III, IV, V** | Lists of permitted colours, preservatives, antioxidants, emulsifiers/stabilisers, and other additives |
| **Codex GSFA (General Standard for Food Additives)** | International reference — used when Indian standards are silent |
| **FSSAI Operational Notifications** | Periodic updates on new additives / bans |

---

## 1.6 Key Principles Governing Use of Additives

The **Codex Alimentarius** and FSSAI both enforce **5 Golden Principles**:

1. **Justification** — must serve a defined technological need.
2. **Safety** — must be evaluated by **JECFA** (Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives) and assigned an **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)**.
3. **GMP Compliance** — Good Manufacturing Practice: use the *minimum* level required.
4. **Permitted List** — only listed additives may be used.
5. **Labelling** — must be declared on the label by **class name + specific name or INS number**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ADI (Acceptable Daily Intake)** is the amount of a chemical in food or drinking water that can be ingested daily over a lifetime by a consumer of standard body mass **60 kg** without appreciable health risk, expressed in **mg/kg body weight/day**.

# MODULE 2 — SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 DEEP DIVE: CLASS-WISE ANALYSIS OF PERMITTED ADDITIVES

### 2.1.1 PERMITTED COLOURS (FSSAI Schedule I)

Colours are added to food to:
- **Restore** colour lost during processing.
- **Enhance** naturally occurring colour.
- **Standardize** batch-to-batch colour uniformity.
- **Make** food visually attractive.

**FSSAI Classification:**

**(A) Natural Colours (Generally Safe — listed in Regulation 3.1.1)**

| Colour | INS | Source | Typical Use |
|--------|-----|--------|-------------|
| Caramel | 150a/150b/150c | Sugar heated (controlled) | Cola drinks, gravies, bread |
| Annatto | 160b | Seeds of *Bixa orellana* | Cheese, butter, snacks |
| Curcumin | 100i | Turmeric rhizome | Curries, pickles, mustard |
| Chlorophyll | 140 | Green plants | Oils, chewing gum |
| β-Carotene | 160a | Carrots, palm oil | Margarine, juice |
| Beetroot Red / Betanin | 162 | Red beet | Ice cream, desserts |
| Paprika Extract / Capsanthin | 160c | Red peppers | Spices, sauces |
| Saffron | — | *Crocus sativus* | Premium sweets |

**(B) Synthetic (Artificial) Colours — Restricted Use (Regulation 3.1.2)**

Only **Eight synthetic colours** are permitted in India:

| Colour | INS Number | Common Use |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Tartrazine | **102** | Cakes, soft drinks |
| Sunset Yellow FCF | **110** | Custard powder, sweets |
| Carmoisine (Azorubine) | **122** | Sweets, jam |
| Ponceau 4R | **124** | Ice cream, pickles |
| Erythrosine | **127** | Candied fruit, cherries |
| Indigo Carmine | **132** | Ice cream, confectionery |
| Brilliant Blue FCF | **133** | Drinks, desserts |
| Fast Green FCF | — | Confectionery |

> [!WARNING]
> **Banned Synthetic Colours in India:** Rhodamine B, Auramine, Malachite Green — these are *carcinogenic* and were historically used in street-side sweets. KTU frequently tests this.

**Maximum Permitted Limits for Synthetic Colours (general):**
- Synthetic colour (alone or in combination) in foods: **100 mg/kg** (or as specified in Schedule I)
- For products like **sweets, ice cream, soft drinks** — individual additive limits vary from **50–500 mg/kg**.

**Labeling Rule (FSSAI):**
> All packages containing permitted synthetic colours must display a warning: **"CONTAINS PERMITTED NATURAL COLOUR(S)"** or **"CONTAINS PERMITTED SYNTHETIC FOOD COLOUR(S)"** along with the **INS number**.

---

### 2.1.2 PERMITTED PRESERVATIVES (FSSAI Schedule II)

Preservatives prevent or retard **microbial spoilage** and **chemical degradation**.

**Two Classes Recognised by FSSR 2011:**

**(A) Class I Preservatives — Natural / Traditional (No numerical limits under GMP)**

Common salt, sugar, dextrose, glucose syrup, jaggery, honey, vinegar, acetic acid, citric acid, tartaric acid, lactic acid, spices, essential oils, ascorbic acid, alcohol (in permitted form).

> These are considered inherently safe and do not require specific maximum limits.

**(B) Class II Preservatives — Chemical Preservatives (Have prescribed limits)**

| Preservative | INS | Form Used | Typical Limit (mg/kg) | Action |
|--------------|-----|-----------|----------------------|--------|
| **Benzoic Acid** | 210 | Sodium benzoate (211) | 150–1000 | Yeast & mould inhibition |
| **Sorbic Acid** | 200 | Potassium sorbate (202) | 200–2000 | Mould inhibitor (cheese) |
| **SO₂ (Sulphur Dioxide)** | 220 | Sodium metabisulphite (223) | 50–2000 | Antimicrobial + antioxidant |
| **Nitrites** | 250 | Sodium nitrite | 100–200 (cured meats) | Botulism prevention |
| **Nitrates** | 251 | Sodium / Potassium nitrate | 200–500 | Curing meats |
| **Propionic Acid** | 280 | Calcium propionate (282) | 1000–3000 | Bread & bakery |
| **Acetic Acid** | 260 | Glacial acetic acid | As needed | Pickles |
| **Lactic Acid** | 270 | — | As needed | Fermented foods |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism Highlights (KTU Theory):**
> - **Benzoate** — undissociated form crosses the microbial membrane; works best at pH < 4.5.
> - **SO₂** — inhibits moulds and bacteria by binding to aldehyde groups of enzymes.
> - **Nitrites** — form **nitrosomyoglobin** (red colour in cured meats) and prevent *Clostridium botulinum* growth.

---

### 2.1.3 PERMITTED EMULSIFIERS, STABILISERS & THICKENERS (FSSAI Schedules III & IV)

These are surface-active substances that control texture and consistency.

**Difference — Don't Confuse:**

| Property | Emulsifier | Stabiliser |
|----------|-----------|------------|
| Function | Allows oil & water to mix | Maintains that mixture over time |
| Example | Lecithin (in mayonnaise) | Xanthan gum (in salad dressing) |
| Mechanism | Reduces interfacial tension | Increases viscosity / forms gel network |

> **In practice, many additives perform BOTH functions** (e.g., pectin stabilises + emulsifies).

**Common Permitted Emulsifiers:**

| Additive | INS | Use |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Lecithin (Soya/Sunflower) | 322 | Chocolate, bakery |
| Mono- and Diglycerides of fatty acids | 471 | Margarine, ice cream |
| Polysorbate 80 | 433 | Ice cream (prevents ice crystal growth) |
| Sorbitan monostearate | 491 | Confectionery |
| Sodium stearoyl lactylate (SSL) | 481i | Bread softening |

**Common Permitted Stabilisers & Thickeners:**

| Additive | INS | Use |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Pectin | 440 | Jam, jelly |
| Carrageenan | 407 | Milk products, desserts |
| Xanthan gum | 415 | Salad dressings, gluten-free |
| Sodium alginate | 401 | Ice cream, restructured foods |
| Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) | 466 | Ice cream, beverages |
| Guar gum | 412 | Sauces, ice cream |
| Carob bean gum (LBG) | 410 | Ice cream |
| Agar | 406 | Confectionery |
| Gelatin | 428 | Confectionery, dairy |

---

### 2.1.4 PERMITTED ANTIOXIDANTS (FSSAI Schedule V)

Antioxidants prevent **lipid oxidation (rancidity)** by scavenging free radicals or chelating metal catalysts.

**Two Sub-classes:**

**(A) Natural Antioxidants (Generally Recognised As Safe — GRAS)**

- Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C) — INS 300
- Tocopherols (Vitamin E) — INS 307
- Lecithin — INS 322
- Rosemary extract (rosmarinic acid) — INS 392
- Gallic acid esters — INS 310

**(B) Synthetic Antioxidants (Strict Numerical Limits)**

| Antioxidant | INS | Maximum Permitted Level (mg/kg) | Notes |
|-------------|-----|-------------------------------|-------|
| **BHA — Butylated Hydroxyanisole** | **320** | **200 mg/kg** (in fats) | Fat-soluble |
| **BHT — Butylated Hydroxytoluene** | **321** | **200 mg/kg** (in fats) | Most common |
| **TBHQ — Tert-Butyl Hydroquinone** | **319** | **200 mg/kg** | Very effective in oils |
| Propyl gallate | 310 | 100 mg/kg (in fats) | Heat-labile |
| Ethoxyquin | 324 | Banned in many countries | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Important KTU Trap:** BHA, BHT, TBHQ may be used **singly or in combination**, but the **total combined level** must not exceed the prescribed individual limit (typically 200 mg/kg of fat content).

---

### 2.1.5 OTHER PERMITTED ADDITIVES (Brief — for completeness)

- **Sweeteners:** Saccharin (INS 954), Aspartame (951), Sucralose (955), Acesulfame-K (950)
- **Anticaking agents:** Silicon dioxide (INS 551), Calcium silicate (552)
- **Sequestrants:** EDTA (INS 386), Citric acid (330)
- **Acid regulators:** Acetic, Citric, Lactic, Malic acid
- **Flavour enhancers:** MSG (INS 621), Disodium 5'-inosinate (631), Disodium 5'-guanylate (627)
- **Raising agents:** Baking soda, Sodium acid pyrophosphate
- **Glazing agents:** Beeswax (901), Shellac (904)

---

## 2.2 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA & TABLE CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it represents 70% of KTU theory questions on Module 2.**

### Table 2.1: ADI of Key Additives (JECFA-assigned)

| Additive | INS | ADI (mg/kg bw/day) | Safety Status |
|----------|-----|--------------------|---------------| 
| Tartrazine | 102 | 0–7.5 | Limited toxicology data |
| Sunset Yellow FCF | 110 | 0–2.5 | Reduced in 2014 |
| Sodium Benzoate | 211 | 0–5 | Safe within limits |
| Sorbic Acid | 200 | 0–25 | High safety margin |
| SO₂ (Sulphites) | 220 | 0–0.7 | **Lowest ADI** — strictest |
| Lecithin | 322 | Not specified | GRAS |
| BHA | 320 | 0–0.5 | Under review |
| BHT | 321 | 0–0.3 | Under review |
| Ascorbic acid | 300 | Not specified | GRAS |

### Table 2.2: The pH-Activity Relationship (KTU Favourite!)

$$ \text{pH}_{\text{work}} < \text{pK}_a + 1 \text{ (for acid preservatives to be effective)} $$

| Preservative | pKa | Effective pH Range |
|--------------|-----|-------------------|
| Benzoic acid | 4.19 | **< 4.5** |
| Sorbic acid | 4.76 | **< 5.5** |
| Propionic acid | 4.87 | **< 5.0** |
| Acetic acid | 4.76 | **< 5.0** |

> **Take-home rule:** The lower the pH of the food, the more effective the acid preservative (because more molecules are in the **undissociated** form that crosses microbial membranes).

---

## 2.3 THE "INS NUMBER" SYSTEM — DEEP DIVE

The **International Numbering System (INS)** for Food Additives is maintained by the **Codex Committee on Food Additives (CCFA)**.

**Numbering Logic:**

- **100–199** → Colours
- **200–299** → Preservatives
- **300–399** → Antioxidants & acidity regulators
- **400–499** → Emulsifiers, thickeners, stabilisers, gelling agents
- **500–599** → Anticaking agents, salt alternatives
- **600–699** → Flavour enhancers
- **700–899** → Antibiotics, sweeteners (varies)
- **900–999** → Glazing agents, propellants, packaging gases

> This numbering is **universally recognised** — it is the most efficient way to identify an additive on a global label.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Application: The "Smart-Label" on Your Chips Pack

A typical Indian chips pack lists:
> *CONTAINS CLASS II PRESERVATIVES (INS 211), PERMITTED ANTIOXIDANT (INS 320), CLASS II EMULSIFIER (INS 471), AND PERMITTED NATURAL COLOUR (INS 100i). ADDED FLAVOUR (NATURAL IDENTICAL FLAVOURING SUBSTANCES).*

**Decoded:**
- **INS 211** = Sodium benzoate (preservative)
- **INS 320** = BHA (antioxidant)
- **INS 471** = Mono-diglycerides (emulsifier)
- **INS 100i** = Curcumin (natural colour)

# MODULE 2 — SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Correct Preservative for a Food

The decision-making workflow for choosing a permitted preservative is shown step-by-step below.

### Step 1 — Identify the food category
- Acidic (pH < 4.5) → fermented products, soft drinks, pickles
- Neutral (pH 4.5–6.5) → sauces, dairy desserts
- Alkaline (pH > 6.5) → bakery, cured meats

### Step 2 — Match the preservative's effective pH

For a tomato ketchup at pH **3.8**:
- The Henderson–Hasselbalch equation is:

$$ \text{pH} = \text{pK}_a + \log\left(\frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}\right) $$

Where $[A^-]$ = dissociated form, $[HA]$ = undissociated form.

For **benzoic acid** ($\text{pK}_a = 4.19$) at pH 3.8:

$$ \frac{[A^-]}{[HA]} = 10^{(\text{pH} - \text{pK}_a)} = 10^{(3.8 - 4.19)} = 10^{-0.39} $$

$$ \frac{[A^-]}{[HA]} \approx 0.407 $$

This means out of every **1.4 molecules**, only **1** is dissociated, and **0.4** is undissociated (i.e., the active form). In percentage:

$$ \%HA = \frac{1}{1 + 0.407} \times 100 \approx 71.0\% $$

So ~**71% of benzoic acid is in the active, undissociated form** at pH 3.8 — therefore benzoic acid is **highly suitable** for tomato ketchup.

> **Rule:** Higher %HA (undissociated) = Higher antimicrobial activity.

### Step 3 — Apply the FSSAI maximum limit

For ketchup, FSSR 2011 specifies a maximum of **750 mg/kg** of benzoic acid. The formulator must add **sodium benzoate (NaC₆H₅COO)** equivalent to ≤ 750 mg/kg benzoic acid.

Conversion: Molecular weight of benzoic acid (C₆H₅COOH) = **122.12 g/mol**
Molecular weight of sodium benzoate (C₆H₅COONa) = **144.11 g/mol**

$$ \text{Sodium benzoate required} = 750 \times \frac{144.11}{122.12} \approx 885 \text{ mg/kg} $$

### Step 4 — Verify ADI

Assuming a 60 kg adult consumes 30 g ketchup/day (high estimate):
Daily benzoic acid intake = $750 \times 0.030 = 22.5$ mg/day
Per kg body weight = $\frac{22.5}{60} = 0.375$ mg/kg bw/day
This is **< ADI of 5 mg/kg bw/day** → **SAFE**.

### Step 5 — Document on label
> "CONTAINS PERMITTED CLASS II PRESERVATIVE (INS 211 — SODIUM BENZOATE)"

---

## 3.2 Derivation: BHA/BHT Combined-Limit Calculation

FSSAI permits a maximum of **200 mg/kg** of BHA or BHT in edible fats and oils, **singly or in combination**. The total combined use must **NOT exceed 200 mg/kg**.

**Worked Example:** A vanaspati manufacturer wants to use both BHA and BHT.

Suppose he uses **80 mg/kg BHA** and **120 mg/kg BHT** in a batch of vanaspati.

$$ \text{Total antioxidant load} = 80 + 120 = 200 \text{ mg/kg} $$

Since 200 mg/kg ≤ 200 mg/kg (limit) → **PERMISSIBLE** ✓

If he used **150 mg/kg BHA** and **100 mg/kg BHT**:

$$ \text{Total antioxidant load} = 150 + 100 = 250 \text{ mg/kg} $$

Since 250 > 200 → **NOT PERMISSIBLE** ✗

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** "A baker uses 100 mg/kg BHA, 80 mg/kg BHT, and 50 mg/kg TBHQ in his shortening. Is this combination legal? Justify with calculations."

**Solution:**
- Individual BHA (100 mg/kg) ≤ 200 mg/kg → OK
- Individual BHT (80 mg/kg) ≤ 200 mg/kg → OK
- Individual TBHQ (50 mg/kg) ≤ 200 mg/kg → OK
- **But** FSSAI rule on combined use of antioxidants: when used in combination, the total must not exceed the prescribed single limit. Here, total = 100 + 80 + 50 = **230 mg/kg > 200 mg/kg** → **NOT LEGAL**.
- The baker must reduce the dose.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: SO₂ Residue Calculation in Dried Fruit

The FSSAI limit for SO₂ in **dried fruits** is **2000 mg/kg (max)**.
Sulphites release SO₂ in the body; the active form added is usually **sodium metabisulphite (Na₂S₂O₅)**, MW = **190.11 g/mol**.

**Reaction in acidic conditions:**

$$ \text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_5 + 2\text{H}^+ \rightarrow 2\text{Na}^+ + 2\text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

1 mole of Na₂S₂O₅ produces **2 moles of SO₂**.

MW of SO₂ = **64.07 g/mol** → 2 moles = 128.14 g SO₂ per mole of Na₂S₂O₅.

**To achieve 2000 mg SO₂/kg in dried fruit:**

$$ \text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ required} = 2000 \times \frac{190.11}{128.14} \approx 2966.5 \text{ mg/kg} $$

**KTU Answer:** The food processor must add **~2.97 g of sodium metabisulphite per kg of dried fruit** to achieve the maximum permitted SO₂ level.

> The actual **residual SO₂** (analysed after processing) is what regulations measure, not the addition level.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Decoding a Food Label (Analytical Skill)

A label says: **"Permitted Colour (INS 110), Class II Preservative (INS 211), Class II Antioxidant (INS 320), Stabiliser (INS 415)"**

| Step | Lookup | Identity | Function |
|------|--------|----------|----------|
| 1 | INS 110 | Sunset Yellow FCF | Synthetic colour |
| 2 | INS 211 | Sodium benzoate | Preservative (Class II) |
| 3 | INS 320 | BHA | Synthetic antioxidant |
| 4 | INS 415 | Xanthan gum | Stabiliser / thickener |

This is the **expected KTU answer format** when given a hypothetical label.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Natural vs Synthetic Additives

| Parameter | Natural Additives | Synthetic Additives |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Source | Plant, animal, mineral | Chemical synthesis |
| Cost | Generally higher | Lower |
| Colour strength | Lower | Higher (more vivid) |
| Stability | Variable, heat-labile | High |
| Toxicity concerns | Low (GRAS status) | Requires strict ADI checks |
| Approval in India | Easier (mostly permitted) | Restricted list (8 synthetic colours) |
| Consumer perception | Positive | "Chemical" — controversial |
| FSSAI labelling | "Permitted Natural Colour(s)" | "Permitted Synthetic Food Colour(s)" + INS |

# MODULE 2 — SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-MARK SHORT-ANSWER QUESTIONS

### Question 1: Define a "Food Additive" as per FSSAI. Give two examples of Class II preservatives. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]

**Model Answer:**

A *Food Additive* means any substance not normally consumed as a food by itself, and not normally used as a typical ingredient of the food, whether or not it has nutritive value, the intentional addition of which to food for a technological (including organoleptic) purpose in the manufacture, processing, preparation, treatment, packing, packaging, transport or holding of such food results, or may be reasonably expected to result, in it or its by-products becoming a component of or otherwise affecting the characteristics of such food (Section 3.1.1, FSSR 2011).

**Two examples of Class II Preservatives:**
1. **Sodium benzoate (INS 211)** — used at 150–1000 mg/kg in acidic foods, sauces, and pickles.
2. **Sulphur dioxide (INS 220)** — used as sodium metabisulphite in dried fruits, wines, and juices (max 2000 mg/kg in dried fruits).

---

### Question 2: What is an INS number? Why is it important in food labelling? [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**INS (International Numbering System)** is a global coding system for food additives, maintained by the *Codex Committee on Food Additives (CCFA)*. Each permitted additive is assigned a unique 3- or 4-digit number that is independent of language.

**Importance in Food Labelling:**
- **Universal identification** — overcomes language barriers (e.g., INS 102 = Tartrazine, recognised worldwide).
- **Consumer transparency** — informs consumers about what additives are present.
- **Regulatory compliance** — FSSAI mandates declaration of class name + INS number.
- **Trade facilitation** — simplifies import/export documentation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-MARK QUESTIONS (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

---

### ⭐ OPTION A — QUESTION A (14 MARKS)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Adapted]**

**Q.A) (a)** Classify food colours permitted under FSSAI. List any **eight permitted synthetic colours** with their INS numbers. Discuss the labelling requirements for products containing synthetic colours. **[7 marks]**

**Q.A) (b)** Explain the concept of **ADI (Acceptable Daily Intake)**. Discuss the ADI of any **three commonly used additives** and explain why sulphites have the lowest ADI among preservatives. **[7 marks]**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER FOR Q.A (a):

**Classification of Food Colours (FSSAI Regulation 3.1.1):**

Food colours are classified into two major groups:

**(i) Natural Colours** — obtained from plant, animal, or mineral sources.
- Caramel (INS 150a/b/c), Curcumin (INS 100i), β-Carotene (INS 160a), Annatto (INS 160b), Chlorophyll (INS 140), Beetroot Red (INS 162), Saffron.

**(ii) Synthetic (Artificial) Colours** — chemically synthesised; only **eight** are permitted under FSSAI.

**The Eight Permitted Synthetic Colours:**

| S.No | Colour | INS Number |
|------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | Tartrazine | 102 |
| 2 | Sunset Yellow FCF | 110 |
| 3 | Carmoisine (Azorubine) | 122 |
| 4 | Ponceau 4R | 124 |
| 5 | Erythrosine | 127 |
| 6 | Indigo Carmine | 132 |
| 7 | Brilliant Blue FCF | 133 |
| 8 | Fast Green FCF | — |

**Labelling Requirements (FSSAI):**
- The label must clearly state: **"CONTAINS PERMITTED NATURAL COLOUR(S)"** or **"CONTAINS PERMITTED SYNTHETIC FOOD COLOUR(S)"**.
- The **INS number(s)** of the colour(s) used must be mentioned.
- This declaration must appear in the list of ingredients on the package.
- A statutory warning is mandatory for synthetic colours in certain products.

> **Banned synthetic colours** in India: Rhodamine B, Auramine, Malachite Green — carcinogenic; banned under the FSS Act, 2006.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER FOR Q.A (b):

**Definition of ADI:**

> **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** of a chemical is the amount, expressed in *mg per kg of body weight per day*, that a person can consume daily over an entire lifetime without any appreciable health risk. (Established by **JECFA** — Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives.)

**ADI of three commonly used additives:**

| Additive | INS | ADI (mg/kg bw/day) |
|----------|-----|-------------------|
| Sodium Benzoate | 211 | **0 – 5** |
| Ascorbic Acid (Vitamin C) | 300 | **Not specified** (GRAS) |
| Tartrazine | 102 | **0 – 7.5** |

**Why Sulphites (SO₂) have the lowest ADI:**

1. **Sulphites are highly reactive** — they bind to vitamins (especially thiamine, B1), reducing nutritional value.
2. **Asthma trigger** — sulphites can trigger severe bronchospasm in asthmatic patients; FDA mandates disclosure on labels.
3. **Carries over** — residual SO₂ remains in food even after processing, leading to cumulative exposure.
4. **JECFA safety review** — the ADI of SO₂ is set at **0–0.7 mg/kg bw/day**, the lowest among preservatives, because of these concerns and the safety margin required to protect sensitive populations.

---

### ⭐ OPTION B — QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Adapted]**

**Q.B) (a)** Differentiate between **emulsifiers and stabilisers** with two examples each. Explain the **INS numbering system** in detail, mentioning the broad category-wise ranges (e.g., 100–199, 200–299, etc.). **[7 marks]**

**Q.B) (b)** Discuss the **regulations governing antioxidants** in India. Calculate whether a vanaspati manufacturer using **150 mg/kg BHA + 70 mg/kg BHT + 30 mg/kg TBHQ** is compliant with FSSAI. **[7 marks]**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER FOR Q.B (a):

**Difference between Emulsifiers and Stabilisers:**

| Parameter | Emulsifier | Stabiliser |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Primary function | Allows oil and water to mix by reducing interfacial tension | Maintains the uniform dispersion by increasing viscosity or forming a gel |
| Stage of action | During formation of emulsion | After emulsion is formed, prevents phase separation |
| Examples | Lecithin (INS 322), Mono- and Diglycerides (INS 471) | Xanthan gum (INS 415), Pectin (INS 440), Carrageenan (INS 407) |
| Mechanism | Surface-active molecules (hydrophilic + lipophilic) | Polysaccharide thickeners / gelling agents |

> Many additives like pectin can act as **both** emulsifier and stabiliser.

**INS Numbering System (International Numbering System):**

Maintained by **Codex Committee on Food Additives (CCFA)**, the INS provides a unique 3- or 4-digit number to each food additive for global identification.

**Broad Category Ranges:**

| Range | Category |
|-------|----------|
| **100 – 199** | **Colours** (e.g., 102 = Tartrazine, 110 = Sunset Yellow) |
| **200 – 299** | **Preservatives** (e.g., 211 = Sodium benzoate, 220 = SO₂) |
| **300 – 399** | **Antioxidants & Acidity Regulators** (e.g., 300 = Ascorbic acid) |
| **400 – 499** | **Emulsifiers, Stabilisers, Thickeners, Gelling agents** (e.g., 415 = Xanthan) |
| **500 – 599** | **Anticaking agents** (e.g., 551 = Silicon dioxide) |
| **600 – 699** | **Flavour Enhancers** (e.g., 621 = MSG) |
| **900 – 999** | **Glazing agents, Propellants, Packaging gases** |

> The INS number is **independent of language** — making it ideal for international trade and harmonised labelling.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER FOR Q.B (b):

**Regulations Governing Antioxidants in India (FSSR 2011 — Schedule V):**

- Only the antioxidants **listed in Schedule V** may be used in food.
- They may be used **singly or in combination**, but the **total combined level** must not exceed the **maximum individual limit** specified.
- Common permitted antioxidants:
  - Natural: Ascorbic acid (INS 300), Tocopherols (INS 307), Lecithin (INS 322), Rosemary extract (INS 392).
  - Synthetic: BHA (INS 320), BHT (INS 321), TBHQ (INS 319), Propyl gallate (INS 310), Octyl gallate (INS 311), Dodecyl gallate (INS 312).
- Maximum limits: **200 mg/kg** for BHA, BHT, TBHQ in edible fats & oils; **100 mg/kg** for propyl/octyl/dodecyl gallate.

**Compliance Calculation for Vanaspati Manufacturer:**

Given:
- BHA = 150 mg/kg
- BHT = 70 mg/kg
- TBHQ = 30 mg/kg
- FSSAI individual limit = 200 mg/kg (singly or combined)

**Step 1 — Check each antioxidant individually:**
- BHA: 150 mg/kg ≤ 200 mg/kg ✓
- BHT: 70 mg/kg ≤ 200 mg/kg ✓
- TBHQ: 30 mg/kg ≤ 200 mg/kg ✓

**Step 2 — Check the combined limit:**

$$ \text{Total} = 150 + 70 + 30 = 250 \text{ mg/kg} $$

**Step 3 — Compare with the rule:**

> The rule states: *"When antioxidants are used in combination, the total combined level must not exceed 200 mg/kg."*

$$ 250 \text{ mg/kg} > 200 \text{ mg/kg} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NON-COMPLIANT} \;\;\boldsymbol{\times} $$

**Conclusion:** The vanaspati manufacturer is **NOT compliant** with FSSAI. He must reduce the total antioxidant load to ≤ 200 mg/kg. For example, he could use 100 mg/kg BHA + 50 mg/kg BHT + 50 mg/kg TBHQ = 200 mg/kg.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "combined limit" rule** — students check each additive individually but miss the cumulative check. Marks are deducted for this.
> 2. **Writing only "legal/illegal"** without showing the **sum and the rule quoted**. Always quote the regulation.
> 3. **Wrong INS numbers** — confusing BHA (320) with BHT (321) and TBHQ (319) is a very common error. Memorise: **B**HA = 3**2**0, **B**HT = 3**2**1, **T**BHQ = 3**1**9.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this for last-night revision!)**

### ✅ Must-Know Definitions
- Food Additive (FSSAI definition) — must be intentional, technological, not a food itself.
- ADI — mg/kg body weight/day, established by JECFA.
- INS — International Numbering System, Codex-managed, language-independent.
- Class I Preservative — traditional (salt, sugar, vinegar), no numerical limit.
- Class II Preservative — chemical, has prescribed numerical limit.
- GRAS — Generally Recognised As Safe.

### ✅ Must-Know INS Numbers (Top Priority)

| Additive | INS |
|----------|-----|
| Tartrazine | 102 |
| Sunset Yellow FCF | 110 |
| Caramel | 150 |
| Sorbic acid | 200 |
| Sodium benzoate | 211 |
| SO₂ | 220 |
| Sodium nitrite | 250 |
| Ascorbic acid | 300 |
| TBHQ | 319 |
| BHA | 320 |
| BHT | 321 |
| Lecithin | 322 |
| Pectin | 440 |
| Carrageenan | 407 |
| Xanthan gum | 415 |
| CMC | 466 |
| Mono- & Diglycerides | 471 |
| Silicon dioxide | 551 |
| MSG | 621 |

### ✅ Must-Know Regulatory Limits
- Synthetic colour (max general): **100 mg/kg**
- SO₂ in dried fruit: **2000 mg/kg**
- BHA/BHT/TBHQ in fats: **200 mg/kg (singly or combined)**
- Benzoic acid general: **150–1000 mg/kg** depending on food

### ✅ Must-Know Principles
1. **Justification** → 2. **Safety (JECFA)** → 3. **GMP** → 4. **Permitted list** → 5. **Labelling (class name + INS)**

### ✅ Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Do NOT confuse *additives* with *contaminants* or *adulterants*.
- ❌ Do NOT forget the **combined-limit** rule for antioxidants.
- ❌ Do NOT forget the **pH-relationship** for preservatives (effective at pH < pKa + 1).
- ❌ Do NOT confuse BHA (320), BHT (321), TBHQ (319) INS numbers.
- ❌ Do NOT use **banned synthetic colours** — Rhodamine B, Auramine, Malachite Green.

### ✅ Frequently Asked Comparative Questions (Ready-Made)
- Natural vs Synthetic Colours.
- Emulsifier vs Stabiliser.
- Class I vs Class II Preservatives.
- BHA vs BHT (solubility, effectiveness, regulation).
- INS vs E-number (European system).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SPS and TBT Agreement, Export & Import Laws and Regulations, WHO/FAO Expert Bodies (JECFA/JEMRA/JMPR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT723 - FOOD LAWS AND LEGISLATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SPS and TBT Agreement, Export & Import Laws and Regulations, WHO/FAO Expert Bodies (JECFA/JEMRA/JMPR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The SPS Agreement (Agreement on the Application of Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures)

### Formal KTU Definition
The **SPS Agreement** is one of the **Annex 1A Multilateral Trade Agreements** of the **World Trade Organization (WTO)**, which entered into force on **1 January 1995** simultaneously with the establishment of the WTO. It governs how governments can apply **food safety, animal health, and plant health measures** without creating unnecessary barriers to international trade.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The SPS Agreement is THE foundational international law that directly affects every country's food import/export regulation, including India's Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (FSSAI).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine two neighboring kingdoms trading fruits. Kingdom A wants to ban Kingdom B's apples because they fear a pest. Without rules, Kingdom A could ban ANYTHING and hide protectionism behind "safety." The SPS Agreement is like an **international referee** that says: *"You can refuse the apples ONLY IF you have scientific proof of risk, you assess that risk fairly, and you don't discriminate unfairly."*

### What "Sanitary" vs "Phytosanitary" Means

| Term | Prefix Origin | Scope | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sanitary** | Latin *sanitas* (health) | Human & Animal health | Bacteria in meat, BSE, Salmonella in eggs |
| **Phyto-sanitary** | Greek *phyton* (plant) | Plant health | Fruit fly infestation, fungal diseases in grains |

## 1.2 The TBT Agreement (Agreement on Technical Barriers to Trade)

### Formal KTU Definition
The **TBT Agreement** is also a **WTO Annex 1A Agreement (1995)**. It ensures that **technical regulations, voluntary standards, and conformity assessment procedures** do not create unnecessary obstacles to international trade, while still allowing members to pursue legitimate policy objectives (such as consumer protection, prevention of deceptive practices, and protection of human health/environment).

### Conceptual Analogy
If the SPS Agreement is the **health referee** (focused on biological/food safety risks), then the TBT Agreement is the **labelling referee** (focused on packaging, ingredients declaration, quality marks, and testing procedures). For instance, if a country insists milk bottles must be exactly **500 mL**, must display ingredients in a **specific font size**, and pass a **specific lab test** before entry — that is governed by TBT, not SPS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Most Asked in KTU):**
> - **SPS = real biological/health risk to humans, animals, plants.**
> - **TBT = quality, labelling, packaging, performance characteristics — not direct health risk.**

## 1.3 Export & Import Laws and Regulations

### Formal Definition
**Export and Import laws** are the body of national statutes, rules, executive orders, and policy frameworks that govern the **cross-border movement of goods**. In India, this is anchored by:
- **Foreign Trade (Development & Regulation) Act, 1992 (FTDR Act)**
- **Customs Act, 1962**
- **Foreign Trade Policy (FTP) 2023** (currently in force, replacing FTP 2015)
- **DGFT** (Directorate General of Foreign Trade) — apex licensing body
- **EIC** (Export Inspection Council) — statutory body under Export Inspection Council Act, 1963
- Product-specific export promotion bodies: **APEDA, MPEDA, Coffee Board, Spices Board**

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of foreign trade as a journey through a **border checkpoint**:
1. **DGFT** issues the passport (license/IEC code).
2. **Customs** verifies the luggage and collects duty.
3. **EIC/APEDA/MPEDA** issue the health certificate proving the goods are safe.
4. **FSSAI** issues the import clearance for food items.

## 1.4 WHO/FAO Expert Scientific Bodies

### Three Core Joint Bodies

| Acronym | Full Form | Established | Primary Mandate |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **JECFA** | Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives | **1956**, Geneva | Evaluates safety of **food additives, contaminants, and residues of veterinary drugs** in food |
| **JEMRA** | Joint FAO/WHO Expert Meetings on Microbiological Risk Assessment | **2000** | Performs **risk assessments of microbiological hazards** in food (Salmonella, Listeria, etc.) |
| **JMPR** | Joint FAO/WHO Meeting on Pesticide Residues | **1963** | Establishes **Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs)** for pesticides in food |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All three bodies function under the joint auspices of FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization) and WHO (World Health Organization).** They provide the **independent scientific basis** upon which the Codex Alimentarius Commission sets international food standards — which in turn underpin the SPS Agreement.

### Intuitive Analogy
If **Codex Alimentarius** is the international cookbook of food standards, then:
- **JECFA** is the chemist deciding "Is this ingredient safe?"
- **JEMRA** is the microbiologist deciding "How dangerous is this bacteria?"
- **JMPR** is the toxicologist deciding "How much pesticide residue is acceptable?"

All three "kitchen scientists" feed their data to the **Codex**, which then becomes the **gold standard** referenced in SPS disputes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SPS Agreement — Deep Analysis

### Three Pillars (KTU High-Yield)

#### Pillar 1 — Right to Adopt SPS Measures (Article 2)
Members have the sovereign right to take SPS measures necessary to protect:
- Human or animal life from food-borne risks
- Human life from animal/plant-carried diseases
- Animal/plant life from pests/diseases

#### Pillar 2 — Basic Obligations & Principles

| Principle | Article | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scientific Justification** | Art. 2.2 | Measures must be based on scientific principles |
| **Risk Assessment** | Art. 5.1–5.3 | Based on *appropriate* risk assessment techniques |
| **Non-discrimination** | Art. 2.3 | No discrimination between domestic & foreign producers |
| **Transparency** | Art. 7 & Annex B | Notify changes, publish regulations, provide enquiry points |
| **Equivalence** | Art. 4 | Accept another country's measures if they achieve the same level of protection |
| **Regionalization** | Art. 6 | Adapt measures to pest/disease-free zones |
| **Harmonization** | Art. 3 | Use international standards (Codex, IPPC, OIE/WOAH) as basis |

#### Pillar 3 — Three International Standard-Setting Bodies (Article 3 & Annex A)

| Body | Scope |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO)** | Food safety |
| **IPPC — International Plant Protection Convention** | Plant health |
| **WOAH (formerly OIE)** | Animal health |

### SPS Risk Assessment Logic Flow (KTU Examiner's Favourite)

$$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Hazard Identification}, \text{Hazard Characterization}, \text{Exposure Assessment}, \text{Risk Characterization})$$

Where:
- **Hazard Identification** — What is the biological/chemical/physical agent?
- **Hazard Characterization** — What is its dose-response?
- **Exposure Assessment** — How much/long are consumers exposed?
- **Risk Characterization** — Integrating above to estimate risk magnitude

> [!NOTE]
> Members are **encouraged** (not strictly bound) to base measures on Codex standards. If a member chooses a measure that results in a *higher* level of protection than Codex, they must provide **scientific justification** under Article 3.3.

## 2.2 TBT Agreement — Deep Analysis

### Three Categories Covered (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Technical Regulations** — Mandatory, legally enforceable (e.g., BIS standards made compulsory)
2. **Standards** — Voluntary, non-binding (e.g., ISO standards)
3. **Conformity Assessment Procedures** — Testing, certification, inspection, accreditation

### Legitimate Objectives (Article 2.2)

The TBT Agreement explicitly permits measures pursuing:
- National security
- Prevention of deceptive practices
- Protection of human health or safety
- Protection of animal/plant life or health
- Protection of the environment

### Code of Good Practice (Article 5 & Annex 3)
- Use of international standards (ISO/IEC/ITU) where available
- Transparency in development
- Non-discrimination

### Key Difference: SPS vs TBT (Most Repeated Question)

| Parameter | **SPS Agreement** | **TBT Agreement** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Risk Type** | Biological, food safety, pests/diseases | Technical, quality, labelling, performance |
| **Standards Body** | Codex, IPPC, WOAH | ISO, IEC, ITU, BIS |
| **Trigger** | Human/Animal/Plant health | Consumer info, deceptive practices, environment |
| **Example** | Ban on apples due to codling moth | Requirement that food labels display ingredients in a specific font |
| **Risk Assessment** | Mandatory (Art. 5) | Not mandatory, but encouraged |
| **Precautionary Principle** | Art. 5.7 (explicit) | Not explicit but acknowledged |

## 2.3 Export & Import Laws — Deep Analysis

### Hierarchical Legal Framework

```
Constitution of India (Art. 53, Art. 246, Schedule VII)
            ↓
Foreign Trade (Development & Regulation) Act, 1992 (FTDR)
            ↓
Foreign Trade Policy (FTP) — issued every 5 years
            ↓
Handbook of Procedures (HoP) — issued annually
            ↓
DGFT Notifications & Public Notices
```

### Key Authorities & Their Role

| Authority | Function | Statutory Basis |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **DGFT** | Issues IEC (Import Export Code), Advance Authorization, EPCG license | FTDR Act 1992 |
| **CBIC** (Central Board of Indirect Taxes & Customs) | Customs clearance, duty assessment, IGST | Customs Act 1962 |
| **EIC** (Export Inspection Council) | Pre-shipment inspection, certification of export consignments | EIC Act 1963 |
| **FSSAI** | Food import clearance, licensing of FBOs | FSS Act 2006 |
| **APEDA** | Export promotion of scheduled products (fruits, vegetables, meat) | APEDA Act 1985 |
| **MPEDA** | Marine product export quality | MPEDA Act 1972 |
| **Plant/Animal Quarantine** | Phytosanitary & veterinary certification | Destructive Insects & Pests Act 1914; Livestock Importation Act 1898 |

### Export Procedure Flow (KTU Diagram Favourite)

1. **Obtain IEC** (10-digit code from DGFT — mandatory for any export/import exceeding ₹25,000 or per FTP terms)
2. **Register with APEDA/EIC** (if exporting scheduled products)
3. **Procure/Manufacture goods** as per buyer/importer specifications
4. **Pre-shipment inspection** by EIC (where mandatory)
5. **Customs clearance** — filing of Shipping Bill, EXIM Bank code, LUT/Bond
6. **Cargo movement** to port/airport
7. **Bill of Lading/Airway Bill** issued
8. **Foreign exchange remittance** through AD Category I bank
9. **Realization of payment** within 9 months (now 15 months in some cases)
10. **Claim export benefits** (Drawback, RoDTEP, RoSCTL)

### Import Procedure Flow

1. **IEC** + **AD code registration**
2. **Check import policy** — Free, Restricted, Prohibited, or STE (State Trading Enterprise)
3. **Apply for import license** if restricted (e.g., through DGFT)
4. **Obtain phytosanitary/animal health certificate** from exporting country
5. **Customs filing** — Bill of Entry (for home consumption) or Warehousing Bill
6. **FSSAI clearance** at port (mandatory for food items)
7. **Payment of Basic Customs Duty + IGST + Social Welfare Surcharge**
8. **Delivery** after examination and out-of-charge order

### KTU Formula Sheet — Trade Indicators (Cheat Sheet)

| Indicator | Formula / Concept | Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Export Value** | $\text{FOB Value} = \text{Ex-factory price} + \text{Profit Margin} + \text{Logistics to port}$ | Customs declaration |
| **Import Value** | $\text{CIF Value} = \text{Cost} + \text{Insurance} + \text{Freight}$ | Duty assessment base |
| **Assessable Value** | $\text{AV} = \text{CIF} \times \text{Exchange Rate (notified by CBIC)}$ | Customs duty calculation |
| **Effective Duty Rate** | $D_{eff} = \frac{\text{Total Duty Paid}}{\text{CIF Value}}$ | Trade cost analysis |
| **Export-Import Cover Ratio** | $\text{EICR} = \frac{\text{Export Earnings}}{\text{Import Payments}} \times 100$ | Balance of payments indicator |
| **Trade Balance** | $\text{TB} = \text{Exports} - \text{Imports}$ | Current account |

## 2.4 WHO/FAO Expert Bodies — Deep Analysis

### JECFA — Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives

- **Established:** 1956 (longest-running of the three)
- **Meets:** Typically twice a year
- **Evaluates:**
  - Food additives (preservatives, colours, sweeteners)
  - Contaminants (heavy metals, mycotoxins)
  - Residues of veterinary drugs in food
- **Key outputs:**
  - **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** for additives
  - **Tolerable Daily Intake (TDI)** for contaminants
  - **Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs)** for veterinary drugs
- **Pivotal principle:** Used by Codex Committees (CCFA, CCCF, CCRVDF)

### JEMRA — Joint FAO/WHO Expert Meetings on Microbiological Risk Assessment

- **Established:** 2000 (newer of the three)
- **Meets:** Ad-hoc basis on priority pathogens
- **Scope:** Risk assessment (qualitative + quantitative) for pathogens in food
- **Priority pathogens:**
  - *Salmonella* spp. in poultry, eggs
  - *Listeria monocytogenes* in ready-to-eat foods
  - *Campylobacter* spp. in broiler meat
  - *E. coli* O157:H7 in beef and leafy greens
  - *Cronobacter* spp. in infant formula
  - *Vibrio* spp. in seafood
- **Outputs:** Risk assessments that inform Codex (CCFH) decisions on food hygiene, microbial criteria

### JMPR — Joint FAO/WHO Meeting on Pesticide Residues

- **Established:** 1963
- **Meets:** Annually (since 1977; earlier biennially)
- **Evaluates:** Chemistry, toxicology, and residues of pesticides
- **Key outputs:**
  - **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)**
  - **Acute Reference Dose (ARfD)**
  - **Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs)** for pesticides in food/feed
- **Workflow:**
  1. Manufacturer submits dossier to national authority
  2. National authority submits to FAO/WHO
  3. JMPR reviews independently
  4. Codex Committee on Pesticide Residues (CCPR) endorses MRLs

### Comparative Matrix

| Feature | **JECFA** | **JEMRA** | **JMPR** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Year Established | 1956 | 2000 | 1963 |
| Hazard Domain | Chemical (additives, contaminants) | Microbiological | Chemical (pesticides) |
| Key Output | ADI, TDI, MRLs (vet drugs) | Risk assessments | ADI, ARfD, MRLs (pesticides) |
| Frequency | Biannual | Ad-hoc | Annual |
| Code Linked Committee | CCFA, CCCF, CCRVDF | CCFH | CCPR |
| Geographic Headquartered | Geneva | Geneva/FAO Rome | Geneva |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Note:** All three bodies produce **scientific advice only**. They do NOT themselves set standards. The **Codex Alimentarius Commission** (CAC) is the standards-setting body that uses this advice.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Models

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: SPS Risk-Based Decision Logic

This section presents the **decision logic** that an SPS-compliant national authority (e.g., FSSAI or a phytosanitary body) must follow when proposing a new food safety or phytosanitary measure. Each step mirrors the analytical framework of the SPS Agreement.

### Step 1 — Identify the Concern

$$\text{Step 1 Output} = \text{Define whether the concern is: } (i) \text{Food Safety}, (ii) \text{Animal Health}, (iii) \text{Plant Health}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The first filter is the scope check under SPS Article 1. If the concern does not fall into one of these three domains, the measure must instead be considered under TBT or GATT 1994.

### Step 2 — Check if International Standard Exists

$$\text{Step 2 Output} = \begin{cases} \text{Codex/IPPC/WOAH Standard Exists} \Rightarrow \text{Step 3} \\ \text{No Standard Exists} \Rightarrow \text{Step 4} \end{cases}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Article 3.1 mandates harmonization. If a Codex MRL exists, the member is presumed compliant.

### Step 3 — Apply International Standard (Default Path)

$$\text{Measure}_{compliant} = \text{Codex MRL or IPPC ISPM or WOAH chapter}$$

**Conversion Logic:** When a member adopts the international standard, the measure is *prima facie* consistent with SPS. No further justification required under Article 3.2.

### Step 4 — If Higher Level of Protection Chosen, Apply Article 3.3 Test

$$\text{Member must demonstrate:} \quad \text{Scientific Justification} \rightarrow \text{Risk Assessment} \rightarrow \text{Appropriate Level of Protection}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Article 3.3 allows deviation only if the member can prove, through risk assessment, that the international standard is insufficient to achieve their ALOP (Appropriate Level of Protection).

### Step 5 — Risk Assessment (Article 5)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk} &= f(H, C, E, RC) \\
\text{where } H &= \text{Hazard Identification} \\
C &= \text{Hazard Characterization (Dose-Response)} \\
E &= \text{Exposure Assessment} \\
RC &= \text{Risk Characterization}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each component is derived from peer-reviewed science or recognized international data. Generic statements are insufficient.

### Step 6 — Provisional Measures (Article 5.7 — Precautionary)

$$\text{When?} \Rightarrow \text{Scientific evidence is insufficient AND} \Rightarrow \text{Member seeks to obtain additional information AND} \Rightarrow \text{Measures are provisional}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Used famously in **EU – Beef Hormones** case (WTO 1998) and **Japan – Apples** case.

### Step 7 — Transparency (Article 7 & Annex B)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Member must:} \quad &(a) \text{ Notify WTO of new/changed measures} \\
&(b) \text{ Allow reasonable time for comments} \\
&(c) \text{ Designate enquiry point}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Failure to notify is a common cause of WTO disputes.

### Step 8 — Equivalence (Article 4)

$$
\text{Member A accepts Member B's measure} \iff \text{Measure achieves A's Appropriate Level of Protection}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Bilateral negotiation; "objective evidence" is the burden.

### Step 9 — Dispute Settlement

$$\text{Dispute} \rightarrow \text{Panel} \rightarrow \text{Appellate Body} \rightarrow \text{Retaliation (suspended concessions)}$$

**Conversion Logic:** If bilateral consultation fails (60-day period), complaint proceeds to WTO Dispute Settlement Body.

## 3.2 Worked Model: Customs Duty Calculation on Imported Food

### Problem Statement (KTU-style)

A food importer in India imports **10,000 kg of apple juice concentrate** with the following data:
- FOB value (supplier's invoice): **USD 50,000**
- Freight (sea): **USD 4,000**
- Insurance: **USD 500**
- Notified exchange rate by CBIC: **USD 1 = ₹83.50**
- Basic Customs Duty (BCD) on apple juice: **30%**
- Social Welfare Surcharge (SWS): **10% of BCD**
- IGST: **12%**

Compute the **total customs duty payable**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute CIF value (in USD)**

$$
\text{CIF} = \text{FOB} + \text{Freight} + \text{Insurance}
$$

$$
\text{CIF}_{USD} = 50{,}000 + 4{,}000 + 500 = 54{,}500 \text{ USD}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** For imports, Customs uses the CIF basis as the cost base.

**Step 2: Convert CIF to INR**

$$
\text{CIF}_{INR} = 54{,}500 \times 83.50 = 4{,}550{,}750 \text{ INR}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** CBIC-notified exchange rate (not market rate) is used.

**Step 3: Assessable Value = CIF in INR (assuming no other additions)**

$$
\text{AV} = 4{,}550{,}750 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Basic Customs Duty (BCD)**

$$
\text{BCD} = 30\% \times \text{AV} = 0.30 \times 4{,}550{,}750 = 1{,}365{,}225 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Social Welfare Surcharge (SWS)**

$$
\text{SWS} = 10\% \times \text{BCD} = 0.10 \times 1{,}365{,}225 = 136{,}522.50 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 6: Compute IGST**

$$
\text{IGST base} = \text{AV} + \text{BCD} + \text{SWS}
$$

$$
\text{IGST base} = 4{,}550{,}750 + 1{,}365{,}225 + 136{,}522.50 = 6{,}052{,}497.50 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
\text{IGST} = 12\% \times 6{,}052{,}497.50 = 726{,}299.70 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 7: Total Customs Duty Payable**

$$
\text{Total Duty} = \text{BCD} + \text{SWS} + \text{IGST}
$$

$$
\text{Total Duty} = 1{,}365{,}225 + 136{,}522.50 + 726{,}299.70 = 2{,}228{,}047.20 \text{ INR}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Customs Duty Payable} \approx ₹\,22{,}28{,}047.20}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective Duty Rate** = $D_{eff} = \frac{2{,}228{,}047.20}{4{,}550{,}750} \times 100 \approx 48.96\%$. This high effective rate is why the WTO bound rates matter — India must respect WTO commitments on bound vs applied rates.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Model: JECFA Evaluation for a Food Additive

### Problem Context
Suppose a food company submits data on a **new sweetener, Compound X**, for JECFA evaluation.

### Step-by-Step Evaluation Logic

**Step 1: Sponsor compiles dossier** — Chemistry, manufacturing, use levels, residues, toxicology, exposure.

**Step 2: FAO/WHO convene JECFA meeting** — Independent experts (no industry observers in scientific session).

**Step 3: Hazard Identification**

$$
\text{Question:} \quad \text{Does Compound X have intrinsic toxic potential at relevant doses?}
$$

**Step 4: Hazard Characterization (Dose-Response)**

$$
\text{Derive:} \quad \text{NOAEL} \rightarrow \text{Apply Safety Factor (typically 100)} \rightarrow \text{ADI}
$$

$$
\text{ADI} = \frac{\text{NOAEL (mg/kg bw/day)}}{\text{Safety Factor (100)}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Safety factor of 100 = 10 for intra-species variation × 10 for inter-species variation.

**Step 5: Exposure Assessment**

$$
\text{Estimated Daily Intake (EDI)} = \sum_{i} (\text{Concentration in food}_i \times \text{Consumption of food}_i)
$$

**Step 6: Risk Characterization**

$$
\text{Margin of Safety (MoS)} = \frac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{Estimated Human Exposure}}
$$

If $\text{MoS} \geq 100$ and $\text{EDI} \leq \text{ADI}$ → **Acceptable**.

**Step 7: JECFA Decision**

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Acceptable} \Rightarrow \text{ADI established} \\ \text{Unacceptable} \Rightarrow \text{Data deficient or toxicological concern} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 8: Codex CCFA considers JECFA advice** → May establish **GSFA (General Standard for Food Additives)** maximum use level.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: SPS vs TBT Dispute Identification

Use this matrix to identify which agreement applies in any trade scenario:

| Scenario | Likely SPS | Likely TBT | Why |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Country bans pork imports due to swine fever | ✔ | | Animal disease risk |
| Country mandates all imported chocolate declare cocoa % on label | | ✔ | Labelling requirement |
| Country rejects wheat due to mycotoxin content | ✔ | | Food safety (chemical contaminant) |
| Country requires imported biscuits to be of a specific shape | | ✔ | Technical regulation (non-health) |
| Country bans oysters due to *Vibrio cholerae* | ✔ | | Microbiological food safety |
| Country requires imported coffee to undergo specific roast test | | ✔ | Conformity assessment |
| Country quarantines plants due to fruit fly | ✔ | | Phytosanitary risk |
| Country requires warning labels on genetically modified food | | ✔ | Labelling/deceptive practices |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 WTO SPS & TBT — Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WTO Agreement 1995] --> B[Annex 1A Multilateral Trade Agreements]
    B --> C[SPS Agreement]
    B --> D[TBT Agreement]
    C --> C1[Codex Alimentarius FAO WHO]
    C --> C2[IPPC Plant Health]
    C --> C3[WOAH Animal Health]
    D --> D1[ISO International Organization for Standardization]
    D --> D2[IEC International Electrotechnical Commission]
    D --> D3[ITU International Telecommunication Union]
    C --> E1[Risk Assessment Art 5]
    C --> E2[Equivalence Art 4]
    C --> E3[Transparency Art 7]
    D --> F1[Legitimate Objectives Art 2.2]
    D --> F2[Conformity Assessment Art 5]
    D --> F3[Code of Good Practice Annex 3]
    E1 --> G[National Authorities e.g. FSSAI India]
    F1 --> G
```

## 4.2 SPS Decision Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Food Safety or Phytosanitary Measure Proposed] --> B{Concern within SPS scope?}
    B -->|No| C[TBT or GATT 1994 applies]
    B -->|Yes| D{International standard exists?}
    D -->|Yes - Codex IPPC WOAH| E[Adopt standard - presumed compliant]
    D -->|No| F[Conduct risk assessment]
    E --> G[Notify WTO SPS Committee]
    F --> H{Risk supports higher level of protection?}
    H -->|No| E
    H -->|Yes| I[Apply Article 3.3 scientific justification]
    I --> G
    G --> J[Implement measure with transparency]
```

```mermaid
    B[WHO] --> C

    C --> D3[JMPR 1963 Pesticide Residues]
    D1 --> E[Codex Alimentarius Commission]

```

```mermaid
    A[Exporter obtains IEC from DGFT] --> B[Register with product council APEDA EIC MPEDA]
    B --> C[Procure goods meeting importer specifications]
```
