# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the scope and objectives of the WTO SPS Agreement.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Agreement on the Application of Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures (SPS)** is an **Annex 1A** agreement of the **WTO** that came into force on **1 January 1995**.

**Scope (1.5 marks):** It applies to all measures whose purpose is to:
1. Protect human or animal life from food-borne risks (sanitary)
2. Protect human life from diseases carried by plants/animals
3. Protect animal/plant life from pests and diseases (phytosanitary)

**Objectives (1.5 marks):**
1. Allow members to adopt necessary SPS measures for health protection
2. Ensure these measures do not become disguised restrictions on trade
3. Promote harmonization with international standards (Codex Alimentarius, IPPC, WOAH)
4. Encourage use of risk assessment, equivalence, and transparency

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse SPS with TBT. Remember: **SPS = biological/chemical safety risk**. If the measure concerns *labelling, packaging, quality, or performance* — it is **TBT**.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Briefly explain the functions of JECFA and JMPR.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**JECFA (1.5 marks):** The **Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives**, established in **1956**, evaluates the safety of:
- **Food additives** (preservatives, colours, sweeteners, antioxidants)
- **Contaminants** in food (heavy metals, mycotoxins)
- **Residues of veterinary drugs**
It establishes the **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** for additives and **Tolerable Daily Intake (TDI)** for contaminants.

**JMPR (1.5 marks):** The **Joint FAO/WHO Meeting on Pesticide Residues**, established in **1963**, evaluates the safety of pesticides. It establishes:
- **ADI** for chronic exposure
- **Acute Reference Dose (ARfD)** for short-term exposure
- **Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs)** of pesticides in food and feed
It reviews pesticide dossiers submitted by national authorities.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme regulations: For each Part B question, the student has an **internal choice** (either Question A or Question B). Both questions carry equal marks.

---

### Question A `[14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principles and obligations** of the SPS Agreement in detail. Discuss the role of international standard-setting bodies under the agreement.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between the **SPS and TBT Agreements** with suitable examples. Why is this distinction important in international food trade?

---

#### Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) Model Solution [7 Marks]

The SPS Agreement rests on **seven core principles**:

**1. Scientific Justification (Article 2.2):** [1 mark] Measures must be based on scientific principles and not maintained without sufficient scientific evidence.

**2. Risk Assessment (Article 5.1–5.3):** [1 mark] Members must base their measures on an assessment of the actual risk to human, animal, or plant health, using recognized techniques developed by relevant international organizations.

**3. Non-Discrimination (Article 2.3):** [1 mark] SPS measures must not arbitrarily or unjustifiably discriminate between members where identical or similar conditions prevail, including between domestic and imported products.

**4. Transparency (Article 7):** [1 mark] Members must notify other members of any new or changed SPS measures through the WTO Secretariat, allow time for comments, and provide an enquiry point.

**5. Equivalence (Article 4):** [1 mark] Members shall accept the SPS measures of other members as equivalent, even if different, provided they achieve the member's appropriate level of protection.

**6. Harmonization with International Standards (Article 3):** [1 mark] Members are encouraged to base their measures on international standards, guidelines, and recommendations.

**7. Regionalization (Article 6):** [1 mark] Measures must be adapted to the SPS characteristics of the area from which the product originated.

**Role of International Standard-Setting Bodies:**
- **Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO):** Food safety standards, MRLs for additives, contaminants, pesticide residues, hygienic codes of practice.
- **International Plant Protection Convention (IPPC):** International Standards for Phytosanitary Measures (ISPMs).
- **World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH, formerly OIE):** Animal health standards.

These three bodies form the **benchmarks** under Article 3.1; their standards are deemed necessary for the protection of health.

##### Part (b) Model Solution [7 Marks]

| Basis of Differentiation | **SPS Agreement** | **TBT Agreement** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Protect humans, animals, plants from biological and food-safety risks | Prevent technical regulations/standards from creating unnecessary trade barriers |
| **Type of Risk** | Real health risk (pathogens, pests, toxins) | Quality, performance, labelling, deceptive practices |
| **International Reference Body** | Codex, IPPC, WOAH | ISO, IEC, ITU |
| **Risk Assessment** | Mandatory under Article 5 | Not mandatory |
| **Precautionary Principle** | Explicit in Article 5.7 | Implicit |
| **Example 1** | Ban on imported poultry due to avian influenza | Mandatory labeling requirement for packaged food showing ingredients |
| **Example 2** | Rejecting wheat shipment due to aflatoxin levels exceeding Codex MRL | Requiring all imported chocolate to be tested by an accredited laboratory |
| **Example 3** | Quarantine of imported apple consignment due to fruit fly | Requirement that imported biscuits use a specified net-weight typeface |
| **Example 4** | Refusal of milk powder due to melamine contamination | Ban on a particular food packaging material due to environmental concern |

**Importance of the distinction (1 mark):**
1. **Dispute identification** — Wrong categorization can lead to a member citing the wrong agreement in a WTO dispute.
2. **Standard-setting** — SPS measures align with Codex; TBT aligns with ISO/IEC.
3. **Domestic regulatory design** — FSSAI notifications fall under SPS-equivalent rationale; BIS standards under TBT.
4. **Trade negotiation** — Different negotiating frameworks and bilateral protocols apply.

---

### Question B `[14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **role and functions of WHO/FAO Expert Bodies** — namely **JECFA, JEMRA, and JMPR** — in setting international food standards.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **regulatory framework for food imports and exports in India**. Discuss the role of DGFT, EIC, and FSSAI.

---

#### Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) Model Solution [7 Marks]

The FAO and WHO jointly sponsor **three independent scientific expert bodies** that provide the **scientific foundation** for international food standards.

**JECFA — Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives (1956):** [2 marks]
- Evaluates the safety of food additives, contaminants, and residues of veterinary drugs.
- Establishes **ADI (Acceptable Daily Intake)** for additives and **TDI (Tolerable Daily Intake)** for contaminants.
- Provides data to the Codex Committees on Food Additives (CCFA), Contaminants in Food (CCCF), and Residues of Veterinary Drugs (CCRVDF).

**JEMRA — Joint FAO/WHO Expert Meetings on Microbiological Risk Assessment (2000):** [2 marks]
- Performs qualitative and quantitative risk assessments for microbiological hazards in food.
- Priority pathogens: *Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Campylobacter, E. coli* O157:H7, *Cronobacter, Vibrio* spp.
- Provides guidance to the Codex Committee on Food Hygiene (CCFH) on microbial criteria and risk-based control measures.

**JMPR — Joint FAO/WHO Meeting on Pesticide Residues (1963):** [2 marks]
- Reviews the chemistry, toxicology, metabolism, and residue data of pesticides.
- Establishes **ADI**, **ARfD (Acute Reference Dose)**, and **MRLs** for pesticides in food and feed.
- Provides data to the Codex Committee on Pesticide Residues (CCPR).

**Significance (1 mark):** The outputs of these three bodies form the **scientific bedrock** of Codex standards. Codex standards, in turn, are referenced by the **SPS Agreement** as the benchmark for international food trade.

##### Part (b) Model Solution [7 Marks]

India's food import-export regulatory framework involves multiple authorities operating under different statutes:

**1. DGFT — Directorate General of Foreign Trade (under Ministry of Commerce):** [2 marks]
- Statutory basis: **Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992**.
- Issues **IEC (Import Export Code)** — a 10-digit mandatory code for any importer/exporter.
- Issues **Advance Authorization** for duty-free import of inputs for export production.
- Administers **EPCG (Export Promotion Capital Goods)** scheme.
- Publishes the **Foreign Trade Policy (FTP)** and **Handbook of Procedures**.

**2. EIC — Export Inspection Council (under Ministry of Commerce):** [2 marks]
- Statutory basis: **Export Inspection Council Act, 1963**.
- Mandate: Pre-shipment inspection and certification of export consignments of notified commodities.
- Ensures Indian exports meet the **quality and safety requirements** of importing countries.
- Designated as the issuing authority for **Certificate of Inspection (COI)** and **Health Certificates**.

**3. FSSAI — Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (under Ministry of Health & Family Welfare):** [3 marks]
- Statutory basis: **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006**.
- Regulates **manufacture, storage, distribution, sale, and import** of food.
- Operates a network of **food laboratories** for sample testing.
- Mandates **food import clearance** — every consignment of imported food must be inspected and cleared at the **point of entry** (port/airport) before release.
- Issues **FSSAI license/registration** to all Food Business Operators (FBOs).
- Reference standards: **FSSAI's Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations**, **Contaminants Regulations**, etc.
- Responsible for **product recall, market surveillance**, and **labelling compliance** under the FSS (Labelling & Display) Regulations, 2020.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing DGFT with Customs.** DGFT = policy & licensing. Customs = duty & clearance.
> 2. **Forgetting that FSSAI clearance is mandatory even for food imports arriving by post/courier.**
> 3. **Mixing the founding years of expert bodies.** JECFA = **1956**, JMPR = **1963**, JEMRA = **2000**.
> 4. **Saying JECFA "sets standards."** It only gives *scientific advice*. Standards are set by **Codex Committees** and then adopted by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 SPS Agreement — Key Recap
- **SPS = Sanitary (animal/human) + Phytosanitary (plant)** measures.
- Came into force **1 January 1995** with WTO.
- **Article 3** — harmonization with Codex, IPPC, WOAH.
- **Article 5** — risk assessment mandatory.
- **Article 5.7** — precautionary principle.
- **Article 7** — transparency obligation.
- **Article 4** — equivalence.

### 🔑 TBT Agreement — Key Recap
- Applies to **technical regulations, standards, conformity assessment**.
- Came into force **1 January 1995** with WTO.
- ISO/IEC/ITU are the international standard-setters for TBT.
- Code of Good Practice in Annex 3.
- **Not the same as SPS** — TBT deals with non-biological/quality/label issues.

### 🔑 Export & Import Laws — Key Recap
- **FTDR Act 1992** → DGFT.
- **Customs Act 1962** → CBIC.
- **FSSAI Act 2006** → FSSAI.
- **EIC Act 1963** → EIC.
- **IEC is mandatory** for ₹25,000+ transactions.
- CIF is the import value base; FOB for exports.
- **FSSAI clearance at port** is mandatory for all food imports.

### 🔑 WHO/FAO Expert Bodies — Key Recap
- **JECFA (1956)** → Food additives, contaminants, vet drug residues → ADI/TDI/MRLs.
- **JEMRA (2000)** → Microbiological risk assessment → Risk assessments.
- **JMPR (1963)** → Pesticide residues → ADI/ARfD/MRLs.
- All three → advise **Codex Committees** → standards adopted by **Codex Alimentarius Commission** → referenced in **SPS Agreement**.

### 🔑 Mnemonic for SPS Articles
**"S**cience, **R**isk, **E**quivalence, **T**ransparency, **H**armonization, **R**egionalization" → **SRETHR**.

### 🔑 Mnemonic for Expert Bodies
**"J**ecfa = **A**dditives; **J**empra = **M**icrobio; **J**mpr = **P**esticides" → **AMP**.

### 🔑 Code References — Always Mention in Answers
- **FSS Act 2006, Section 25** — Prohibited articles.
- **FSS Act 2006, Section 47** — Import regulations.
- **Customs Act 1962, Section 2(33)** — Definition of "import."
- **FTDR Act 1992, Section 3** — DGFT powers.
- **EIC Act 1963** — Pre-shipment inspection mandate.

### 🔑 Frequently Confused Pairs
- **DGFT vs Customs** — Policy vs Duty.
- **SPS vs TBT** — Health risk vs Technical/Quality.
- **JECFA vs JMPR** — Additives vs Pesticides.
- **CIF vs FOB** — Imports vs Exports.
- **BIS (national standards) vs Codex (international)** — TBT reference vs SPS reference.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voluntary Quality Standards and Certifications – GMP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT723 - FOOD LAWS AND LEGISLATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Voluntary Quality Standards and Certifications – GMP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP)** is a voluntary, internationally recognized quality assurance system that defines the operational and environmental conditions required to ensure that food products are consistently produced and controlled to quality standards appropriate to their intended use. GMP is a **prerequisite program (PRP)** that establishes the foundational hygiene, sanitation, and process control baseline upon which advanced systems such as **HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point)** and food safety management systems like **ISO 22000** are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Codex Alimentarius, WHO/FAO):**
> *GMP represents the practical, operational foundation of food safety — it specifies the minimum sanitary and hygienic practices required in the manufacture, packaging, storage, and distribution of food products to ensure that products are safe for human consumption and fit for trade.*

### 1.2 Distinguishing Mandatory vs. Voluntary Standards

| Aspect | Mandatory Standards | Voluntary Standards (GMP, ISO) |
|---|---|---|
| Legal force | Enforceable by law | Adopted by choice for market access |
| Issuing body | Government regulator (FSSAI, FDA, EFSA) | International bodies (Codex, ISO, BRC, IFS) |
| Example | FSSAI Licensing, BIS Standards | GMP, GHP, HACCP, ISO 22000 |
| Compliance | Statutory obligation | Commercial / reputational incentive |

> [!NOTE]
> Although GMP is technically **voluntary**, in modern food trade it has become a **de facto mandatory requirement** because retailers, importers, and regulatory audits globally reject suppliers without GMP certification.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Clean Kitchen Rulebook" Intuition

Imagine a small-scale home kitchen preparing sweets for a large Diwali/Onam festival order:

- You would instinctively insist that the cook **washes hands**, the **utensils are spotless**, the **ingredients are stored off the floor**, and the **cooking temperature** is monitored.
- You would also demand that the same recipe yields the same taste **every batch**.
- **GMP is precisely this rulebook — but formalized, documented, audited, and enforced in a factory setting.**

Without GMP, HACCP becomes meaningless: a HACCP plan on a dirty, uncontrolled line is like a fire evacuation plan in an unmarked, cluttered building — technically documented but practically unworkable.

### 1.4 Key Operational Metrics in GMP

The following **physical, microbiological, and process constants** are highlighted in KTU's GMP module:

- **Process temperature threshold for hot holding:** **≥ 63 °C**
- **Cold chain storage temperature:** **≤ 5 °C** (chilled) / **≤ -18 °C** (frozen)
- **Maximum ambient humidity in processing halls:** **< 70 % RH**
- **Aerobic Plate Count (APC) limits** for cleaned equipment: **< 100 CFU/cm²**
- **Personal hygiene:** hairnets, gloves, no jewellery, no open wounds, hand-wash every **30 minutes** during production.

These numbers are not arbitrary — they are derived from decades of epidemiological data correlating process deviations with foodborne illness outbreaks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The 10 Pillars of GMP (Codex/WHO Framework)

GMP operates on **ten interdependent principles**. Each pillar addresses a specific failure mode that has historically caused food contamination incidents.

1. **Design and Layout of Premises** — Single-direction flow of raw → process → finished goods to avoid cross-contamination.
2. **Sanitation and Hygiene of Premises** — Documented cleaning schedules using validated chemical concentrations.
3. **Equipment Design and Maintenance** — Food-grade stainless steel (SS 304 / SS 316), smooth welds, no dead legs.
4. **Personnel Hygiene and Training** — Medical fitness certificates, induction training, periodic refresher courses.
5. **Raw Material Control** — Approved supplier list (ASL), incoming inspection, allergen segregation.
6. **Production and Process Control** — Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), batch records, in-process checks.
7. **Water and Air Quality Control** — Potable water tested monthly; filtered positive air pressure in packaging zones.
8. **Pest Control** — Integrated Pest Management (IPM) with rodent stations, insectocutors, no chemical sprays near open product.
9. **Waste Management** — Segregated colour-coded bins, daily removal, documented disposal chain.
10. **Documentation and Record Keeping** — Traceability records, retention of documents for **minimum one product shelf-life + 1 year** (or **5 years**, whichever is longer).

### 2.2 The "Why-How" Logic — Why Each Principle Exists

| Pillar | Failure Without It (Real Case) | How GMP Prevents It |
|---|---|---|
| Layout | Listeria cross-flow in dairy plant (2015 EU outbreak) | Physical separation & airflow control |
| Sanitation | E. coli from poorly cleaned slicing machines (2018 US) | CIP (Clean-in-Place) + swab testing |
| Personnel | Norovirus from infected handler (cruise ships, 2019) | Health screening + glove/hairnet protocols |
| Raw materials | Sudan-I dye scandal in chilli powder (2005) | ASL + COA verification |
| Documentation | Horsemeat scandal in Europe (2013) | Batch traceability within 4 hours |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — GMP Requirements Matrix

| GMP Element | Specific Requirement | Verification Frequency | Responsible Authority |
|---|---|---|---|
| Premises | Floors: epoxy / non-absorbent; walls: light-coloured tiles | Daily visual; weekly deep clean | Plant Maintenance |
| Lighting | **≥ 300 lux** at inspection points; **≥ 540 lux** on production lines | Lux meter check monthly | Engineering |
| Ventilation | **15–20 air changes/hour**; filtered (G4 + F8) | Filter replacement quarterly | HVAC Team |
| Water | Potable; < **1 CFU/100 mL** coliform | Microbiological test monthly | QA Lab |
| Equipment | SS 304/316; Ra ≤ 0.8 µm surface finish | Calibration annually | Engineering |
| Personnel | Hairnets, gloves, no jewellery, beard covers | Continuous supervisor check | Production Supervisor |
| Storage | FIFO/FEFO; **15 cm off floor**; **45 cm off walls** | Daily stack inspection | Warehouse Manager |
| Pest Control | Bait stations every **6–8 m**; UV insectocutors | Service monthly | Contracted agency |
| Allergens | Dedicated lines or validated cleaning | Allergen swab annually | QA Manager |
| Documentation | SOPs, batch records, logbooks | Internal audit every 6 months | QA Department |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** Always cite the **three evidence pillars** of GMP — *what you do (procedure)*, *how you prove it (record)*, and *how you verify it (audit)*. Examiners reward answers that link a requirement to its verification method.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of GMP

- **Export Compliance:** EU, US-FDA, GCC, ASEAN markets reject shipments without GMP evidence.
- **Retailer Mandates:** Walmart, Tesco, Carrefour, and Reliance Retail require GMP/GFSI-recognized certification.
- **Insurance Premiums:** Certified plants attract **15–25 % lower** product liability insurance premiums.
- **Litigation Defence:** In court cases, GMP records serve as *prima facie* evidence of due diligence.
- **Operational Efficiency:** Empirical studies show GMP-compliant plants reduce batch rejection rates by **40–60 %** versus non-compliant units.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Logical Derivations

### 3.1 GMP Implementation Roadmap — Exhaustive 12-Step Framework

Because GMP is a **process-driven** (not formula-driven) topic, the analytical derivation here is a **logical causal chain** — each step is a precondition for the next.

**Step 1: Top Management Commitment**
Management must formally declare GMP implementation as a strategic objective through a written policy signed by the CEO. Without this, every later step fails due to lack of authority and budget.

**Step 2: Gap Analysis Against Codex/FSSAI GMP Benchmarks**
The current state of the facility is audited against the **11 GMP Schedule requirements** of FSSAI (Schedule 4 of *Food Safety and Standards (Licensing and Registration of Food Businesses) Regulations, 2011*).

$$ \text{Gap Score (\%)} = \frac{\text{Compliant Requirements}}{\text{Total Requirements}} \times 100 $$

For example, if 47 of 60 sub-requirements are met:

$$ \text{Gap Score} = \frac{47}{60} \times 100 = 78.33 \ \% $$

A score **below 80 %** indicates a major implementation project; **above 80 %** suggests a focused upgrade.

**Step 3: Plant Layout Redesign**
Establish unidirectional flow: **raw material receipt → storage → processing → packaging → finished goods dispatch**. Logical separation of high-risk (e.g., raw meat) and low-risk (e.g., packaged bread) zones. Cross-flow boundaries require physical barriers or temporal separation.

**Step 4: Infrastructure Upgrades**
Install epoxy flooring, SS 304 wall cladding (up to **1.5 m** height), washable ceilings, foot-operated wash basins, soap dispensers, hand-dryers, and colour-coded segregated waste bins (typically **green (wet/biodegradable)**, **blue (dry/recyclable)**, **red (biohazard)**, **black (general)**).

**Step 5: Equipment Procurement and Validation**
All product-contact surfaces must be food-grade **SS 304 or SS 316** with surface roughness **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** (mirror finish) to prevent microbial harborage in micro-crevices.

**Step 6: Water and Effluent Treatment**
Install reverse osmosis (RO) or UV-treated potable water system. Daily residual chlorine maintained at **0.2–0.5 ppm** at point of use. Effluent must meet CPCB/SPCB discharge norms before release.

**Step 7: Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures (SSOPs)**
Document, for every equipment surface:
- *What* chemical is used (e.g., 200 ppm quaternary ammonium)
- *How long* it contacts (e.g., 30 seconds)
- *Concentration* (validated by titration strips)
- *Verification* swab (e.g., ATP bioluminescence **< 100 RLU**)

**Step 8: Personnel Training and Medical Surveillance**
Every worker undergoes:
- Pre-employment medical fitness (TB, typhoid, hepatitis B screening)
- GMP induction (typically **8 hours**)
- Annual refresher (typically **4 hours**)
- Specific task training (e.g., allergen handling)

**Step 9: Documentation System**
Implement a **four-tier document hierarchy**:

$$
\text{L1 Policy} \rightarrow \text{L2 Procedure} \rightarrow \text{L3 SOP} \rightarrow \text{L4 Record/Log}
$$

For instance:

- L1: *Quality Policy* (1 page, CEO signed)
- L2: *Sanitation Procedure* (5–10 pages)
- L3: *Equipment Cleaning SOP-SS-014* (2–3 pages with diagrams)
- L4: *Daily Cleaning Log-SS-014* (filled daily by operator)

**Step 10: Internal Audits and Management Review**
Internal audits conducted **every 6 months** by trained internal auditors. Findings classified as Critical (immediate stop), Major (30-day closure), Minor (90-day closure). Management reviews audit results quarterly.

**Step 11: Pre-Certification Audit (Stage 1 & Stage 2)**
A third-party certifying body (e.g., TÜV, SGS, Bureau Veritas) performs:
- **Stage 1:** Documentation review (typically 1 day)
- **Stage 2:** On-site implementation audit (typically 2–5 days depending on size)

**Step 12: Continuous Improvement via PDCA**
Apply the **Plan-Do-Check-Act** cycle perpetually:

$$
\text{P} \xrightarrow{\text{Plan}} \text{D} \xrightarrow{\text{Do}} \text{C} \xrightarrow{\text{Check}} \text{A} \xrightarrow{\text{Act}} \text{P (improved)}
$$

Each cycle reduces deviations by approximately **10–20 %**, targeting a long-term GMP compliance rate of **> 95 %**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — GMP vs. HACCP vs. ISO 22000

| Dimension | GMP | HACCP | ISO 22000 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scope | Operational hygiene & process control | Hazard control at critical points | Management system integration |
| Origin | WHO/Codex (1969) | NASA/Pillsbury (1960s) | ISO (2005) |
| Mandatory? | Voluntary (de facto mandatory) | Voluntary | Voluntary |
| Prerequisite? | Foundation | Requires GMP | Requires GMP+HACCP |
| Documentation | SOPs and logs | CCP monitoring records | Full management system manual |
| Audit cycle | 6 months internal | 12 months internal | 12 months external |

### 3.3 Logical Proof — Why GMP Must Precede HACCP

**Premise 1:** HACCP identifies *critical control points* (CCPs) where significant hazards can be prevented, eliminated, or reduced to acceptable levels.

**Premise 2:** A CCP fails if the surrounding environment (equipment cleanliness, personnel hygiene, raw material integrity) is uncontrolled.

**Premise 3:** GMP provides exactly that environmental control — clean equipment, trained personnel, controlled raw materials.

**Conclusion:** Therefore, GMP is a **logically necessary** (not merely desirable) precondition for HACCP.

This is why international standard setters — Codex Alimentarius, FSSC 22000, BRC, IFS — all state: *"HACCP systems can only be effectively implemented where prerequisite programs including GMP are in place."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP) as applied to the food industry. List any six major elements of GMP.*

**Model Answer:**
GMP is a voluntary quality assurance system that establishes the operational, hygienic, and environmental conditions required to consistently produce safe and quality food. It serves as the foundational prerequisite program for HACCP and ISO 22000.

**Six major elements:**
1. Premises design and layout
2. Equipment design and maintenance
3. Sanitation and hygiene
4. Personnel training and medical fitness
5. Raw material and water control
6. Documentation and record keeping
7. Pest control
8. Production and process control

*(Any six with one-line description fetches full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is GMP considered a prerequisite for the implementation of HACCP? Explain briefly.*

**Model Answer:**
HACCP relies on the identification and monitoring of Critical Control Points (CCPs). A CCP can only function reliably when the surrounding environment — equipment cleanliness, personnel hygiene, raw material integrity — is controlled. GMP provides exactly this foundational control. Without GMP, CCP monitoring data becomes unreliable, leading to false-negative hazard decisions. Codex Alimentarius explicitly mandates that *“HACCP systems can only be effectively implemented where prerequisite programs including GMP are already in place.”*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students frequently lose marks by (a) writing only definitions without linking to *why* each requirement exists, (b) forgetting the **frequency/responsibility** dimension of any GMP requirement, and (c) omitting Indian regulatory anchors (FSSAI Schedule 4). Always structure Part B answers using **element → requirement → verification → responsible person**.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the ten major requirements of GMP as prescribed by Codex Alimentarius / FSSAI for a food manufacturing establishment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Premises — Design and Layout:** Floors must be impervious, washable, non-toxic, with slopes ≥ 1:100 towards drains. Walls tiled up to 1.5 m with epoxy paint above. Ceilings smooth, light-coloured. Single-direction flow of material and personnel to prevent cross-contamination.

**2. Equipment:** All product-contact equipment in SS 304 / SS 316 with surface roughness Ra ≤ 0.8 µm. No dead legs, wooden surfaces, or lead-based solder. Calibrated annually with traceability to NABL/NPL standards.

**3. Sanitation:** Documented SSOPs for every equipment and zone. Cleaning-in-Place (CIP) for closed systems; manual Cleaning-Out-of-Place (COP) for disassembled parts. Verification by ATP swab (<100 RLU) and microbiological swab (<100 CFU/cm²).

**4. Personnel Hygiene:** Pre-employment medical fitness, daily health declaration, no jewellery/watches, hairnets/beard covers, gloves changed every 30 minutes or on contamination. Hand-wash stations at every entry/exit point.

**5. Raw Material Control:** Approved Supplier List (ASL) maintained. Each incoming batch accompanied by Certificate of Analysis (COA). Allergen segregation, FIFO/FEFO inventory, cold chain integrity verified by data loggers.

**6. Production Control:** Batch Manufacturing Records (BMR) for every batch. In-process checks at defined frequencies — temperature, pH, viscosity, weight — with tolerances documented.

**7. Water Quality:** Potable water tested daily for residual chlorine (0.2–0.5 ppm) and monthly microbiologically (<1 CFU/100 mL coliforms). Effluent tested daily against discharge norms.

**8. Pest Control:** Integrated Pest Management (IPM). Rodent stations every 6–8 m. UV insectocutors at all entry points. No chemical sprays in production zone.

**9. Waste Management:** Colour-coded segregated bins (green/blue/red/black), daily removal, documented disposal to authorized vendor. Spillage procedure in place.

**10. Documentation:** Four-tier hierarchy (Policy → Procedure → SOP → Record). Records retained for *one product shelf-life + 1 year*, or *5 years*, whichever is longer.

---

**(b)** Explain the role of GMP in ensuring food safety. How does GMP differ from HACCP and ISO 22000? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role in Food Safety:**
GMP prevents contamination at source by controlling the manufacturing environment itself, rather than testing the end product. It reduces microbial load, chemical residues, physical hazards, and allergen cross-contact. GMP also enables traceability — every batch can be traced forward to customer and backward to supplier within 4 hours (KTU/Codex requirement).

**Difference Table:**

| Aspect | GMP | HACCP | ISO 22000 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Focus | Hygiene & process environment | Hazard control at CCPs | Management system |
| Approach | Prescriptive (what to do) | Risk-based (where risks are) | System-based (how to manage) |
| Scope | Operational | Specific product/process | Entire organization |
| Prerequisite relationship | Foundation for HACCP | Built on GMP | Integrates both |

**Integrated View:** In a modern food plant, **GMP provides the floor**, **HACCP provides the safety net**, and **ISO 22000 provides the management system** that governs both. They are not alternatives — they are layers of the same safety architecture.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Describe the step-by-step implementation of GMP in a food processing unit. Include the documentation hierarchy in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Top Management Commitment:** Written GMP policy signed by CEO, budget allocation, GMP Steering Committee formed.

**Step 2 — Gap Analysis:** Current state assessed against FSSAI Schedule 4 / Codex GMP requirements. Gap score computed as compliant requirements ÷ total × 100.

**Step 3 — Layout Redesign:** Unidirectional flow, cross-contamination barriers installed.

**Step 4 — Infrastructure Upgrades:** Epoxy flooring, SS wall cladding, washable ceilings, foot-operated wash basins, colour-coded bins.

**Step 5 — Equipment Validation:** SS 304/316 procurement with surface roughness certification, calibration certificates.

**Step 6 — Water Treatment:** RO/UV system, daily residual chlorine monitoring, monthly microbiological testing.

**Step 7 — SSOPs:** Documented cleaning procedures for every zone and equipment, validated concentrations and contact times.

**Step 8 — Personnel Training:** Induction training (8 hours), annual refresher (4 hours), task-specific modules, medical surveillance.

**Step 9 — Documentation Hierarchy (L1 → L4):**

$$ \text{L1 Quality Manual} \to \text{L2 Procedures} \to \text{L3 SOPs} \to \text{L4 Records and Logs} $$

**Step 10 — Internal Audits:** Every 6 months; findings tracked to closure.

**Step 11 — Third-Party Certification:** Stage 1 (documentation) + Stage 2 (on-site audit) by accredited body.

**Step 12 — PDCA Cycle:** Plan → Do → Check → Act continuous improvement.

---

**(b)** Explain the significance of personnel hygiene, water quality, and pest control as three critical GMP elements. Support your answer with one real-world contamination case for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Personnel Hygiene:** Improper handwashing has caused numerous outbreaks — the **2011 E. coli O104:H4 outbreak in Germany** (3,842 cases, 53 deaths) was traced to infected food handlers. GMP mandates hand-wash stations at every entry, glove protocols, medical fitness, and hair/beard covers.

**Water Quality:** The **1996 Cyclospora outbreak in the USA** (1,465 cases) was linked to contaminated rinse water on raspberries imported from Guatemala. GMP mandates daily residual chlorine checks (0.2–0.5 ppm) and monthly microbiological testing (<1 CFU/100 mL coliforms).

**Pest Control:** The **2008 Salmonella outbreak in the USA** (1,442 cases, 286 hospitalizations) was traced to a peanut processing plant with rodent infestation. GMP mandates IPM, rodent bait stations every 6–8 m, UV insectocutors, and contracted monthly pest-control service with documented visit reports.

**Conclusion:** Each of these three elements addresses a *historically proven* contamination pathway. GMP codifies lessons learned from outbreaks into preventive controls — this is the essence of risk-based food safety management.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **GMP is voluntary** but a *de facto* mandatory requirement for food trade.
- ✅ GMP is the **foundation**; HACCP is the **safety net**; ISO 22000 is the **management umbrella**.
- ✅ FSSAI enforces GMP through **Schedule 4** of the Licensing Regulations, 2011.
- ✅ The **ten pillars** are: premises, equipment, sanitation, personnel, raw materials, production, water, pests, waste, documentation.
- ✅ Key metrics: **≥ 63 °C hot hold**, **≤ 5 °C cold hold**, **< 70 % RH**, **< 100 CFU/cm² on equipment**, **0.2–0.5 ppm chlorine**.
- ✅ Documentation hierarchy: **L1 Policy → L2 Procedure → L3 SOP → L4 Record**.
- ✅ Record retention: **max(product shelf-life + 1 year, 5 years)**.
- ✅ Internal audits: **every 6 months**; certification audit cycle: **every 12 months**.
- ✅ Continuous improvement via **PDCA cycle**.
- ✅ Always cite **Codex Alimentarius, WHO, FSSAI Schedule 4** in exam answers to score high on regulatory anchoring.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "GMP-SANDWICH":**
> **G**ood layout, **M**aintenance, **P**ersonnel, **S**anitation, **A**llergen control, **N**o pests, **D**ocumentation, **W**ater, **I**nternal audit, **C**ontinuous improvement, **H**ACCP-ready.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Good Animal Husbandry Practices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT723 - FOOD LAWS AND LEGISLATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Good Animal Husbandry Practices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Good Animal Husbandry Practices (GAHP)** refers to the set of principles, codes of practice, and management standards applied at the **primary production level** of the food chain to ensure that food derived from animals (meat, milk, eggs, honey, fish, and their by-products) is **safe, suitable, and wholesome** for human consumption. GAHP encompasses the holistic management of animal health, animal welfare, feeding, housing, hygiene, veterinary drug usage, environmental sustainability, and traceability from "farm to fork."

Under the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO)** framework and the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (FSSAI, India)**, GAHP is recognized as the foundational layer of any **Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point (HACCP)** or **Food Safety Management System (FSMS)**, because the majority of biological, chemical, and physical hazards in animal-origin foods originate at the farm itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GAHP is the **first link in the food safety chain**. Failure at this stage cannot be fully rectified by downstream processing. Hence, GAHP is a **statutory prerequisite** under FSSAI's licensing and registration conditions for primary food producers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are baking a cake. The flour you use must be free from stones, insects, and musty smell. No matter how perfectly you mix, bake, or frost that cake, **if the flour was bad, the cake will be bad**. 

Similarly, animals are the "raw ingredients" of milk, meat, eggs, and honey. If the animal was sick, fed contaminated feed, given unsafe medicines, or kept in filthy conditions, then no amount of pasteurization, cooking, or chilling downstream will make the food perfectly safe. **GAHP is the discipline of keeping the "raw ingredient" clean from the very start.**

Think of GAHP as the **"health insurance policy of the food industry"** — small investments at the farm level prevent massive recalls, disease outbreaks, and legal liabilities later.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Benchmark Values

| Parameter | Benchmark Standard |
|---|---|
| **Stocking Density** | **1.4 m² per adult cow** (as per BIS / OIE welfare norms) |
| **Withdrawal Period** | **7–28 days** for veterinary drugs (drug-dependent) |
| **Somatic Cell Count (SCC)** in raw milk | **< 4,00,000 cells/mL** (FSSAI) |
| **Total Bacterial Count (TBC)** | **< 1,00,000 CFU/mL** (raw milk standard) |
| **Cold Chain Temperature** | **4 °C ± 2 °C** for raw milk storage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Snapshot:** *GAHP = Safe Feed + Healthy Animal + Hygienic Environment + Traceable Records.*

---

## 1.4 Scope & Coverage of GAHP

GAHP applies across all categories of food-producing animals:

- **Terrestrial livestock:** Cattle, buffalo, sheep, goat, pig
- **Poultry:** Broiler, layer, duck
- **Aquaculture:** Fish, shrimp, prawn
- **Apiculture:** Honey bees
- **Equines & others:** Used for milk (mare) or draft purposes

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Good Animal Husbandry Practices

GAHP is best understood as a structured framework built on **seven interlinked pillars**. Each pillar addresses a specific class of hazard.

### Pillar 1 — Animal Health Management
- Routine **veterinary supervision** and preventive health programmes.
- Implementation of **scheduled vaccination** (e.g., FMD, Brucellosis, HS, BQ, Rabies, Newcastle Disease, Marek's Disease).
- **Quarantine** of newly introduced animals for a minimum of **21 days** before mixing with the herd.
- **Disease surveillance** and prompt reporting of notifiable diseases to the **Department of Animal Husbandry**.
- Compliance with the **Prevention and Control of Infectious and Contagious Diseases in Animals Act, 2009**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A sick animal is a **biological hazard factory** — pathogens such as *Salmonella*, *E. coli* O157:H7, *Listeria*, *Campylobacter*, *Brucella*, and *Mycobacterium bovis* can directly enter the food chain.

### Pillar 2 — Feed and Water Safety
- Feed must be **free from mycotoxins** (Aflatoxin B1, Ochratoxin, Fumonisin), heavy metals, pesticide residues, and zoonotic pathogens.
- Compliance with **BIS standards** for compounded feed (IS 2052 for poultry feed, IS 15080 for cattle feed).
- **Potable water** must be provided; microbial limits should match **drinking water standards (IS 10500)**.
- Proper storage to prevent rodent, insect, and fungal contamination.
- **No feed containing meat, bone meal, or blood meal** to ruminants (to prevent BSE / "Mad Cow Disease" transmission).

### Pillar 3 — Veterinary Drugs & Pharmaceuticals
- Use of drugs **only on veterinary prescription**.
- Strict adherence to **withdrawal periods** — the time required for drug residues to deplete below **Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs)**.
- Documentation in a **drug usage register** (animal ID, drug, dose, date, route, withdrawal period).
- **Prohibition** of hormones, growth promoters, and antibiotics used as growth promoters — banned under FSSAI and the **FSS (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** Students often confuse *withdrawal period* with *expiry date*. **Withdrawal period** = time between last drug administration and slaughter/milk collection. **Expiry date** = shelf life of the drug.

### Pillar 4 — Housing, Hygiene & Sanitation
- Adequate **ventilation, lighting, drainage, and floor space**.
- Regular **disinfection** of sheds using approved agents (e.g., phenol-based, quaternary ammonium compounds, lime washing).
- **Pest and rodent control** programmes.
- **Manure and waste management** to prevent environmental contamination and zoonotic cycles.
- **Personal hygiene** of farm workers — gloves, boots, handwashing, no smoking/eating in animal areas.

### Pillar 5 — Animal Welfare (The 5 Freedoms)
Originated by the **Farm Animal Welfare Council (UK, 1965)** and adopted globally by **OIE/WOAH**:

1. **Freedom from hunger and thirst** — ready access to fresh water and a balanced diet.
2. **Freedom from discomfort** — appropriate shelter and resting area.
3. **Freedom from pain, injury, and disease** — prevention and rapid treatment.
4. **Freedom to express normal behaviour** — adequate space and facilities.
5. **Freedom from fear and distress** — conditions that avoid mental suffering.

In India, animal welfare is supported by the **Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960** and **PCA Rules, 2001** (slaughterhouse rules), which prescribe humane slaughter.

### Pillar 6 — Traceability & Record Keeping
- **Unique identification** of animals (ear tags, RFID, microchips, tattoos).
- Records of birth, vaccination, treatment, breeding, feed, and movement.
- Retention of records for a **minimum of 2–3 years** (or as prescribed).
- Enables **forward and backward traceability** in the event of a food safety incident.

### Pillar 7 — Environmental Sustainability
- Efficient use of **water, energy, and land**.
- Proper **disposal of carcasses** (incineration, deep burial, rendering — as per **BMW (Management and Handling) Rules**).
- **Manure-to-biogas/compost** conversion reduces GHG emissions.
- Compliance with **EPA-1986 (Environment Protection Act)** and state pollution control norms.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

| S.No | Concept | Definition / Key Value | Relevant Law / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **MRL (Maximum Residue Limit)** | Maximum legally permitted concentration of a veterinary drug residue in food | FSSAI Regulations, 2011 |
| 2 | **Withdrawal Period** | Time between last drug dose and food collection (slaughter/milk) | Drug-dependent; typically **7–28 days** |
| 3 | **Zoonosis** | Disease transmissible from animals to humans | OIE List of Zoonoses |
| 4 | **OIE/WOAH** | World Organisation for Animal Health | International standard setter |
| 5 | **Codex Alimentarius** | FAO/WHO international food standards | "Code of Hygienic Practice for Meat" CAC/RCP 58-2005 |
| 6 | **HACCP** | Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point | Codex, 1993 |
| 7 | **GAHP** | Good Animal Husbandry Practices | Codex, FAO |
| 8 | **5 Freedoms** | Animal welfare framework | FAWC, UK / OIE |
| 9 | **Quarantine period** | Isolation before herd mixing | **Minimum 21 days** |
| 10 | **Notifiable diseases** | Diseases legally reportable to government | **Prevention & Control of Infectious and Contagious Diseases in Animals Act, 2009** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** A certified GAHP-compliant farm qualifies for **higher export prices**, **easier market access** (EU, US, Japan), **reduced insurance premiums**, and is **legally defensible** in case of food safety litigations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Framework

## 3.1 Hazard-to-Control Mapping (Step-by-Step Logic)

GAHP works on a structured **Hazard Identification → Risk Assessment → Control Measure → Monitoring → Corrective Action** loop. The following exhaustive analysis walks through each hazard class.

### Step 1 — Biological Hazard Analysis

$$
\text{Biological Hazards} = \left\{ \text{Pathogens}, \text{Parasites}, \text{Prions}, \text{Zoonotic agents} \right\}
$$

- **Primary pathogens of concern:** *Salmonella spp., E. coli O157:H7, Campylobacter jejuni, Listeria monocytogenes, Brucella abortus, Mycobacterium bovis, Clostridium botulinum*.
- **GAHP control at source:**
  1. Vaccination (e.g., Brucella S19 vaccine for cattle).
  2. Test-and-slaughter policy (especially for TB and Brucellosis).
  3. Cleaning & disinfection of housing.
  4. Pasteurization of milk.
  5. Personal hygiene of workers.

### Step 2 — Chemical Hazard Analysis

$$
\text{Chemical Hazards} = \left\{ \text{Veterinary drug residues},\ \text{Pesticides},\ \text{Mycotoxins},\ \text{Heavy metals},\ \text{Cleaning chemicals} \right\}
$$

- **GAHP control at source:**
  1. Prescription-only drug use.
  2. Strict observance of **withdrawal period** = $t_w$.
  3. Use of BIS-compliant feed.
  4. Mycotoxin testing of feed (Aflatoxin B1 limit in cattle feed = **< 50 ppb**).
  5. Safe storage of agrochemicals away from animal feed.

### Step 3 — Physical Hazard Analysis

$$
\text{Physical Hazards} = \left\{ \text{Glass fragments},\ \text{Sharp metals},\ \text{Stones},\ \text{Plastic},\ \text{String} \right\}
$$

- **GAHP control at source:**
  1. Glass-and-metal policy on dairy and meat farms.
  2. Magnetic separators during feed milling.
  3. Inspection of bedding and pasture.

### Step 4 — Cross-Contamination Control

$$
\text{Cross-contamination} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Sanitation index} \times \text{Personal hygiene index}}
$$

- Segregate **clean and dirty zones**.
- Separate young, sick, and lactating animals.
- Color-coded equipment (red = slaughter area, green = clean area).

### Step 5 — Verification & Validation

$$
\text{Verification} = \left\{ \text{Microbiological testing},\ \text{Residue testing},\ \text{Audit},\ \text{Training records} \right\}
$$

- Periodic milk testing: **SCC, TPC, antibiotic residue**.
- Periodic meat testing: **pathogen screening**.
- Internal audit every **6 months**, third-party audit annually.

---

## 3.2 The Farm-to-Fork GAHP Continuum (Analytical Trace)

$$
\text{Farm} \xrightarrow{\text{GAHP}} \text{Transport} \xrightarrow{\text{GHP (Transport)}} \text{Slaughter/Processing} \xrightarrow{\text{GMP/HACCP}} \text{Retail} \xrightarrow{\text{Storage}} \text{Consumer}
$$

**Key Insight:** GAHP controls **input-side hazards** (what enters the animal), while GMP/HACCP controls **process-side hazards** (what happens during processing). Both ends must be sealed for total food safety.

---

## 3.3 GAHP Compliance Checklist (Ready-Reference)

| Checkpoint | Required Action | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| Vaccination schedule | Follow prescribed programme | As per schedule |
| Quarantine of new animals | **21 days minimum** | Every introduction |
| Feed mycotoxin test | Aflatoxin B1 < **50 ppb** | Every new batch |
| Water potability | E. coli < **0/100 mL** | Quarterly |
| Drug withdrawal record | Verified before slaughter | Every treatment |
| Shed disinfection | Lime + approved disinfectant | Weekly |
| Pest control | Traps + rodenticides (covered) | Monthly |
| Animal ID | Ear tag / RFID | At birth / on entry |
| Record retention | **Minimum 2 years** | Continuous |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When answering essay-type questions, always **link the GAHP principle to a specific Indian law or Codex standard**. This demonstrates applied knowledge, not rote memorization.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**Define Good Animal Husbandry Practices (GAHP). List any four key components of GAHP.**

**Model Answer:**
Good Animal Husbandry Practices (GAHP) is a set of science-based management principles applied at the farm level to ensure that animal-origin foods are safe, suitable, and wholesome for human consumption, while safeguarding animal welfare and environmental sustainability. The four key components are:

1. **Animal health management** — vaccination, disease surveillance, and quarantine.
2. **Feed and water safety** — mycotoxin-free feed and potable water.
3. **Veterinary drug control** — adherence to MRL and withdrawal period.
4. **Animal welfare** — upholding the Five Freedoms.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**What is the 'withdrawal period' for veterinary drugs? Why is it critical in GAHP?**

**Model Answer:**
The withdrawal period is the minimum time interval that must elapse between the **last administration of a veterinary drug** to a food-producing animal and the **collection of food (slaughter, milk, or eggs)** from that animal. It ensures that drug residues in the food fall below the legally permitted **Maximum Residue Limit (MRL)**.

It is critical in GAHP because violation can lead to **chemical contamination** of food with antibiotic residues, hormones, or pesticides, causing:
- Direct **health hazards** (antibiotic resistance, allergies, endocrine disruption) to consumers.
- **Trade rejection** of exported products.
- **Legal prosecution** under FSSAI regulations.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **seven pillars of Good Animal Husbandry Practices**. Highlight the link between each pillar and a relevant Indian law or international standard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **MRL (Maximum Residue Limit)** and **withdrawal period**. With an example, show how non-compliance with the withdrawal period leads to chemical contamination in milk. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

Good Animal Husbandry Practices rests on seven interconnected pillars:

1. **Animal Health Management** — Vaccination against FMD, Brucellosis, HS, BQ, and Newcastle Disease; quarantine of newly introduced animals for **21 days**; disease surveillance under the **Prevention and Control of Infectious and Contagious Diseases in Animals Act, 2009**.

2. **Feed and Water Safety** — Feed must conform to **BIS standards (IS 2052, IS 15080)** with Aflatoxin B1 below **50 ppb**; water must meet **IS 10500** drinking water specifications.

3. **Veterinary Drug Control** — Use only on prescription; comply with MRL and withdrawal period; prohibit growth-promoting antibiotics as mandated by **FSSAI Regulations**.

4. **Housing and Hygiene** — Adequate ventilation, drainage, stocking density, and disinfection; pest and rodent control; personal hygiene of workers to prevent zoonotic transmission.

5. **Animal Welfare (Five Freedoms)** — Originated by the **Farm Animal Welfare Council (UK)** and adopted by **OIE/WOAH**; enforced in India through the **Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960** and **PCA Rules, 2001**.

6. **Traceability and Record Keeping** — Unique animal identification (ear tag/RFID), movement and treatment records; retention for **minimum 2 years** for downstream traceability and recall management.

7. **Environmental Sustainability** — Manure management, carcass disposal as per **BMW Rules**, water and energy efficiency; aligned with the **Environment Protection Act, 1986**.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Maximum Residue Limit (MRL):** MRL is the legally permissible **maximum concentration** of a veterinary drug residue (or its metabolite) permitted in food (meat, milk, eggs) at the point of sale. MRLs are established by **FSSAI / Codex / EU / USFDA** based on toxicological studies and Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI).

**Withdrawal Period:** It is the mandatory interval between the **last dose of a drug** to an animal and the **collection of food** from that animal, ensuring residues fall below MRL.

**Numerical Example — Antibiotic (Oxytetracycline) Residue in Milk:**

Assume a lactating cow is treated with Oxytetracycline. The drug's plasma half-life is approximately **9 hours**, and residues are eliminated via milk. The label withdrawal period is **7 days**.

$$
t_w = 7 \text{ days}
$$

If milk is collected on **Day 4** (before completion of withdrawal), the residue concentration may be:

$$
C_{\text{day 4}} \approx 25\ \mu g/kg \quad (\text{hypothetical measured value})
$$

$$
\text{MRL}_{\text{Oxytetracycline in milk}} = 100\ \mu g/kg
$$

Since **25 < 100**, this individual reading is *technically* below MRL, but **continuous consumption** by the consumer across multiple such contaminated lots causes cumulative exposure, fostering **antimicrobial resistance (AMR)**.

If milk is collected on **Day 1** post-treatment:

$$
C_{\text{day 1}} \approx 400\ \mu g/kg \quad (\text{far above MRL})
$$

This is a clear **FSSAI violation**, punishable under **Section 26 / Section 59 of the FSS Act, 2006** with fines up to ₹10 lakh and possible imprisonment. Hence, strict observance of withdrawal period is a **non-negotiable GAHP control**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a)** What are the **Five Freedoms of Animal Welfare**? Explain how each freedom is operationalized on a modern dairy farm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **Codex Alimentarius** and **OIE/WOAH** in shaping Good Animal Husbandry Practices globally. Mention at least two specific Codex codes applicable to animal-origin food. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

The **Five Freedoms**, formulated by the **Farm Animal Welfare Council (UK, 1965)**, constitute the universal benchmark for animal welfare, adopted by **OIE/WOAH** and operationalized by the **PCA Act, 1960** in India:

| Freedom | On-Farm Operationalization |
|---|---|
| **1. Freedom from hunger and thirst** | Continuous access to **balanced ration** (green fodder, dry fodder, concentrate) and **clean, potable water**; automatic water troughs; scheduled feeding. |
| **2. Freedom from discomfort** | Adequate **floor space (≥ 1.4 m²/cow)**, ventilation, fans/misters in summer, dry bedding, non-slip flooring, shade. |
| **3. Freedom from pain, injury, and disease** | Preventive **vaccination**, prompt **veterinary care**, **hoof trimming**, mastitis control, hygienic milking to prevent injury. |
| **4. Freedom to express normal behaviour** | Sufficient space to **walk, lie down, socialize**; group housing for social animals; pasture access where feasible. |
| **5. Freedom from fear and distress** | Calm handling, **no electric prodders**, noise control, gentle weaning practices, no overcrowding, separation of aggressive animals. |

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO)** is the global standard-setting body for food safety. It develops **Codes of Hygienic Practice** and **commodity standards** that member countries adopt into national law. For animal-origin food, key Codex texts include:

- **CAC/RCP 58-2005** — *Code of Hygienic Practice for Meat*.
- **CAC/RCP 57-2003** — *Code of Hygienic Practice for Milk and Milk Products*.
- **CAC/RCP 1-1969** — *General Principles of Food Hygiene*.

**OIE / WOAH (World Organisation for Animal Health)** sets **Terrestrial and Aquatic Animal Health Codes**, focusing on:
- Disease surveillance and notification.
- **Zoonosis control** (tuberculosis, brucellosis, avian influenza).
- **Animal welfare standards** in farming and slaughter.
- **Antimicrobial resistance** stewardship in veterinary practice.

**Joint Impact on GAHP:**

$$
\text{GAHP} = \left( \text{Codex food safety principles} \right) \cap \left( \text{OIE animal health and welfare standards} \right)
$$

In India, FSSAI **benchmarks its regulations** against Codex, and the **Department of Animal Husbandry & Dairying** aligns with OIE. Thus, GAHP at the Indian farm is essentially the **operational implementation of these global norms**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Do **not** write "GAHP = GMP". They are **different** — GAHP operates at the **farm** (primary production); GMP operates at the **processing facility**.
> 2. Do **not** confuse **withdrawal period** (drug clearance) with **expiry date** (drug shelf life).
> 3. Always cite the **specific Act/Section** when mentioning a law — vague references ("according to FSSAI") lose marks.
> 4. Avoid generic statements like "maintain hygiene" — specify **how** (e.g., "lime wash sheds weekly", "tricolor-coded equipment").
> 5. For 14-mark questions, **always structure the answer into bullet points / tables** — the examiner awards marks for clarity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- GAHP = **first link** of food safety chain; cannot be compensated by downstream processing.
- The **Seven Pillars:** Animal Health, Feed & Water, Veterinary Drugs, Housing & Hygiene, Welfare, Traceability, Environment.
- **MRL** = maximum permitted drug residue in food; **Withdrawal period** = time between last dose and food collection.
- **5 Freedoms** are the foundation of animal welfare (FAWC/OIE).
- **Quarantine period for new animals = 21 days minimum.**
- **Aflatoxin B1 in cattle feed < 50 ppb** (BIS standard).
- **SCC in raw milk < 4,00,000 cells/mL** (FSSAI standard).
- **Banned practices:** No hormones, no growth-promoting antibiotics, no meat/bone meal to ruminants (BSE prevention).
- **Key Indian laws:** FSS Act 2006, PCA Act 1960, Prevention & Control of Infectious Diseases in Animals Act 2009, EPA 1986.
- **Key global bodies:** Codex Alimentarius (FAO/WHO), OIE/WOAH.
- **Record retention ≥ 2 years** for traceability.
- **Carcass disposal:** Incineration / deep burial / rendering (per BMW Rules).
- **Export markets (EU, US, Japan)** require documented GAHP compliance.
- **Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR)** is the major global driver of strict drug withdrawal compliance.
- **Tag every answer with a law name + section** to score full marks in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISO 9000
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT723 - FOOD LAWS AND LEGISLATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** ISO 9000

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO 9000** is a series of internationally recognized **Quality Management System (QMS)** standards published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)**, headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland. The series establishes the foundational vocabulary, principles, and requirements that organizations across all industries — including the food processing sector — must follow to demonstrate the ability to consistently provide products and services that meet customer and regulatory requirements.

The ISO 9000 family (as revised under **ISO 9000:2015** and the supporting standard **ISO 9001:2015**) is structured into three core documents:

| Standard | Title | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 9000:2015** | Quality management systems — Fundamentals and vocabulary | Defines the **terminology, concepts, and principles** underpinning QMS. |
| **ISO 9001:2015** | Quality management systems — Requirements | Specifies the **mandatory requirements** for certification of a QMS. |
| **ISO 9004:2018** | Quality management — Quality of an organization | Provides **guidance** on sustained success and performance improvement (not for certification). |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** When examiners ask "What is ISO 9000?", they expect the answer to mention the *family of standards*, the *issuing body (ISO)*, and the *focus on Quality Management Systems*. The year 2015 revision is the **current benchmark** and is what KTU 2024 scheme students must study.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Card for a Restaurant Chain"

Imagine you own a small South Indian restaurant in Kerala that becomes wildly popular. You decide to **franchise** it across India. The challenge is: *how do you guarantee that the masala dosa in Chennai tastes exactly like the one in Thrissur?*

You cannot be present in every outlet. So you write a **Master Recipe Card** — it specifies:
- The exact grade of rice and urad dal
- The fermentation time (**48 hours**)
- The oil temperature (**180 °C ± 5 °C**)
- The plate presentation (3 chutneys, 1 sambar)
- A feedback mechanism for customer complaints
- A periodic audit of every franchise

**ISO 9000 is exactly this Master Recipe Card — but for an entire business process.** It tells every branch of a company — and every supplier — *"these are the documented, verifiable, and repeatable standards you must follow to deliver consistent quality."* In the food industry, this becomes the bedrock upon which food safety standards like **ISO 22000** and **HACCP** are layered.

### 1.3 Origin and Evolution (Historical Context for KTU Answers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ISO 9000 series was **first published in 1987**, drawing heavily from the **British Standard BS 5750**. It has undergone major revisions in **1994, 2000, 2008, and 2015**. The **2015 revision** shifted the framework from a *procedural* model to a *risk-based* and *process* model — a frequently asked KTU question.

**Key milestones:**
- **1987:** Initial publication (driven by manufacturing and defense sectors).
- **2000:** Shift to process approach and the **PDCA cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act).
- **2015:** Introduction of **risk-based thinking**, **context of the organization**, and the **seven quality management principles**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Matrix

### 2.1 The Seven Quality Management Principles (QMS Principles of ISO 9000:2015)

The 2015 revision codifies **seven principles** that are examinable at both 3-mark and 14-mark levels:

1. **Customer Focus** — The primary focus is to meet customer requirements and exceed expectations. *In food industry:* ensuring the snack is safe, tasty, and labelled correctly.
2. **Leadership** — Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction. *In food industry:* the plant manager must champion food safety culture.
3. **Engagement of People** — Competent, empowered, and engaged people at all levels enhance the organization's capability. *In food industry:* trained floor workers who follow GMP.
4. **Process Approach** — Consistent and predictable results are achieved more effectively when activities are understood and managed as interrelated processes. *This is the **heart of ISO 9001:2015**.*
5. **Improvement** — Successful organizations have an ongoing focus on improvement. *In food industry:* reducing recall incidents year-on-year.
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** — Decisions based on the analysis and evaluation of data are more likely to produce desired results.
7. **Relationship Management** — For sustained success, an organization manages its relationships with interested parties (suppliers, regulators, retailers).

### 2.2 The Process Approach — PDCA Cycle

The PDCA (Deming Cycle / Plan-Do-Check-Act) is the **operational backbone** of any ISO 9001 implementation:

| Stage | Action | Food Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Establish objectives and processes | Plan a new low-sugar jam production line; define critical control limits. |
| **Do** | Implement the planned processes | Train staff, calibrate the Brix refractometer, run the batch. |
| **Check** | Monitor and measure performance | Test pH (target **3.8–4.2**), record microbial counts. |
| **Act** | Take corrective actions to improve | Adjust sugar concentration, retrain operator, revise SOP. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Formula / Concept:**
> $$\text{QMS Effectiveness} = f(\text{Process Identification} \times \text{Process Interaction} \times \text{PDCA Discipline})$$
> In words: a QMS works only when **processes are identified, their interactions are mapped, and PDCA is rigorously applied** at every level.

### 2.3 Clause Structure of ISO 9001:2015 (Frequently Asked in KTU)

ISO 9001:2015 follows the **10-clause High Level Structure (HLS)** shared by all ISO management system standards. This is **critical** for answering 14-mark questions:

| Clause | Title | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| 1–3 | Scope, Normative refs, Definitions | Contextual framing |
| 4 | **Context of the Organization** | Internal/external issues, scope of QMS |
| 5 | **Leadership** | Top management commitment, quality policy |
| 6 | **Planning** | Risks, opportunities, quality objectives |
| 7 | **Support** | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information |
| 8 | **Operation** | Operational planning and control |
| 9 | **Performance Evaluation** | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit, management review |
| 10 | **Improvement** | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on documentation:** The 2015 revision replaced the term *"documents and records"* with the broader concept of **"documented information"** (Clauses 7.5). This terminology change is itself a popular KTU question.

### 2.4 Relevance to the Food Industry

| Aspect | How ISO 9000 Applies |
|---|---|
| Supplier Control | Clause 8.4 ensures incoming raw materials meet specifications. |
| Traceability | Required under Clause 8.5.2 — critical for food recall management. |
| Customer Satisfaction | Clause 9.1.2 mandates monitoring *perceived* customer satisfaction. |
| HACCP Integration | ISO 9001 provides the management framework; HACCP/ISO 22000 provides food safety specifics. |
| Regulatory Compliance | Clause 8.2.2 mandates identification of applicable food laws (FSSAI, Codex, EU regulations). |

### 2.5 The ISO 9000 Family — Supplementary Standards

Beyond the core three, the ISO 9000 family includes:
- **ISO 19011:2018** — Guidelines for auditing management systems.
- **ISO/TS 9002:2016** — Guidance on the application of ISO 9001:2015.
- **ISO 9000-3** (legacy) — Guidelines for software.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Analytical Matrix: ISO 9000 Implementation Logic & Analytical Reasoning

### 3.1 Logical Decomposition of the "Risk-Based Thinking" Mandate

**Step 1 — Define the organizational context (Clause 4):**
The organization must determine **internal issues** (e.g., outdated refrigeration units) and **external issues** (e.g., new FSSAI labelling rules).

**Step 2 — Identify risks and opportunities (Clause 6.1):**
For each process, the organization asks:
- *What can go wrong?* (risk)
- *What advantages exist?* (opportunity)

**Step 3 — Plan actions (Clause 6.2):**
Translate risks into **quality objectives** with measurable targets.

**Step 4 — Integrate actions into QMS processes (Clause 6.3):**
Embed the actions into day-to-day operations — not in a separate "risk binder" no one reads.

**Mathematical representation of risk prioritization (KTU analytical concept):**

$$
\text{Risk Priority Number (RPN)} = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- $S$ = **Severity** (impact if the risk materializes), usually on a scale of 1–10.
- $O$ = **Occurrence** (likelihood), on a scale of 1–10.
- $D$ = **Detection** (difficulty of detecting the risk before customer impact), on a scale of 1–10.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **higher RPN** means the risk demands more immediate QMS intervention. Food-industry example: *Salmonella contamination* in a poultry plant has $S = 10$, $O = 4$, $D = 3$, giving $\text{RPN} = 120$. In contrast, *minor packaging misalignment* might have $S = 2$, $O = 5$, $D = 8$, giving $\text{RPN} = 80$. Both are above the action threshold (typically **100**), but the *Salmonella* risk must be controlled with higher urgency.

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — A Food Processing Plant

> A Kerala-based spice export company, **"GreenLeaf Spices Pvt. Ltd."**, wants ISO 9001:2015 certification. Below is the analytical mapping KTU examiners love.

**Step A — Context Analysis (Clause 4):**
- *Internal:* Workforce skill gaps in moisture-content testing.
- *External:* New EU maximum residue limits (MRLs) for pesticides in cardamom.

**Step B — Interested Parties (Clause 4.2):**
- Customers (EU importers), regulators (FSSAI, Spices Board India), employees, suppliers.

**Step C — Risk Register (Clause 6.1):**
$$
\text{Risk 1: Pesticide Residue Exceedance} \Rightarrow \text{RPN} = 9 \times 5 \times 4 = 180
$$

$$
\text{Risk 2: Moisture-induced Aflatoxin} \Rightarrow \text{RPN} = 10 \times 6 \times 5 = 300
$$

> The aflatoxin risk (RPN = **300**) is the *highest* and must drive corrective resource allocation.

**Step D — Quality Objectives (Clause 6.2):**
- Reduce aflatoxin exceedances to **< 1 per 1000 batches** within 12 months.
- Achieve **100% staff certification** in ISO 22000/HACCP awareness.

**Step E — Operational Controls (Clause 8):**
- Implement a **validated drying protocol** with critical limits: moisture content **$\leq 10\%$** (w.b.).
- Deploy **HACCP-based CCP monitoring** at the drying and storage stages.

**Step F — Performance Evaluation (Clause 9):**
- Internal audits every **6 months**.
- Customer satisfaction surveys sent to **all EU clients** after each shipment.

**Step G — Continual Improvement (Clause 10):**
- If audit findings exceed **3 non-conformities per cycle**, trigger management review for **root cause analysis** (e.g., 5-Why or Fishbone / Ishikawa diagram).

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix — ISO 9000 vs ISO 22000 vs HACCP

| Feature | **ISO 9000 / 9001** | **ISO 22000** | **HACCP** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scope | Generic QMS for any industry | Food Safety Management System (FSMS) | Food safety hazard control |
| Issued by | ISO | ISO | Codex Alimentarius Commission |
| Origin | 1987 (rev. 2015) | 2005 (rev. 2018) | 1960s (Pillsbury/NASA), formalized by Codex 1993 |
| Focus | Customer satisfaction + process consistency | Food safety across the chain | Specific CCP-based hazard control |
| Certification | Yes | Yes | Not directly; through third-party audits |
| Relationship | Foundation / umbrella | Built on ISO 9001 structure | Embedded within ISO 22000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept Ladder:** ISO 9000 is the **quality foundation** → ISO 22000 builds **food safety** on top → HACCP provides the **hazard control engine** inside ISO 22000. This hierarchy is frequently asked in 14-mark questions.

### 3.4 Certification Process — Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Gap Analysis** — Compare current QMS against ISO 9001:2015 clauses.
2. **Documentation** — Develop Quality Manual, procedures, SOPs, and records.
3. **Implementation** — Train staff, deploy processes, run for at least **3 months**.
4. **Internal Audit** — Conducted by trained internal auditors against **ISO 19011:2018**.
5. **Management Review** — Top management reviews audit findings and adequacy.
6. **Stage 1 Audit (Documentation)** — Certification body verifies documentation.
7. **Stage 2 Audit (On-site)** — Auditor verifies actual implementation.
8. **Certification Decision** — Issued by accredited body; valid for **3 years**, with surveillance audits every **12 months**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```

```
