# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define ISO 14001. State any two key benefits of ISO 14001 certification to a food processing industry.**

**Model Answer:**

ISO 14001 is an internationally recognized standard published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) that specifies the requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an Environmental Management System (EMS) within an organization. It is the only certifiable standard in the ISO 14000 family, currently in its 2015 revision (ISO 14001:2015), and follows the Annex SL High-Level Structure.

**Two key benefits to a food processing industry:**

1. **Regulatory Compliance and Risk Mitigation:** Systematic identification and management of environmental aspects (effluent, emissions, packaging waste) ensures compliance with laws such as the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act 1974, Air Act 1981, and Hazardous Waste Management Rules — avoiding penalties, prosecution, and shutdown risks.

2. **Market Access and Brand Image:** ISO 14001 certification is increasingly demanded by global buyers (e.g., Walmart, Tesco, Nestlé suppliers) as a pre-condition for vendor approval. It enhances corporate reputation, supports ESG reporting, and provides competitive advantage in export markets.

*(Examiner Note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for benefits — allocate 1 mark per distinct benefit.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Differentiate between ISO 14000 and ISO 22000.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | ISO 14000 | ISO 22000 |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Environmental management and sustainability. | Food safety management across the chain. |
| **Certifiable Standard** | ISO 14001 (Environmental Management System). | ISO 22000 (Food Safety Management System). |
| **Objective** | Reduce environmental aspects, impacts, and ensure legal compliance. | Identify and control food safety hazards (biological, chemical, physical). |
| **Mandatory Hazard Tool** | None — uses risk-based thinking. | Requires Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP). |
| **Key Drivers** | Stakeholder pressure, regulatory compliance, ESG goals. | Consumer safety, regulatory mandates (FSSAI, Codex). |
| **Origin** | Published 1996 (revised 2004, 2015). | Published 2005 (revised 2018). |

*(Examiner Note: Any 4 valid differentiation points yield full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle** as applied to ISO 14001:2015 Environmental Management System. Describe each phase with suitable food industry examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **benefits and limitations** of implementing ISO 14001 in a food manufacturing unit. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### (a) PDCA Cycle in ISO 14001:2015 [7 Marks]

The PDCA cycle is the foundational methodology that gives ISO 14001:2015 its dynamic, improvement-oriented character.

**1. PLAN (Clauses 4, 5, 6) [2 Marks]**
- Establish context, identify interested parties, determine scope.
- Define environmental policy and leadership commitment.
- Identify environmental aspects and impacts, determine compliance obligations, set SMART objectives.

*Food Industry Example:* A packaged juice manufacturer identifies that orange peel waste (5 tonnes/day) is a significant environmental aspect. The planning phase establishes an objective: convert peel waste to animal feed or compost within 12 months.

**2. DO (Clauses 7, 8) [2 Marks]**
- Provide resources, competence, training, awareness, and communication.
- Implement operational controls, document procedures, and ensure emergency preparedness.

*Food Industry Example:* Installing a biogas digester, training operators on effluent pH monitoring, documenting SOPs for refrigeration gas leak checks, conducting mock drill for ammonia leak emergency.

**3. CHECK (Clause 9) [1.5 Marks]**
- Monitor, measure, and analyse environmental performance.
- Conduct internal audits and management reviews.
- Evaluate compliance with legal obligations.

*Food Industry Example:* Monthly BOD/COD testing of effluent against CPCB norms, energy audits, internal audit of the boiler emission control area, management review of monthly KPIs.

**4. ACT (Clause 10) [1.5 Marks]**
- Take corrective action on non-conformities.
- Drive continual improvement of the EMS.

*Food Industry Example:* When internal audit reveals boiler fuel consumption is 8% above target, root-cause analysis identifies faulty burners; corrective action includes burner recalibration and preventive replacement schedule, achieving 6% reduction in 3 months.

---

### (b) Benefits and Limitations of ISO 14001 in a Food Manufacturing Unit [7 Marks]

**Benefits [4 Marks]:**

1. **Regulatory Compliance** – Structured framework to track and comply with environmental laws, reducing the risk of fines and prosecution.
2. **Cost Reduction** – Efficient use of water, energy, and raw materials reduces operating costs (e.g., 15–25% energy savings reported in many certified plants).
3. **Market Advantage** – Preferred supplier status with international buyers, eligibility for "green" public procurement tenders.
4. **Improved Stakeholder Relations** – Better relationships with regulators, local communities, NGOs, and consumers.
5. **Risk Management** – Proactive identification of environmental risks (e.g., groundwater contamination, refrigerant leak) prevents crises.
6. **Employee Engagement** – Defined roles, training, and participation boost morale and accountability.

**Limitations [3 Marks]:**

1. **High Initial Cost** – Certification involves consultancy, audits, training, and infrastructure investment — challenging for MSMEs.
2. **Documentation Burden** – Heavy paperwork and record-keeping can strain small organizations.
3. **Does Not Guarantee Performance** – Certification confirms the *system* exists, not absolute environmental outcomes; "greenwashing" risk remains.
4. **Sector-Agnostic** – Generic standard; food-industry-specific risks (e.g., pesticide residues in effluents) may require additional sectoral frameworks.
5. **Continual Resource Demand** – Surveillance audits, internal audits, and management reviews require sustained staff time.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Describe the **structure of ISO 14000 series of standards**. Explain briefly any five sub-standards with their focus area. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **steps involved in implementing ISO 14001 EMS** in a food processing organization. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### (a) Structure of ISO 14000 Series [7 Marks]

The ISO 14000 series is a **family of voluntary, consensus-based international standards** for environmental management, coordinated by **ISO/TC 207**. It is broadly divided into five clusters:

**1. Environmental Management System (EMS) Standards [1.5 Marks]**
- **ISO 14001:2015** – Specifies the requirements for an EMS. The only certifiable standard.
- **ISO 14004** – Provides general guidelines on EMS implementation.
- **ISO 14005** – Guidelines for phased implementation of an EMS.

**2. Environmental Auditing Standards [1.5 Marks]**
- **ISO 14015** – Environmental assessment of sites and organizations.
- **ISO 19011** – Guidelines for auditing management systems (jointly used with ISO 9001).

**3. Environmental Labelling Standards [1.5 Marks]**
- **ISO 14020** – General principles of environmental labelling.
- **ISO 14021** – Self-declared environmental claims (Type II eco-labels).
- **ISO 14024** – Multi-criteria third-party labels (Type I, e.g., EU Ecolabel, India GreenPro).
- **ISO 14025** – Environmental product declarations, Type III (quantified lifecycle information).

**4. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) Standards [1.5 Marks]**
- **ISO 14040** – Principles and framework for LCA.
- **ISO 14044** – Requirements and guidelines for conducting LCA.

**5. Other Environmental Management Standards [1 Mark]**
- **ISO 14031** – Environmental performance evaluation.
- **ISO 14064/65/66** – Greenhouse gas (GHG) quantification, verification, and accreditation.
- **ISO 14067** – Carbon footprint of products.

---

### (b) Steps in Implementing ISO 14001 EMS [7 Marks]

The implementation of ISO 14001 EMS follows a structured 10-step roadmap:

**Step 1: Top Management Commitment [0.5 Mark]**
The leadership declares intent, allocates budget, and appoints an **EMS Leader/Representative**.

**Step 2: Initial Environmental Review (IER) [0.5 Mark]**
A baseline study of current environmental performance, legal compliance status, and existing practices.

**Step 3: Gap Analysis [0.5 Mark]**
Compare current practices with each clause of ISO 14001 to identify gaps.

**Step 4: Define Scope of EMS [0.5 Mark]**
Specify whether the EMS covers a single plant, a division, or the entire company.

**Step 5: Identify Environmental Aspects and Impacts [1 Mark]**
Enumerate all activities, products, services; assess significance using scoring matrices.

**Step 6: Determine Compliance Obligations [1 Mark]**
Catalogue all applicable legal and regulatory requirements (Indian: Water Act, Air Act, EPA 1986, E-Waste Rules, Plastic Waste Management Rules).

**Step 7: Develop Environmental Policy [0.5 Mark]**
A policy drafted and signed by top management committing to prevention of pollution, legal compliance, and continual improvement.

**Step 8: Set Objectives and Targets [1 Mark]**
SMART goals such as "reduce specific water consumption by 15% by FY 2026."

**Step 9: Establish Documentation and Operational Controls [0.5 Mark]**
Develop procedures, SOPs, records, and engineering controls.

**Step 10: Training, Communication, and Documentation [0.5 Mark]**
Train all employees, communicate externally to interested parties, and maintain documented information as required by Clauses 7 and 8.

After these steps, **internal audits** are conducted, followed by a **management review** and finally **external certification audit** by an accredited third-party body.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ISO 14000 with ISO 14001:** ISO 14000 is the *family*; ISO 14001 is the *certifiable standard*. Examiners deduct marks if students interchange them.
>
> 2. **Forgetting that ISO 14001 is voluntary:** It is **not legally mandated**; organizations adopt it for compliance, market, and reputational benefits.
>
> 3. **Skipping examples:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for food-industry examples. Always cite a dairy/bakery/beverage scenario.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Indian Legal Context:** When discussing compliance obligations, name Indian statutes (Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, EPA 1986) — Indian examiners prefer Indian references over only ISO/EU citations.
>
> 5. **Missing PDCA Mapping:** Many students describe PDCA without mapping it to ISO 14001 clauses. Always explicitly link each PDCA phase to its clause number.
>
> 6. **Treating Continual Improvement as Optional:** Clause 10 mandates continual improvement as a core requirement, not an aspiration.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember for KTU Exam — At a Glance:**

- **ISO 14000** = Family of international standards on environmental management.
- **ISO 14001** = The only certifiable standard in the family.
- **Publisher:** International Organization for Standardization (ISO), Geneva.
- **First Published:** 1996; **Current Version:** ISO 14001:2015.
- **Structure:** Based on **Annex SL High-Level Structure (HLS)** with **10 clauses**.
- **Methodology:** **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act).
- **Voluntary:** Not legally mandatory but driven by stakeholder pressure and market demand.
- **Applicability:** Universal — any organization of any size or sector.
- **Key Concepts:** Environmental Aspect, Environmental Impact, Compliance Obligations, Lifecycle Perspective, Risk-Based Thinking, Continual Improvement.
- **Sub-Standards Families:** EMS (14001), Auditing (14015, 19011), Labelling (14020–14025), LCA (14040–14044), GHG (14064–14067).
- **Certification Cycle:** Stage 1 → Stage 2 → Certification → Surveillance (Year 1, 2) → Re-certification (Year 3).
- **Indian Statutes Linked:** Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, Environment Protection Act 1986, Hazardous Waste Rules 2016, E-Waste Rules, Plastic Waste Management Rules 2016.
- **Food Industry Relevance:** Effluent management (BOD/COD), packaging waste, energy efficiency, refrigerant emissions, water stewardship.
- **Distinguish Carefully:** ISO 9000 (Quality) ≠ ISO 14000 (Environment) ≠ ISO 22000 (Food Safety).
- **Benefits:** Legal compliance, cost reduction, market access, ESG/sustainability reporting, risk management, employee engagement.
- **Limitations:** High initial cost, documentation burden, doesn't guarantee absolute performance, generic across sectors.
- **PDCA Mapping to ISO 14001:** Plan → Clauses 4,5,6 | Do → Clauses 7,8 | Check → Clause 9 | Act → Clause 10.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISO 17025
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT723 - FOOD LAWS AND LEGISLATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ISO 17025

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO/IEC 17025:2017 — General requirements for the competence of testing and calibration laboratories** is the international reference standard issued jointly by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** and the **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)**. It prescribes the general requirements that a laboratory must demonstrate in order to prove that it operates competently, impartially, and is capable of generating technically valid results.

In the Indian regulatory context, this standard is operationalized through the **National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories (NABL)**, which is the sole accreditation body authorized by the Government of India to assess laboratory conformance to ISO/IEC 17025.

### Key Terminology Decoded

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Accreditation** | Formal third-party recognition that a lab is competent to perform specific tests/calibrations. |
| **Impartiality** | Actual and perceived objectivity of the laboratory in its operations. |
| **Risk-based thinking** | Identifying and addressing risks that may affect the validity of laboratory results. |
| **Decision rule** | A documented rule that describes how measurement uncertainty is accounted for when declaring conformity. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Passport" of a Food Testing Lab**

Imagine you want to travel abroad. A **passport** issued by your government certifies that you are who you claim to be, and that you meet international standards. Without it, no country will trust your entry documents.

A **food testing laboratory** faces the same problem. When a spice exporter in Kerala sends a sample to a lab to certify that **aflatoxin** levels are below **20 µg/kg** for the EU market, the buyer in Europe does not personally know the Indian lab. The European buyer needs a **universal credential** that says, *"This lab is competent, its equipment is calibrated, its people are trained, and its results can be trusted worldwide."*

That universal credential is **ISO/IEC 17025 accreditation**. Just like a passport, it is issued by a recognized authority (NABL in India, ILAC/APLAC internationally), it is portable across borders through the **ILAC MRA (Mutual Recognition Arrangement)**, and it expires if the lab does not maintain its standards.

**Three everyday scenarios where ISO 17025 matters:**
1. **FSSAI notification compliance** — Food labs testing under the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 must be NABL-accredited (ISO 17025).
2. **Export certification** — A peanut exporter needs a lab whose test report is accepted by the **European Commission** without re-testing.
3. **Legal disputes** — A court will only accept analytical evidence from an accredited lab, because the chain of competence is provable.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU Examiners

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Relevance:**
> - This topic directly tests the **Course Outcome (CO4)** — *"Comprehend the role of international quality and accreditation frameworks in ensuring food safety."*
> - It is a **favourite 7-mark and 14-mark question** in the End Semester Examinations because it bridges **law (FSSAI notification), technology (testing methods), and trade (export compliance)**.
> - ISO 17025 sits at the intersection of **FSSAI, BIS, Codex Alimentarius, and WTO-SPS measures** — the four pillars of Indian food regulation.

---

## 1.4 Historical Evolution — Exam-Favourite Timeline

| Year | Milestone | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1999** | First edition of ISO/IEC 17025 issued | Established the original framework. |
| **2005** | ISO/IEC 17025:2005 published | Aligned with ISO 9001; added emphasis on management system. |
| **2017** | ISO/IEC 17025:2017 (current version) | Restructured into risk-based thinking; removed explicit link to ISO 9001; expanded on impartiality and confidentiality. |
| **India** | NABL operates accreditation under this standard | **National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories**. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Pearl:** The 2017 revision is **NOT a rewrite** — it is a **restructuring**. Examiners often ask: *"What are the major changes introduced in the 2017 revision?"*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Structure

## 2.1 Architectural Framework of ISO/IEC 17025:2017

The standard is organized into **5 mandatory clauses + 3 informative annexes**. The 5 clauses are divided into two major pillars:

### **Pillar I — Management Requirements (Clauses 4 & 5)**

> [!NOTE]
> These requirements address the **organizational, legal, and procedural** integrity of the laboratory. They are identical in spirit to a quality management system (QMS), but with one critical difference: **they also require technical competence, not just paperwork.**

| Clause | Requirement | Plain English Purpose |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **4.1** | Impartiality | The lab must be objective; no commercial/financial pressure may bias results. |
| **4.2** | Confidentiality | Lab must protect client information and proprietary data. |
| **4.3** | Structural requirements | Legal identity, defined responsibility, and a single "top management" accountable for compliance. |
| **5.1 to 5.10** | Resource, process, and management system requirements | Cover everything from personnel (5.2) to records (5.13). |

### **Pillar II — Technical Requirements (Clauses 6 to 8)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **heart of ISO 17025**. They are what distinguish a competent testing lab from a generic service provider. A lab can have the best quality manual in the world but fail accreditation if these technical requirements are not met.

| Clause | Requirement | Real-World Engineering Example |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **6.1 to 6.6** | Personnel, facilities, equipment, metrological traceability, externally provided products & services | **HPLC machine** must be calibrated with a **traceable reference standard**; analyst must be **trained on the specific method** (e.g., AOAC 991.31 for fat content). |
| **7.1 to 7.10** | Process requirements — review of requests, methods, sampling, handling, technical records, measurement uncertainty, validation, reporting, complaints, non-conforming work, control of data | Covers the **entire life cycle** of a sample, from receipt to final report. |
| **8.1 to 8.9** | Management system — options A and B, risk management, internal audit, management review, corrective actions | The **continuous improvement engine** of the lab. |

---

## 2.2 The Risk-Based Thinking Revolution (2017 Edition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single biggest change in the 2017 revision is the introduction of **risk-based thinking (Clause 8.5)**. The standard no longer asks the lab to "prevent all non-conformities" (an impossible mandate); it asks the lab to **identify risks and opportunities** and act on them.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Risk-Based Thinking

1. **Identify the risk** — e.g., power failure during an HPLC run that may invalidate the result.
2. **Assess the impact** — Will the result be unreliable? Will the client be harmed?
3. **Implement a control** — Install a **Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS)** and a backup chiller.
4. **Monitor and review** — Periodically test the UPS and document it.
5. **Take corrective action** — If a power failure still corrupts a run, issue a revised report (Clause 7.10).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Key Definitions to Memorize

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **top 12 definitions** that have appeared in KTU 2024 Scheme model papers and previous B.Tech examinations. Memorize them verbatim.

| # | Term | Definition (Verbatim) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Laboratory** | An entity that performs testing, calibration, or sampling. |
| 2 | **Accreditation** | Third-party attestation that a laboratory is competent. |
| 3 | **Impartiality** | Presence of objectivity — absence of conflicts of interest. |
| 4 | **Verification** | Provision of objective evidence that a given item fulfils specified requirements. |
| 5 | **Validation** | Confirmation that the method is fit for the intended use. |
| 6 | **Measurement uncertainty (MU)** | Parameter characterizing the dispersion of values attributable to a measurand. |
| 7 | **Metrological traceability** | Property of a measurement result whereby it can be related to a reference through an unbroken chain of calibrations. |
| 8 | **Decision rule** | Rule that describes how measurement uncertainty is accounted for when stating conformity with a specified requirement. |
| 9 | **Proficiency testing (PT)** | Evaluation of participant performance against pre-established criteria by means of inter-laboratory comparisons. |
| 10 | **Reference standard** | A standard, generally of the highest metrological quality, used to realize the unit. |
| 11 | **Non-conforming work** | Any activity or result that does not conform to the laboratory's own procedures or to the standard. |
| 12 | **Management system** | Set of interrelated or interacting elements to establish policies and objectives. |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters beyond exams:**

1. **International Trade:** An ISO 17025-accredited test report is accepted in **120+ economies** through the ILAC MRA. This is critical for India's **$50+ billion food export industry**.
2. **Legal Admissibility:** Indian courts (under the **Indian Evidence Act, 1872, Section 293**) accept reports from accredited labs as evidence.
3. **FSSAI Compliance:** The **Food Safety and Standards (Recognition and Notification of Laboratories) Regulations, 2018** mandate NABL accreditation.
4. **Public Health:** A negative test for *Salmonella* from an accredited lab prevents foodborne outbreaks — every food recall in India is validated by an ISO 17025 lab.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Application Matrix

## 3.1 The Laboratory Workflow under ISO 17025:2017

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **operational backbone** of the standard. A 14-mark question can be asked on the **"stepwise process from sample receipt to report issuance under ISO 17025."** The following exhaustive framework is your model answer.

### Step 1 — Review of Request, Tender, and Contract (Clause 7.1)

The lab must:
- **Establish whether it has the competence and resources** to perform the test.
- **Select the appropriate method** (e.g., BIS, AOAC, ISO, or in-house validated method).
- **Resolve any difference of understanding** between the lab and the client in writing.
- **Document the review** — the **Review Record** is an auditable document.

> **Example:** A spice exporter requests aflatoxin testing. The lab must verify that it has an **HPLC with fluorescence detector**, a **validated AOAC method (e.g., 991.31)**, and a **trained analyst** before accepting the contract.

### Step 2 — Selection, Verification, and Validation of Methods (Clause 7.2)

| Method Type | Action Required |
| :--- | :--- |
| **International/regional standard method (e.g., ISO, BIS)** | **Verify** that the lab can perform it competently (precision, accuracy, detection limits). |
| **Standard method modified by the lab** | **Validate** the modification and provide evidence of fitness-for-purpose. |
| **In-house / non-standard method** | **Full validation** including specificity, linearity, accuracy, precision, LOD, LOQ, and robustness. |
| **Standard method used outside its intended scope** | **Validate** the extension of scope. |

### Step 3 — Sampling (Clause 7.3)

> [!NOTE]
> Sampling is **not always done by the lab** (e.g., when a FSSAI inspector draws a market sample). But when it is, the lab must have a **sampling plan and procedure** based on statistical principles (e.g., **ISO 2859-1** for attribute sampling).

### Step 4 — Handling of Test Items (Clause 7.4)

Critical controls:
- **Unique identification** of each item.
- **Chain of custody** documentation.
- **Storage conditions** (e.g., refrigerated at **4°C ± 2°C**) — recorded continuously.
- **Protection from deterioration** — food samples are particularly prone to microbial or chemical changes.
- **Disposal** — after retention period, samples must be disposed of safely.

### Step 5 — Technical Records (Clause 7.5)

Every observation, calculation, and derived data must be recorded **at the time it is made**. The "**raw data + calculation + final result + uncertainty**" trail must be **traceable**.

### Step 6 — Evaluation of Measurement Uncertainty (MU) (Clause 7.6)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-weightage topic** for KTU 14-mark questions.

The laboratory must **identify the contributions to uncertainty** and evaluate the **combined standard uncertainty ($u_c$)** and the **expanded uncertainty ($U$)**.

#### 3.6.1 The Standard Formula (GUM Approach)

The **expanded uncertainty** at a confidence level of approximately **95%** is:

$$
U = k \cdot u_c
$$

Where:
- $U$ = expanded uncertainty
- $k$ = coverage factor (typically $k = 2$ for 95% confidence)
- $u_c$ = combined standard uncertainty

#### 3.6.2 Combined Standard Uncertainty (Independent Inputs)

When input quantities $X_i$ are independent:

$$
u_c(y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \right)^{2} u^{2}(x_i)}
$$

Where:
- $u_c(y)$ = combined standard uncertainty of the measurand $y$
- $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}$ = sensitivity coefficient (partial derivative)
- $u(x_i)$ = standard uncertainty of input $X_i$

#### 3.6.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Aflatoxin in Groundnut

**Scenario:** A lab measures aflatoxin B1 in groundnut using HPLC. The calibration curve gives a concentration of **15.2 µg/kg**. The standard uncertainty from the calibration ($u_{cal}$) is **0.8 µg/kg**, and the standard uncertainty from sample weighing ($u_{w}$) is **0.3 µg/kg**.

**Step 1:** Identify uncertainty sources.
- $u_{cal} = 0.8\ \mu g/kg$ (from calibration curve)
- $u_{w} = 0.3\ \mu g/kg$ (from balance repeatability)

**Step 2:** Assume independence and apply the combined uncertainty formula.

$$
u_c = \sqrt{u_{cal}^{2} + u_{w}^{2}}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$
u_c = \sqrt{(0.8)^{2} + (0.3)^{2}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the squares.

$$
u_c = \sqrt{0.64 + 0.09}
$$

**Step 5:** Sum the squares.

$$
u_c = \sqrt{0.73}
$$

**Step 6:** Take the square root.

$$
u_c \approx 0.854\ \mu g/kg
$$

**Step 7:** Apply the coverage factor $k = 2$ to obtain the expanded uncertainty.

$$
U = 2 \times 0.854
$$

**Step 8:** Final result.

$$
U \approx 1.71\ \mu g/kg
$$

**Step 9:** Express the final reported value with uncertainty.

$$
\text{Result} = (15.2 \pm 1.71)\ \mu g/kg\ \text{at}\ 95\%\ \text{confidence}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision rule (Clause 7.8.6):** The FSSAI limit for aflatoxin B1 in groundnut is **20 µg/kg**. With a result of $(15.2 \pm 1.71)\ \mu g/kg$, the upper bound is **16.91 µg/kg**, which is below the limit. The decision rule is: **conform** if the upper bound $\leq$ limit. This is the **"shared risk" decision rule** (no more than 50% probability of false accept).

### Step 7 — Reporting of Results (Clause 7.8)

A valid test report (or calibration certificate) must include:
- **Title** (e.g., "Test Report")
- **Name and address of the lab**
- **Unique identification** of the report (e.g., TR/2024/F/0521)
- **Identification of the customer**
- **Identification of the method used**
- **Description of the item tested**
- **Date of receipt and date of performance**
- **Reference to the sampling plan (if relevant)**
- **Results with units and uncertainty**
- **A statement of conformity (if applicable), with the decision rule**
- **Authorized signatory**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Opus 7.8.2 — The "Common Rule" Trap:** If the report contains opinions or interpretations, the lab must document the **basis for the opinion**. This is a common audit non-conformance.

### Step 8 — Complaints and Non-Conforming Work (Clauses 7.9 & 7.10)

If a client complains, the lab must:
- Acknowledge receipt within a defined time.
- Investigate the root cause.
- Decide on corrective action.
- Communicate the outcome to the client.

If internal work is found non-conforming, the lab must:
- **Stop the work** or recall the report.
- **Evaluate the impact** (e.g., is the report already with the customer?).
- **Take corrective action** (e.g., revise the report, re-test the sample).
- **Document everything**.

---

## 3.2 Comparison Matrix — ISO 17025 vs ISO 9001 vs GLP

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **favourite KTU comparison question.** Examiners love asking: *"Differentiate between ISO 17025 and ISO 9001."*

| Parameter | ISO/IEC 17025 | ISO 9001 | GLP (Good Laboratory Practice) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Type** | Standard for laboratory competence | Generic QMS for any organization | Regulatory standard for non-clinical safety studies |
| **Focus** | Technical competence + management | Customer satisfaction + process | Study integrity and traceability |
| **Issued by** | ISO/IEC | ISO | OECD |
| **Who needs it?** | Testing & calibration labs | Manufacturing, services, education | Pharmaceutical, pesticide, chemical testing |
| **Accreditation body** | NABL (India), A2LA (USA), UKAS (UK) | IAF member bodies | National GLP compliance programs |
| **Key difference** | Demands **demonstrated technical competence** | Demands **process consistency** | Demands **data integrity for regulatory studies** |
| **Mutual recognition** | Yes (via ILAC MRA) | Yes (via IAF MLA) | Yes (via OECD MAD) |

---

## 3.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison — NABL and International Equivalents

| Country | Accreditation Body | Scope |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **India** | **NABL** (National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories) | All testing/calibration fields including food |
| **USA** | A2LA (American Association for Laboratory Accreditation) | Testing, calibration, inspection |
| **UK** | UKAS (United Kingdom Accreditation Service) | All conformity assessment |
| **EU** | National bodies, coordinated by EA | All testing/calibration |
| **Asia-Pacific** | APAC (formerly APLAC) | Regional cooperation |
| **Global** | ILAC (International Laboratory Accreditation Cooperation) | Worldwide MRA |

> [!NOTE]
> **ILAC MRA** is the single most important reason a test report from an NABL-accredited lab in Kochi is accepted in Hamburg, Houston, and Hong Kong without retesting.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISO 17025 Clause Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["ISO IEC 17025 2017 Standard"]
    nodeB["Clause 1 Scope"]
    nodeC["Clause 2 Normative References"]
    nodeD["Clause 3 Terms and Definitions"]
    nodeE["PILLAR 1 Management Requirements Clauses 4 and 5"]
    nodeF["Clause 4 General Requirements Impartiality Confidentiality"]
    nodeG["Clause 5 Structural and Resource Requirements"]
    nodeH["PILLAR 2 Technical Requirements Clauses 6 7 8"]
    nodeI["Clause 6 Resource Requirements Personnel Facilities Equipment"]
    nodeJ["Clause 7 Process Requirements Sampling Testing Reporting"]
    nodeK["Clause 8 Management System Requirements Risk Based Thinking"]
    nodeL["INDIAN CONTEXT NABL Accreditation FSSAI Notification"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeH
    nodeA --> nodeL
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeK
```

> **Figure 1:** The two-pillar architecture of ISO/IEC 17025:2017. The Management pillar (Clauses 4–5) and the Technical pillar (Clauses 6–8) are both mandatory, but the Technical pillar is the **unique identifier** of an ISO 17025 lab.

---

## 4.2 Risk-Based Thinking Flow (Clause 8.5)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStep1["STEP 1 Identify Risks and Opportunities"]
    nodeStep2["STEP 2 Assess Impact and Likelihood"]
    nodeStep3["STEP 3 Plan and Implement Controls"]
    nodeStep4["STEP 4 Monitor and Review Effectiveness"]
    nodeStep5["STEP 5 Document and Communicate"]
    nodeStep6["STEP 6 Take Corrective Action if Needed"]

    nodeStep1 --> nodeStep2
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5
    nodeStep5 --> nodeStep6
    nodeStep6 -.-> nodeStep1
```

> **Figure 2:** The continuous improvement cycle of risk-based thinking introduced in the 2017 revision.

---

## 4.3 Sample Lifecycle — From Receipt to Report (Clause 7)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR1["STEP 1 Request Review and Contract Acceptance Clause 7 1"]
    nodeR2["STEP 2 Method Selection and Validation Clause 7 2"]
    nodeR3["STEP 3 Sampling if Applicable Clause 7 3"]
    nodeR4["STEP 4 Sample Receipt and Storage Clause 7 4"]
    nodeR5["STEP 5 Test Execution and Technical Records Clause 7 5 7 7"]
    nodeR6["STEP 6 Measurement Uncertainty Evaluation Clause 7 6"]
    nodeR7["STEP 7 Decision Rule Application Clause 7 8 6"]
    nodeR8["STEP 8 Report Issuance and Review Clause 7 8"]
    nodeR9["STEP 9 Archival and Retention Records Control"]

    nodeR1 --> nodeR2
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3
    nodeR3 --> nodeR4
    nodeR4 --> nodeR5
    nodeR5 --> nodeR6
    nodeR6 --> nodeR7
    nodeR7 --> nodeR8
    nodeR8 --> nodeR9
```

> **Figure 3:** The complete lifecycle of a food sample in an ISO 17025-accredited lab, mapped to the relevant clauses. This figure is the **definitive diagram** for a 14-mark descriptive question.

---

## 4.4 Decision Rule Visualization (Conformity Assessment)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Measured Value 15.2 ug per kg"]
    nodeB["Expanded Uncertainty 1.71 ug per kg"]
    nodeC["Combined Result 15.2 plus or minus 1.71"]
    nodeD["FSSAI Limit 20 ug per kg"]
    nodeE["Upper Bound 16.91 ug per kg"]
    nodeF["Decision CONFORM"]
    nodeG["Decision NON CONFORM"]

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE -- Less than or equal to limit --> nodeF
    nodeE -- Greater than limit --> nodeG
```

> **Figure 4:** Application of the "shared risk" decision rule. The lab's reported value with uncertainty is compared against a regulatory limit. In this case, the upper bound (16.91 µg/kg) is below the FSSAI limit of 20 µg/kg, so the sample is declared **conform**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define ISO/IEC 17025. Mention its significance in the context of food testing laboratories in India.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

ISO/IEC 17025:2017 is an international standard published jointly by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC). It specifies the **general requirements for the competence, impartiality, and consistent operation** of testing and calibration laboratories.

In the context of food testing in India, this standard is enforced by the **National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories (NABL)**. Under the Food Safety and Standards (Recognition and Notification of Laboratories) Regulations, 2018, FSSAI recognizes only those food testing laboratories that are **NABL-accredited under ISO/IEC 17025**. Accreditation ensures that the analytical reports on food safety parameters (e.g., pesticide residues, microbial counts, additives, contaminants) are technically valid, legally admissible, and internationally recognized through the **ILAC Mutual Recognition Arrangement (MRA)**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between verification and validation of a method under ISO 17025.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Confirmation that a method works as documented by the method developer. | Confirmation that the method is fit for the **specific intended use** of the lab. |
| **Trigger** | Adoption of an international/national standard method (e.g., BIS, AOAC). | Use of a non-standard method, or a standard method used outside its scope. |
| **Depth** | Simpler — confirm the lab can achieve the performance claimed. | Comprehensive — specificity, linearity, accuracy, precision, LOD, LOQ, robustness. |
| **Documentation** | Verification record (e.g., "We achieved the precision claimed in ISO method X."). | Full validation report with all performance characteristics. |

> **Memory Anchor:** *Verification = "Does it work here?"* — *Validation = "Is it suitable for the purpose?"*

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`
**(a)** Explain the **structure and major clauses of ISO/IEC 17025:2017**. Differentiate between the **management requirements and technical requirements** of the standard. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **process of accreditation** of a food testing laboratory in India by NABL. Highlight the role of proficiency testing and measurement uncertainty in maintaining accreditation. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Structure of ISO/IEC 17025:2017 (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
ISO/IEC 17025:2017 is the current international standard for laboratory competence. It was published in November 2017 and supersedes the 2005 edition. The standard is organized into **5 normative clauses** plus **3 informative annexes**.

**Clause-by-Clause Breakdown (5 marks):**

1. **Clause 1 — Scope:** Defines that the standard applies to all organizations performing laboratory activities, regardless of sector or size.
2. **Clause 2 — Normative References:** Refers to ISO and IEC documents indispensable for application (e.g., ISO/IEC Guide 99, VIM).
3. **Clause 3 — Terms and Definitions:** Aligns with ISO/IEC 17000 series vocabulary.
4. **Clauses 4 & 5 — Management Requirements:** Cover impartiality (4.1), confidentiality (4.2), structural requirements (4.3), and the operational management of resources, processes, and the management system.
5. **Clauses 6, 7 & 8 — Technical Requirements:** Cover personnel, facilities, equipment, metrological traceability, externally provided products, sampling, testing methods, technical records, measurement uncertainty, reporting, complaints, non-conforming work, and management system options.

**Difference Between Management and Technical Requirements (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Management Requirements | Technical Requirements |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Focus** | Organization, impartiality, QMS | People, equipment, methods, results |
| **Clauses** | 4 and 5 | 6, 7, and 8 |
| **Analogous to** | ISO 9001 (but not identical) | Unique to ISO 17025 |
| **Test of competence** | Indirect | Direct |

#### Part (b) — NABL Accreditation Process (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Application (1 mark):** The lab submits an application to NABL via the NABL-200 portal, specifying the scope of testing (e.g., microbiology, chemical, pesticide residues) and the methods (e.g., FSSAI, AOAC, ISO).

**Step 2 — Document Review (1 mark):** NABL appoints a lead assessor who reviews the lab's **Quality Manual, SOPs, and technical documents** for compliance with ISO 17025:2017.

**Step 3 — Pre-Assessment (1 mark):** A voluntary on-site visit to identify major gaps before the formal assessment.

**Step 4 — On-Site Assessment (1 mark):** A team of technical assessors visits the lab, witnesses tests, checks equipment calibration, reviews records, and interviews staff. Non-conformities are documented.

**Step 5 — Corrective Actions (1 mark):** The lab must close all non-conformities within a stipulated time and submit evidence to the assessment team.

**Step 6 — Accreditation Decision (1 mark):** NABL's accreditation committee reviews the assessment report and grants accreditation for a defined scope, valid for 2 years.

**Step 7 — Surveillance and Reassessment (1 mark):** Annual surveillance visits and biennial re-assessment ensure continued compliance. **Proficiency testing (PT)** is mandatory — the lab must participate in inter-laboratory comparisons (e.g., **FSSAI PT schemes, AOCS, AOAC PT programs**) and demonstrate acceptable $z$-scores (typically $\vert z \vert \leq 2$). **Measurement uncertainty** must be estimated for each test method and reported when relevant.

---

### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**(a)** Discuss the **role of measurement uncertainty** in food testing laboratories. With a suitable example, explain how the **combined standard uncertainty ($u_c$) and expanded uncertainty ($U$)** are calculated. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **risk-based thinking approach** introduced in ISO/IEC 17025:2017. Differentiate between the two **management system options (Option A and Option B)** available to laboratories. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Measurement Uncertainty (7 marks)

**Definition and Importance (2 marks):**
Measurement uncertainty (MU) is a **parameter characterizing the dispersion of values** that can reasonably be attributed to the measurand. It is a quantitative expression of the **doubt** that exists about the result of any measurement. In food testing, MU is essential because:
- Regulatory limits (e.g., FSSAI, Codex) are prescribed with a **maximum level**; the lab's reported value must lie within the limit with **known confidence**.
- MU is required to make a **statement of conformity** (Clause 7.8.6).
- MU supports the **decision rule** — for instance, the "shared risk" rule (50% probability of false accept) versus the "simple acceptance" rule (zero probability of false accept).

**Formulas and Worked Example (5 marks):**

The **combined standard uncertainty** for independent input quantities is given by:

$$
u_c(y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \right)^{2} u^{2}(x_i)}
$$

The **expanded uncertainty** is:

$$
U = k \cdot u_c
$$

**Worked Example — Moisture Content in Butter:**
A lab measures moisture in butter and obtains a value of **16.2%**. The relative standard uncertainty from the drying oven ($u_{oven}$) is **0.5%**, and from the balance ($u_{bal}$) is **0.3%**.

**Step 1:** Identify the input uncertainties.
- $u_{oven} = 0.5\%$
- $u_{bal} = 0.3\%$

**Step 2:** Apply the combined standard uncertainty formula.

$$
u_c = \sqrt{(0.5)^{2} + (0.3)^{2}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the squares.

$$
u_c = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.09}
$$

**Step 4:** Sum the squares.

$$
u_c = \sqrt{0.34}
$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root.

$$
u_c \approx 0.583\%
$$

**Step 6:** Apply $k = 2$ for 95% confidence.

$$
U = 2 \times 0.583
$$

**Step 7:** Final expanded uncertainty.

$$
U \approx 1.17\%
$$

**Step 8:** Final reported result.

$$
\text{Result} = (16.2 \pm 1.17)\% \text{ at 95\% confidence}
$$

#### Part (b) — Risk-Based Thinking and Management System Options (7 marks)

**Risk-Based Thinking — Introduction (2 marks):**
The 2017 revision of ISO 17025 introduced **risk-based thinking** (Clause 8.5) to replace the preventive-action concept of the 2005 edition. The standard now requires the lab to:
- Identify **risks and opportunities** associated with its activities.
- Plan actions to **address risks** and pursue opportunities.
- **Integrate these actions** into the management system.
- **Evaluate the effectiveness** of these actions.

**Examples of Risks in a Food Lab:**
- Equipment failure (e.g., HPLC breakdown during a sequence).
- Power outage.
- Loss of trained personnel.
- Cybersecurity threats to LIMS.
- Sample mix-up.
- Method scope creep.

**Difference Between Option A and Option B (5 marks):**

| Feature | **Option A** | **Option B** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Description** | Minimum management system requirements as described in Clauses 8.1 to 8.9. | The lab establishes and maintains a management system in accordance with **ISO 9001**. |
| **Documentation** | Required: scope, policies, objectives, internal audit, management review, corrective actions, risk management. | Required: all of Option A + alignment with ISO 9001 structure (process approach, customer focus, leadership, etc.). |
| **Audit burden** | Lighter — covers only ISO 17025. | Heavier — covers ISO 17025 + ISO 9001. |
| **Most chosen** | **Most common choice** for accredited labs. | Chosen by labs that already hold ISO 9001 and want to leverage that investment. |
| **Flexibility** | Greater flexibility to design a lean system. | Less flexibility — must follow ISO 9001's mandatory clauses. |
| **Example** | A small food testing start-up. | A large multi-site contract testing lab already ISO 9001-certified. |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning — Read This Before You Write**
> 1. **Do not confuse ISO 17025 with ISO 9001.** Examiners deduct up to **3 marks** if you use the terms interchangeably. ISO 9001 is a generic QMS; ISO 17025 is **competence-focused**.
> 2. **Always cite the 2017 version.** Saying "ISO 17025:2005" without mentioning the 2017 revision can cost you **2 marks** because the 2017 edition is the **current** version.
> 3. **Do not write "NABL gives ISO certification."** NABL gives **accreditation**, not certification. The exam explicitly tests this distinction.
> 4. **MU calculation: Show the formula before substituting.** A common 1-mark loss is for jumping straight to a numerical answer.
> 5. **Use the right clause numbers.** Clause 7.6 is measurement uncertainty; Clause 7.8 is reporting; Clause 8.5 is risk-based thinking. Mixing them up is a marker of incomplete preparation.
> 6. **Avoid abbreviations without expansion.** First reference: **NABL (National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories)**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **The 20-Point Rapid Revision List — Memorize Before the Exam**

1. ISO/IEC 17025:2017 is the **current** standard for testing and calibration laboratories.
2. It is jointly published by **ISO and IEC**.
3. The 2017 edition replaced the **2005** edition.
4. The standard has **5 mandatory clauses** (Clauses 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 — actually 8 clauses, with 4–5 being Management and 6–8 being Technical).
5. **Impartiality (Clause 4.1)** and **confidentiality (Clause 4.2)** are non-negotiable general requirements.
6. The standard's **two pillars** are Management Requirements and Technical Requirements.
7. **NABL** is the Indian accreditation body; it is a constituent board of **QCI (Quality Council of India)**.
8. **ILAC MRA** ensures global acceptance of accredited test reports.
9. **Verification** applies to standard methods; **validation** applies to non-standard or modified methods.
10. **Measurement uncertainty** is mandatory under Clause 7.6.
11. The **expanded uncertainty formula** is $U = k \cdot u_c$ with $k = 2$ for 95% confidence.
12. **Risk-based thinking** (Clause 8.5) is the major innovation of the 2017 edition.
13. **Proficiency testing (PT)** is mandatory and uses $z$-scores (acceptable range: $\vert z \vert \leq 2$).
14. The **decision rule** (Clause 7.8.6) must be documented and applied when declaring conformity.
15. **Option A** is the minimum management system; **Option B** aligns with **ISO 9001**.
16. **Metrological traceability** must be established for all measurement equipment.
17. **Sampling** is governed by Clause 7.3 and may follow **ISO 2859-1** for attribute sampling.
18. **Non-conforming work** (Clause 7.10) requires immediate stop, evaluation, and corrective action.
19. The **test report** must include a unique identification, method used, results with units, and authorized signatory.
20. For food testing in India, FSSAI mandates **NABL accreditation under ISO 17025** under the 2018 Laboratory Regulations.

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 — ISO 17025 NOTES**
> **Course:** OEFTT723 — FOOD LAWS AND LEGISLATION
> **Module:** 4
> **Topic:** ISO 17025
> **Scheme:** KTU 2024 (NEP 2020 Aligned)
