topics.title: PAS 22000
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT723 - FOOD LAWS AND LEGISLATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PAS 22000

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**PAS 22000** (Publicly Available Specification 22000) is a publicly available specification that was published by the **British Standards Institution (BSI)** in **2008**. It establishes the requirements for **Prerequisite Programmes (PRPs)** for organizations involved in the manufacturing and processing of food products. It was specifically designed to be used **in conjunction with ISO 22000:2005** (Food Safety Management Systems) by providing detailed, sector-specific prerequisite programme requirements that bridge the gap left by ISO 22000's general PRP framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official Full Title:** *PAS 22000:2008 - Food manufacturing - Requirements for prerequisite programmes and the design and implementation of food safety management systems.* *(Note: PAS 22000 has been superseded by ISO/TS 22002-1:2009 for food manufacturing, but it remains a historically and academically important reference in the KTU 2024 syllabus.)*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a **cloud kitchen** (a food business operating entirely on online delivery). **ISO 22000** gives you the *master rulebook* for managing food safety — it tells you to "have good kitchen hygiene," "control hazards," and "communicate with suppliers." But it does NOT tell you the *exact* number of times you must sanitize chopping boards, the *exact* layout of your cold storage, or the *exact* way to segregate raw chicken from ready-to-eat salads.

That is where **PAS 22000** steps in.

Think of PAS 22000 as the **"Detailed House Rules Manual"** that sits next to the master rulebook. It provides the **practical, hands-on, kitchen-floor-level instructions** that the broad ISO 22000 standard assumes you already know. It converts a *philosophy* of food safety into a *checklist of operational requirements* — covering building layout, equipment design, cleaning schedules, personal hygiene of workers, supplier controls, and waste management.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Publicly Available"?** Unlike ISO standards (which are copyrighted and paid), a PAS is freely accessible in draft form, allowing wider adoption. This is why PAS 22000 spread quickly across global food supply chains, especially among SMEs (Small and Medium Enterprises).

### 1.3 Key Standard Identifiers

| Identifier | Meaning |
|------------|---------|
| **PAS** | Publicly Available Specification |
| **22000** | Series number aligned with ISO 22000 family |
| **:2008** | Year of first publication |
| **BSI** | Issuing body — British Standards Institution |

### 1.4 Origin and Genesis

PAS 22000 emerged from a recognized industry need:

- **ISO 22000:2005** introduced the concept of **PRPs (Prerequisite Programmes)** but treated them as basic, general, foundational conditions.
- The **Global Food Safety Initiative (GFSI)** and the **Confederation of the Food and Drink Industries of the EU (CIAA)** identified that ISO 22000 alone did not provide enough detail for **food manufacturers** to achieve benchmarked food safety standards.
- Industry leaders including **Nestlé, Unilever, Kraft, and Coca-Cola** pushed for a sector-specific PRP document — leading BSI to publish PAS 22000 in **2008**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Transition:** PAS 22000:2008 → **ISO/TS 22002-1:2009** (Food manufacturing) → **ISO/TS 22002-1:2022** (current revised version). PAS 22000 is the *parent* specification that evolved into the ISO Technical Specification (TS) 22002 series.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Specification Sheet

### 2.1 Purpose and Scope of PAS 22000

PAS 22000 was developed with the following **core objectives**:

1. **Bridge the Gap** — Provide the missing operational detail in ISO 22000:2005's PRP section.
2. **Enable GFSI Recognition** — Help food manufacturers satisfy GFSI benchmarked standards (BRC, FSSC 22000, SQF).
3. **Standardize PRPs** — Offer a single, harmonized PRP framework applicable across food manufacturing sectors.
4. **Support HACCP** — Strengthen the foundation on which Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP) plans are built.
5. **Facilitate Trade** — Create a globally accepted food safety baseline for international food trade.

### 2.2 Relationship with ISO 22000:2005

PAS 22000 does **NOT** replace ISO 22000. It **supplements** it. The structural relationship is:

- **ISO 22000:2005** = The Management System (Plan, Do, Check, Act cycle + Hazard Analysis + PRP framework + OPRP + CCPs).
- **PAS 22000:2008** = The Operational PRP Detail (Specific, measurable, auditable prerequisite conditions).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Specification Sheet (Clauses & Requirements)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **17 key clause areas** of PAS 22000:2008. This is the high-yield reference table for KTU exams.

| Clause | Subject | Key Requirement Summary |
|--------|---------|--------------------------|
| **4.1** | Documentation and Records | Maintain documented PRP procedures and records of all prerequisite activities. |
| **4.2** | Site Location, Design and Layout | Site must be located away from polluted/contaminated zones; design must allow hygienic operations and prevent cross-contamination. |
| **4.3** | Construction and Layout of Buildings | Walls, floors, ceilings must be smooth, cleanable, and non-toxic. Separation of high/low-risk areas. |
| **4.4** | Services (Air, Water, Energy) | Potable water, controlled ventilation, adequate lighting, and reliable energy supply. |
| **4.5** | Waste Disposal | Defined waste management system — segregation, collection, and disposal. |
| **4.6** | Equipment Suitability, Cleaning and Maintenance | Food-contact surfaces must be non-toxic; preventive maintenance schedule mandatory. |
| **4.7** | Cross-Contamination Control | Physical/temporal separation of raw and finished products. |
| **4.8** | Cleaning and Sanitation | Documented cleaning schedules, validated sanitation methods, control of chemicals. |
| **4.9** | Pest Control | Documented pest management programme with regular inspections. |
| **4.10** | Personnel Hygiene and Facilities | Locker rooms, toilets, hand-washing stations, protective clothing. |
| **4.11** | Personal Practices | Hair restraints, jewellery restrictions, no eating/smoking in production areas. |
| **4.12** | Storage and Transport | Segregated storage, FIFO/FEFO, controlled transport conditions (temperature, humidity). |
| **4.13** | Product Information and Consumer Awareness | Accurate labels, allergen declarations, traceability codes. |
| **4.14** | Food Defence, Bioterrorism, and Biosecurity | Protection of food from intentional adulteration or malicious acts. |
| **4.15** | Allergen Management | Segregation, validation of cleaning, and labelling of allergen-containing materials. |
| **4.16** | Training and Competence | All personnel must be trained in food safety; competency must be documented. |
| **4.17** | Internal Audits and Management Review | Periodic audits to verify PRP effectiveness. |

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Major Requirement Cluster

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite Concept:** Why are PRPs necessary?

**Cluster A — Environmental Controls (Clauses 4.2 to 4.5):**
- **Why?** The physical environment is the *first line of defence*. A factory next to an open drain cannot produce safe food no matter how good its HACCP plan is.
- **How?** Zoning, hygienic design, controlled utilities, and waste management prevent *introduction* of hazards from outside.

**Cluster B — Equipment & Sanitation (Clauses 4.6 to 4.9):**
- **Why?** Equipment surfaces harbour pathogens (e.g., *Listeria monocytogenes* biofilms). Pests are vectors of *Salmonella* and *rodent-borne* diseases.
- **How?** Hygienic equipment design, validated CIP (Clean-In-Place) systems, SSOPs (Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures), and IPM (Integrated Pest Management).

**Cluster C — People Controls (Clauses 4.10 to 4.11, 4.16):**
- **Why?** Humans are the most common source of cross-contamination. Improper handwashing alone causes ~30% of foodborne outbreaks globally.
- **How?** Handwashing SOPs, glove protocols, training, medical fitness checks, and supervision.

**Cluster D — Material Flow Controls (Clauses 4.7, 4.12, 4.13, 4.15):**
- **Why?** Allergen mix-ups, mislabelling, and raw-to-finished cross-contamination are major recall triggers.
- **How?** Segregated lines, validated cleaning between allergens, traceability codes, and clear labelling.

**Cluster E — Strategic Controls (Clauses 4.1, 4.14, 4.17):**
- **Why?** Documentation ensures consistency; food defence addresses intentional threats; audits verify that PRPs are *living systems* and not *paper systems*.
- **How?** Document control, TACCP (Threat Assessment Critical Control Points), and internal audit programmes.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Industry

- **Certifications built on PAS 22000 lineage:** FSSC 22000 (Foundation FSSC) — used by 30,000+ companies globally.
- **Audit standard:** Auditors use PAS 22000 / ISO 22002-1 requirements to check whether PRPs are *adequate* before evaluating the HACCP system.
- **Export compliance:** Many global retailers (Walmart, Tesco, Carrefour) require suppliers to meet GFSI-benchmarked standards, which are built on PAS 22000 / ISO 22002-1.
- **Legal evidence:** In foodborne illness litigation, PRPs conforming to PAS 22000 serve as *due diligence* evidence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: Implementation Logic of PAS 22000

> [!NOTE]
> Since PAS 22000 is a *process standard* (not a mathematical one), the "derivations" below are presented as a **logical implementation flow** — the equivalent of a step-by-step derivation for a regulatory framework.

### 3.1 The 7-Step Implementation Logic

**Step 1 — Define the Scope of the Food Safety Management System (FSMS)**
- Identify which products, processes, and sites fall under PAS 22000 coverage.
- Documentation requirement: Scope statement (mandatory under ISO 22000:2005 Clause 4.1).
- Logic: You cannot audit or verify something that is not *clearly bounded*.

**Step 2 — Establish Documentation and Record-Keeping System**
- Create a controlled document register.
- Define retention periods for records (typical industry practice: minimum 2 years or shelf life + 1 year, whichever is longer).
- Logic: PRPs fail not because of poor procedures, but because of *missing proof* that procedures were followed.

**Step 3 — Conduct Site and Facility Assessment**
- Inspect site against Clause 4.2 (location), 4.3 (building layout), 4.4 (services).
- Identify potential sources of contamination: neighbouring industries, drainage patterns, airborne pollutants.
- Logic: A hygienic factory begins at the *site selection stage* — retrofitting a contaminated site is rarely fully effective.

**Step 4 — Implement Equipment and Sanitation Programmes**
- Develop a **Master Cleaning Schedule** (MCS) for all equipment.
- Validate cleaning methods (e.g., ATP swab testing, allergen swab testing).
- Implement a **Preventive Maintenance** programme.
- Logic: Equipment that is *clean* but *broken* is as dangerous as equipment that is *broken* but *clean*.

**Step 5 — Establish Personnel Controls**
- Conduct pre-employment medical screening.
- Provide training on personal hygiene, allergens, and food defence.
- Display visual SOPs at handwash stations and entry points.
- Logic: Behaviour-based controls require *continuous reinforcement*, not one-time training.

**Step 6 — Implement Material Flow and Allergen Controls**
- Map product flow from raw material intake → processing → packaging → dispatch.
- Create **allergen matrices** identifying which products contain which allergens.
- Validate cleaning between allergen changeovers.
- Logic: Cross-contamination is the *silent* hazard — it is invisible, tasteless, and odourless.

**Step 7 — Verification Through Internal Audits and Reviews**
- Conduct internal audits of PRPs at planned intervals.
- Hold management review meetings to evaluate PRP effectiveness.
- Trigger corrective actions and update PRPs.
- Logic: A PRP that is *never audited* is, in practice, a *non-existent* PRP.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Apply PAS 22000 vs Other Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a KTU 2024 high-yield analytical question area.**

| Scenario | Applicable Standard | Rationale |
|----------|---------------------|-----------|
| A packaged food manufacturer wants third-party certification of FSMS | **FSSC 22000** (built on ISO 22000 + ISO/TS 22002-1, which evolved from PAS 22000) | GFSI-benchmarked, globally recognized |
| A small restaurant wants a basic food safety system | **ISO 22000 + local regulations** | PAS 22000 is too detailed for retail/restaurant; restaurant sector uses ISO 22002-5 |
| A primary producer (farm) wants food safety certification | **ISO 22002-6 (Feed/Primary production)** or **GLOBALG.A.P.** | PAS 22000 is for *manufacturing*, not primary production |
| A food packaging material manufacturer | **ISO 22002-4 (Packaging)** | Specific to packaging manufacturers |
| A food caterer / transport operator | **ISO 22002-5 (Retail/catering)** | Specifically scoped for service operations |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Comparison: PAS 22000 vs ISO 22000:2005

**Step 1: Identify the Document Type**
- ISO 22000:2005 = International Standard (mandatory for FSMS certification).
- PAS 22000:2008 = Publicly Available Specification (supporting document, not standalone certifiable).

**Step 2: Identify the Core Focus**
- ISO 22000:2005 = Management system + Hazard analysis + Communication + Continual improvement.
- PAS 22000:2008 = Detailed PRPs (operational conditions) for food manufacturing.

**Step 3: Identify the User**
- ISO 22000:2005 = Top management, FSMS team leaders, auditors.
- PAS 22000:2008 = Floor supervisors, sanitation teams, maintenance teams, production staff.

**Step 4: Identify the Application Stage**
- ISO 22000:2005 = System-level, applied during FSMS design and review.
- PAS 22000:2008 = Operational level, applied *daily* on the shop floor.

**Step 5: Identify the Audit Reference**
- ISO 22000:2005 = Primary audit standard.
- PAS 22000:2008 = Used by auditors as a *reference* to verify PRP adequacy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PAS 22000 Position in the Food Safety Standard Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Global Food Safety Standards Hierarchy]:::title --> B[Level 1: ISO 22000:2005 - FSMS]
    A --> C[Level 2: Sector-Specific PRPs]
    A --> D[Level 3: Industry-Specific Standards]

    B --> B1[ISO 22000:2018 - Updated FSMS]:::update
    B --> B2[Management System Requirements]
    B --> B3[HACCP Principles]
    B --> B4[Communication]

    C --> C1[PAS 22000:2008 - Food Manufacturing]:::pas
    C --> C2[ISO 22002-1 - Food Manufacturing]
    C --> C3[ISO 22002-4 - Packaging]
    C --> C4[ISO 22002-5 - Retail and Catering]
    C --> C5[ISO 22002-6 - Feed and Primary Production]

    D --> D1[FSSC 22000 - GFSI Benchmarked]:::fssc
    D --> D2[BRC Global Standard]
    D --> D3[SQF Code]
    D --> D4[IFS Food]

    C1 -.->|Superseded by| C2
    B -.->|Combined with| C
    C -.->|Combined with| D

    classDef title fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef pas fill:#d4a017,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef fssc fill:#2d7a2d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef update fill:#a04040,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 PAS 22000 Implementation Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Inputs: Raw Materials, Environment, Personnel]:::input --> SITE[Site and Facility Controls - Clause 4.2 to 4.5]:::control
    SITE --> EQUIP[Equipment and Sanitation - Clause 4.6 to 4.9]:::control
    EQUIP --> PEOPLE[Personnel Hygiene and Training - Clause 4.10, 4.11, 4.16]:::control
    PEOPLE --> FLOW[Material Flow and Allergen Control - Clause 4.7, 4.12, 4.13, 4.15]:::control
    FLOW --> DEFENCE[Food Defence and Security - Clause 4.14]:::control
    DEFENCE --> DOC[Documentation and Records - Clause 4.1]:::control
    DOC --> AUDIT[Internal Audits and Review - Clause 4.17]:::control
    AUDIT --> OUTPUT[Output: Safe Food Product + Verified Records]:::output
    AUDIT -.->|Feedback Loop| SITE

    classDef input fill:#4a7ba8,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef control fill:#d4a017,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#2d7a2d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 PRP-HACCP Integration Pyramid (PAS 22000 Context)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[Continual Improvement and Management Review]:::top
    MID1[HACCP Plan and CCPs]:::haccp
    MID2[OPRPs - Operational Prerequisite Programmes]:::oprp
    BASE[PAS 22000 PRPs - Foundation Layer]:::base

    BASE -->|Supports| MID2
    MID2 -->|Supports| MID1
    MID1 -->|Reviewed by| TOP
    TOP -.->|Drives Improvement of| BASE

    classDef top fill:#a04040,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef haccp fill:#c25a00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef oprp fill:#d4a017,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef base fill:#2d7a2d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:4px,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Pyramid:** The PRP foundation (PAS 22000) is the *widest* because it touches the *most* operations daily. Without a strong PRP base, the HACCP plan above it becomes unstable. This is why PAS 22000 clauses are often called "the foundation of ISO 22000."

### 4.4 Cross-Contamination Control Logic (Detailed Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RAW[Raw Material Area]
        R1[Receiving Bay]
        R2[Cold Storage]
        R3[Thawing Station]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[Processing Area]
        P1[Sorting and Washing]
        P2[Cutting and Mixing]
        P3[Heat Treatment]
    end

    subgraph FINISH[Finished Product Area]
        F1[Packaging Line]
        F2[Labeling Station]
        F3[Dispatch Bay]
    end

    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> F1 --> F2 --> F3

    R1 -.Controlled Flow.-> P1
    R3 -.Temperature Control.-> P2
    P3 -.Metal Detection.-> F1
    F1 -.Allergen Verification.-> F2
    F2 -.Traceability Code.-> F3

    RAW:::raw
    PROCESS:::process
    FINISH:::finish

    classDef raw fill:#a04040,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef process fill:#d4a017,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef finish fill:#2d7a2d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*Define PAS 22000. State its issuing body and year of publication. Why was PAS 22000 developed?*

**Model Answer:**
PAS 22000 (Publicly Available Specification 22000) is a specification published by the **British Standards Institution (BSI) in 2008**. It specifies requirements for **Prerequisite Programmes (PRPs)** applicable to organizations involved in food manufacturing. It was developed to **supplement ISO 22000:2005**, which provided only a generic PRP framework. The food industry — through bodies like the **CIAA and GFSI** — recognized that ISO 22000 alone lacked sufficient operational detail for food manufacturers to establish robust PRPs. PAS 22000 bridges this gap by specifying detailed, auditable requirements across 17 key areas including site design, sanitation, personnel hygiene, allergen control, and food defence. *(3 marks)*

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any six major areas covered under PAS 22000. How does PAS 22000 relate to ISO 22000:2005?*

**Model Answer:**
Six major areas covered under PAS 22000:
1. Site location, design and layout
2. Construction and layout of buildings
3. Equipment suitability, cleaning and maintenance
4. Cleaning and sanitation
5. Personnel hygiene and facilities
6. Allergen management

**Relationship with ISO 22000:2005:** PAS 22000 is a *supplement*, not a replacement. ISO 22000:2005 provides the overall Food Safety Management System framework including HACCP and generic PRPs, whereas PAS 22000 provides **detailed, sector-specific operational requirements** for PRPs in food manufacturing. Both are used together to establish a robust FSMS. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, OEFTT723]**

**(a) Explain in detail the origin, purpose, and scope of PAS 22000. How does it supplement ISO 22000:2005? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Origin:** PAS 22000 was developed by the **British Standards Institution (BSI)** and published in **2008**. The development was driven by major food industry stakeholders (CIAA — Confederation of the Food and Drink Industries of the EU) and the **Global Food Safety Initiative (GFSI)**. These bodies identified that ISO 22000:2005, while revolutionary in combining HACCP with a management systems approach, did not provide enough **granular, sector-specific** detail for the establishment of Prerequisite Programmes (PRPs) in food manufacturing.

**Purpose:** The primary purposes of PAS 22000 are:
1. To provide a **harmonized, detailed PRP specification** for food manufacturing organizations.
2. To support the implementation of **ISO 22000:2005** by filling the operational gap.
3. To enable food manufacturers to meet **GFSI benchmark requirements** (used in BRC, FSSC 22000, SQF).
4. To provide an **auditable standard** for PRPs that auditors can use to assess compliance.
5. To serve as a **common reference** for global food trade, reducing audit duplication.

**Scope:** PAS 22000 applies to organizations involved in the **manufacturing of food products**, including:
- Processing of raw materials into finished products
- Packaging operations
- Re-packaging operations
- Storage and distribution of manufactured foods

It is *not* applicable to primary production (farms) or retail/catering.

**Relationship with ISO 22000:2005:** PAS 22000 is **subordinate and supplementary** to ISO 22000:2005. ISO 22000:2005 provides the *high-level FSMS* (Clauses 4–8), while PAS 22000 provides the *detailed operational PRP* requirements that feed into ISO 22000:2005's PRP requirement (Clause 7.2). Together, they form a complete food safety system. *(7 marks)*

**(b) Discuss the key requirements of PAS 22000 related to (i) cross-contamination control, (ii) allergen management, and (iii) food defence. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Cross-Contamination Control (Clause 4.7):**
Cross-contamination occurs when pathogens, allergens, or foreign materials transfer from one product/area to another. PAS 22000 requires:
- **Physical separation** of raw material and finished product areas.
- **Temporal separation** (production scheduling) when physical separation is not possible.
- Use of **dedicated equipment** for high-risk and low-risk products, or validated cleaning between changeovers.
- **Unidirectional flow** of materials and personnel to prevent backflow contamination.
- **Air handling systems** (positive/negative pressure zoning) to prevent airborne cross-contamination.

**(ii) Allergen Management (Clause 4.15):**
With the global rise in food allergies, PAS 22000 dedicates a specific clause to allergen control:
- Maintain an **allergen register** listing all raw materials and their allergenic components (the "Big 9" — milk, eggs, fish, shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, sesame).
- Implement **segregated storage** of allergen-containing materials.
- **Validate cleaning procedures** between allergen and non-allergen production runs (e.g., ELISA swab testing).
- Establish **allergen labelling** controls and cross-check during packaging.
- **Train personnel** on allergen risks and emergency response.

**(iii) Food Defence (Clause 4.14):**
This clause addresses protection of food from **intentional adulteration or malicious acts** (bioterrorism, sabotage):
- Conduct a **Threat Assessment (TACCP)** to identify vulnerable points.
- Implement **access controls** (key cards, biometric access, visitor logs).
- Secure storage of chemicals, additives, and high-risk materials.
- **Background checks** for personnel handling sensitive materials.
- **Surveillance systems** (CCTV) in critical areas.
- Establish a **response plan** for security breaches.
- Reference to international frameworks like the **US FDA Food Defence Mitigation Strategies** (IAVA — Intentional Adulteration Vulnerability Assessment).

*(7 marks)*

#### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, OEFTT723]**

**(a) Explain the role of Prerequisite Programmes (PRPs) in food safety management. Describe the major areas covered under PAS 22000 with specific examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of PRPs:** Prerequisite Programmes are the **foundational practices and conditions** that must be in place before HACCP can be effectively applied. They control the *general* hazards in the food manufacturing environment and create a hygienic baseline. PRPs:
1. **Reduce the likelihood of contamination** entering the product.
2. **Minimize the number of CCPs** required in the HACCP plan by controlling general hazards.
3. Provide a **stable operational platform** for the food safety management system.
4. Demonstrate **due diligence** to regulators and customers.
5. Form the **basic level of compliance** expected by GFSI-benchmarked standards.

**Major Areas Covered Under PAS 22000 (with examples):**

| Clause | Area | Practical Example |
|--------|------|-------------------|
| 4.2–4.3 | Site and Building Design | Factory located away from chemical plants; smooth stainless-steel wall panels for easy cleaning |
| 4.4 | Services | Potable water tested weekly; HVAC filters changed quarterly |
| 4.5 | Waste Disposal | Segregated colour-coded waste bins; daily waste removal from production floor |
| 4.6 | Equipment | Stainless steel tables with rounded corners; CIP system for milk processing line |
| 4.7 | Cross-Contamination | Separate chiller for raw meat and ready-to-eat salads |
| 4.8 | Cleaning | Sanitation SOPs with 200 ppm chlorine solution on food-contact surfaces |
| 4.9 | Pest Control | Quarterly pest control contract; fly-killer units at entry points |
| 4.10–4.11 | Personnel | Mandatory handwashing; no jewellery; hairnets and aprons |
| 4.12 | Storage and Transport | FIFO inventory system; refrigerated trucks at 4°C |
| 4.13 | Product Information | Allergen declaration on label; batch code for traceability |
| 4.14 | Food Defence | CCTV in ingredient store; visitor log book |
| 4.15 | Allergen Management | Allergen register; dedicated allergen-free production line |
| 4.16 | Training | Induction training for all new workers; annual refresher course |
| 4.17 | Internal Audits | Monthly PRP audits; quarterly management review |

*(7 marks)*

**(b) Critically analyze the relationship between PAS 22000 and ISO/TS 22002-1. Why was PAS 22000 superseded, and what lessons does its evolution provide for food safety management? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Relationship between PAS 22000 and ISO/TS 22002-1:**

PAS 22000:2008 and ISO/TS 22002-1:2009 are **conceptually identical** — both specify PRPs for food manufacturing. However, they differ in **status, authority, and scope**:

- **PAS 22000** is a *national-level Publicly Available Specification* issued by BSI. It is a **transitional** document, expected to be replaced.
- **ISO/TS 22002-1** is an *international Technical Specification* issued by ISO (International Organization for Standardization). It carries **global recognition** and is a member of the ISO 22000 family of standards.

**Why was PAS 22000 superseded?**

1. **Demand for international harmonization:** The food industry operates globally. A BSI-issued specification had limited authority outside the UK/EU. A ISO-issued document carries universal acceptance.
2. **GFSI recognition:** GFSI required PRP standards to be ISO-issued for global benchmarking. PAS 22000, being a national document, was not permanently eligible.
3. **Industry demand for stability:** Industry stakeholders needed a *long-term* reference, not a *transitional* one. ISO/TS 22002-1 promised long-term stability.
4. **Consolidation under the ISO 22000 family:** ISO wanted all related food safety standards to live under one coherent family tree, with consistent terminology, format, and review cycles.
5. **Update of ISO 22000 itself:** The eventual revision of ISO 22000 to the 2018 version reaffirmed the need for a *newer* PRP specification, leading to ISO 22002-1:2022 (current edition).

**Lessons from the evolution:**

1. **PRPs are not static** — they must evolve with new hazards (e.g., allergen management became prominent only in the late 2000s).
2. **International cooperation** is essential in food safety — diseases do not respect borders.
3. **Standards are living documents** — they undergo review every 5–7 years.
4. **Sector specificity matters** — different food sectors (manufacturing, packaging, catering, primary production) need different PRP standards, which led to the **ISO 22002-x series** (Parts 1 through 6).
5. **Documentation must translate to practice** — PAS 22000 and ISO 22002-1 both emphasize that PRPs must be *implemented, monitored, and verified*, not just written.

*(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse PAS 22000 with ISO 22000.** Students frequently interchange them. Remember: ISO 22000 is the *management system*; PAS 22000 is the *detailed PRP supplement*.
> 2. **Do NOT write "PAS 22000 is a certification."** It is a specification — organizations are *certified to FSSC 22000 or ISO 22000*, not to PAS 22000 itself.
> 3. **Do NOT list only ISO 22000 clauses** when asked about PAS 22000 areas. PAS 22000 has its own 17 sub-clauses (4.1 to 4.17) — know them distinctly.
> 4. **Always state the issuing body (BSI) and year (2008)** when defining PAS 22000 in Part A questions. Examiners award marks for these specifics.
> 5. **Mention the supersession by ISO/TS 22002-1:2009** in at least one answer — examiners specifically look for this updated knowledge.
> 6. **Avoid over-simplification** of food defence (Clause 4.14) — it is a *strategic* clause, not just a routine SOP. Use terms like TACCP and IAVA to demonstrate depth.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **PAS 22000** stands for **Publicly Available Specification 22000**, issued by **BSI in 2008**.
- [x] It is a **supplement to ISO 22000:2005**, providing **detailed PRP requirements** for **food manufacturing**.
- [x] It contains **17 key clause areas** covering site, equipment, sanitation, personnel, allergens, food defence, and audits.
- [x] PAS 22000 was the **predecessor of ISO/TS 22002-1:2009**, which has now been revised to **ISO/TS 22002-1:2022**.
- [x] It is **NOT a stand-alone certification**; it is used **with ISO 22000** to build certifiable systems like **FSSC 22000**.
- [x] The standard was driven by **GFSI and CIAA** to harmonize PRPs in global food manufacturing.
- [x] **Allergen management** and **food defence** are unique, modern additions introduced specifically in PAS 22000 (not in older HACCP-only systems).
- [x] It is **not applicable** to primary production, catering, or packaging — these have separate **ISO 22002-x** parts.
- [x] The hierarchy is: **PRPs (PAS 22000) → OPRPs → HACCP/CCPs → Continual Improvement**.
- [x] For KTU exams, always remember: **BSI, 2008, 17 clauses, food manufacturing, supplement to ISO 22000, superseded by ISO/TS 22002-1**.
- [x] Real-world impact: **FSSC 22000** (used by 30,000+ companies) and **BRC, SQF, IFS** all trace their PRP requirements back to PAS 22000.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Halal & Kosher Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT723 - FOOD LAWS AND LEGISLATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Halal & Kosher Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Halal — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Halal** (Arabic: حلال, meaning "permissible" or "lawful") is a comprehensive set of dietary and processing standards rooted in **Islamic Shariah Law**, primarily derived from the **Quran** and the **Hadith** (sayings and practices of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ). For a food product to be certified as Halal, every ingredient, processing aid, and method of manufacturing must comply with the prohibitions (Haram) and prescriptions (Halal) outlined in Islamic jurisprudence (**Fiqh**).

The technical scope of Halal certification extends far beyond the absence of pork or alcohol. It encompasses:
- **Source of animal** (species permitted by Shariah)
- **Method of slaughter** (Dhabihah — a precise ritual)
- **Processing equipment** (must not be contaminated with non-Halal substances)
- **Storage and logistics** (segregation from Haram products)
- **Sanitization agents** (must be alcohol-free and non-animal derived)

## 1.2 Kosher — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Kosher** (Hebrew: כָּשֵׁר, meaning "fit" or "proper") refers to a body of Jewish dietary law (**Kashrut**) derived from the **Torah** (the first five books of the Hebrew Bible), the **Talmud**, and rabbinical interpretations. A Kosher-certified food product must adhere to strict rules governing the species, preparation, and combination of ingredients, and must bear the endorsement of a recognized rabbinical authority.

The three foundational pillars of Kashrut are:
1. **Animal species selection** (only cloven-hoofed ruminants and specific fish)
2. **Separation of meat and dairy** (Basar Be-Chalav prohibition)
3. **Ritual slaughter** (Shechitah) by a trained **Shochet**

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Two-Key Security Lock"

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine entering a high-security facility. To gain access, you must satisfy **TWO independent security keys**. The first key (Halal or Kosher) verifies that the **raw materials** are ethically and spiritually sourced. The second key verifies that the **process** itself — every machine, every worker, every cleaning cycle — has not been "contaminated" by non-compliant operations. Halal and Kosher function exactly like this two-key system, ensuring that **both the input AND the process** are pure, traceable, and religiously sanctioned.

## 1.4 Core Standardized Metrics & Reference Authorities

> [!NOTE]  
> **Halal Standardization Bodies:**
> - **JAKIM** (Department of Islamic Development Malaysia) — global reference for Halal certification
> - **MUI** (Majelis Ulama Indonesia) — Indonesia's Halal certifying body
> - **MS 1500:2019** — Malaysian Halal Standard
> - **GSO 993:2015** — Gulf Standard for Halal Food

> [!NOTE]  
> **Kosher Standardization Authorities:**
> - **OU (Orthodox Union)** — largest Kosher certifier globally
> - **OK Kosher Certification**
> - **Star-K, KOF-K, CRC** — other major U.S. certifiers
> - **Badar Jerusalem, OK Israel** — international authorities

Both systems fundamentally ensure **food safety, ethical sourcing, and consumer trust** through a tightly regulated certification lifecycle.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparative Matrix

## 2.1 Foundational Principles — Halal

The Halal compliance system rests on **three operational pillars**:

1. **Permissibility of Source (Asl)**
   - The raw material must originate from a **Halal species**.
   - Land animals must be herbivores and must not be forbidden explicitly (e.g., pig, dog, predatory beasts, animals that died of natural causes — **Maytah**).
   - Aquatic animals must have **fins and scales** (majority view).
   - Blood and its derivatives are **Haram**.

2. **Method of Procurement (Kasb)**
   - Animals must be **slaughtered alive** by a **Muslim** of sound mind using a **sharp blade**.
   - The **Tasmiyah** (invocation "Bismillah Allahu Akbar") must be recited before the cut.
   - The cut must sever the **trachea, esophagus, and both carotid arteries** in a single swift motion to ensure rapid exsanguination.

3. **Processing Purity (Tahara)**
   - Cross-contamination with **Najis** (impure substances) is strictly prohibited.
   - Equipment used for Halal must be **cleaned** in a manner that removes residues of non-Halal products.
   - **Alcohol** is forbidden as a beverage and as a processing solvent in most interpretations.

## 2.2 Foundational Principles — Kosher

Kashrut compliance is governed by **six operative rules**:

1. **Permitted Species (Beheimah, Chayyah, Ofos)**
   - **Mammals**: must have cloven hooves AND chew cud (e.g., cattle, sheep, goat; pig is forbidden despite having cloven hooves — it doesn't chew cud).
   - **Fish**: must have **fins and scales** (shellfish, eels, sharks are forbidden).
   - **Birds**: must be from a kosher-approved list (chicken, turkey, duck are generally accepted; predatory birds are forbidden).

2. **Ritual Slaughter — Shechitah**
   - Performed by a trained **Shochet** using a **Halaf** (razor-sharp knife with no nicks).
   - The animal must be **conscious and healthy** at the time of slaughter.
   - A single swift cut severs the trachea, esophagus, and major blood vessels.

3. **Treifot (Prohibited Defects)**
   - An animal with specific organ defects (e.g., perforated lung) is considered **Treif** (non-Kosher) and cannot be consumed.

4. **Blood Removal (Meliach)**
   - Meat must be soaked and salted within 72 hours to remove blood (a Kosher requirement).

5. **Separation of Meat and Dairy (Basar Be-Chalav)**
   - Cannot cook, eat, or derive benefit from a mixture of meat and milk.
   - Requires **separate utensils, dishes, and waiting times** (typically 1–6 hours).

6. **Bishul Akum (Breads and Cooked Foods)**
   - In strict interpretations, bread and certain cooked foods must involve **Jewish participation** in preparation.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU High-Yield Comparative Matrix** — A summary of the two regulatory regimes:

| Parameter | Halal Standard | Kosher Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Religious Origin** | Islamic Shariah (Quran + Hadith) | Jewish Kashrut (Torah + Talmud) |
| **Permitted Land Animal** | Herbivores, not prohibited | Cloven hooves AND ruminant |
| **Permitted Aquatic** | Fins and scales (majority) | Fins and scales |
| **Ritual Slaughter** | Dhabihah (Muslim, Tasmiyah) | Shechitah (Shochet, Shochet knife) |
| **Blood Removal** | Required (exsanguination) | Soaking + salting (Meliach) |
| **Pork Prohibition** | Absolute (Haram) | Absolute (Treif) |
| **Alcohol Prohibition** | Forbidden as beverage & solvent | Permitted (no prohibition) |
| **Meat + Dairy** | No prohibition (only Haram) | Absolute separation required |
| **Slaughterer Requirement** | Must be Muslim | Must be trained Jewish Shochet |
| **Certification Body** | JAKIM, MUI, GSO | OU, OK, Star-K, KOF-K |
| **Standard Document** | MS 1500:2019, GSO 993:2015 | Star-K Standards, OU Handbook |
| **Cross-Contamination** | Najis avoidance | Pareve/equipment segregation |
| **Global Market Size** | ~USD 2.5 trillion (2024 est.) | ~USD 24 billion certified (U.S. alone) |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These standards are crucial in **Food Process Engineering, Cold Chain Logistics, and Quality Assurance Engineering** for designing segregated production lines, traceable batch coding systems, and HACCP-integrated certification protocols. Multinational food manufacturers (e.g., Nestlé, Unilever, Cargill) maintain dedicated Kosher and Halal production lines that incur measurable CapEx and OpEx, which the **Cost Accounting and Operations Management** modules address under quality cost classifications.

## 2.3 Certification Lifecycle (Operational Steps)

The process of obtaining certification in both systems is a **multi-stage, audit-driven workflow**:

**Halal Certification Lifecycle:**
1. **Application** → 2. **Document Review** → 3. **Factory Audit** → 4. **Ingredient Analysis** → 5. **Process Verification** → 6. **Slaughter Inspection** (if applicable) → 7. **Certificate Issuance** → 8. **Surveillance Audit (Annual)**

**Kosher Certification Lifecycle:**
1. **Application** → 2. **Rabbinical Review of Ingredients** → 3. **Facility Inspection (Cholov Yisroel/Pareve assessment)** → 4. **Process Equipment Audit** → 5. **Ongoing Mashgiach Supervision** → 6. **Kosher Symbol Issuance (e.g., OU, OK)** → 7. **Continuous Monitoring**

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners frequently award marks for correctly identifying that **both standards mandate a continuous, recurring audit cycle** — not a one-time approval. Failing to mention surveillance audits is a common 2-mark deduction in 14-mark questions.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Regulatory Derivation Logic

## 3.1 Derivation of Halal Compliance — Step-by-Step Logic

The legal reasoning behind each Halal rule is derived through a **Quranic source → Hadith verification → Fiqh interpretation** methodology. Let us walk through this analytically for the **Dhabihah slaughter method**:

**Step 1 — Quranic Mandate (Source Identification)**
The Quran explicitly states in Surah Al-Ma'idah (5:3):
> "Prohibited to you are the dead animals, blood, the flesh of swine, and that which is slaughtered for other than Allah."

**Step 2 — Hadith Specification (Method Specification)**
The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ prescribed (Hadith in Sahih Muslim):
> "The knife must be sharpened and the animal should be spared the agony of repeated cuts."

This mandates a **single, swift, complete cut** minimizing suffering — a principle that modern **animal welfare science** independently validates.

**Step 3 — Operational Translation (Fiqh Implementation)**
Islamic jurists derived operational rules:
- Cut must sever the **esophagus, trachea, jugular veins, and carotid arteries**.
- The **spinal cord** should NOT be severed (to avoid cardiac arrest before exsanguination).
- The animal must be **alive and conscious** at the moment of incision.

**Step 4 — Scientific Validation (Engineering Compatibility)**
$$\text{Exsanguination Efficiency} = \frac{V_{\text{out}} \text{ (blood lost)}}{V_{\text{total}} \text{ (total blood volume)}}$$

A complete severance of the four vessels ensures:
$$V_{\text{out}} \rightarrow \text{Maximum Exsanguination}$$

This produces:
$$\text{Residual Blood} \approx 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10 \text{ of total volume (acceptable threshold)}$$

This aligns with **Halal food safety science** and **Codex Alimentarius** thresholds for residual blood in meat.

## 3.2 Derivation of Kosher Compliance — Step-by-Step Logic

The Kosher derivation uses a different methodology — **Torah text → Talmudic expansion → Rabbinical codification (Halacha)**.

**Step 1 — Torah Mandate (Source)**
Leviticus 11 and Deuteronomy 14 enumerate permitted and forbidden species. The **dual-sign rule** (Hoof + Cud) for land animals is derived from these texts.

**Step 2 — Talmudic Logic Expansion**
The Talmud derives the principle: *"An animal possessing one Kosher sign but missing the other is forbidden."*
- Pig → has cloven hooves ✗, does not chew cud → **Treif**.
- Camel → chews cud ✗, does not have hooves → **Treif**.

**Step 3 — Slaughter Methodology (Shechitah)**
The Mishnah (Chullin 1:2) dictates five requirements for Shechitah:
1. **Shehiyah** (no hesitation)
2. **Derashah** (no pressing)
3. **Haladah** (no pause)
4. **Hagramah** (knife must be examined post-cut for any defect)
5. **Ikur** (the cut must sever major vessels)

**Step 4 — Post-Slaughter Processing (Meliach)**
Meat is soaked in water for **30 minutes → 1 hour** to release blood, then salted on all sides for **~1 hour**, then rinsed three times. This is a **standardized time-temperature-salt protocol**.

$$\text{Meliach Time Equation: } t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{soak}} + t_{\text{salt}} + t_{\text{rinse}}$$

Where:
- $t_{\text{soak}} \in [30, 60]$ **minutes**
- $t_{\text{salt}} \approx 60$ **minutes**
- $t_{\text{rinse}} = 3 \text{ rinses (sequence-bound)}$

**Step 5 — Separation of Meat and Dairy (Basar Be-Chalav)**
Three time-bound operational rules are derived:

$$\text{Waiting time after meat} \rightarrow 6 \text{ hours (Orthodox)}$$
$$\text{Waiting time after dairy} \rightarrow 1 \text{ hour (if not in same meal)}$$

This generates a **multi-vector compliance matrix** affecting utensils, dishwashers, cooking vessels, and food preparation surfaces.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Engineering Decision Logic

For a **food process engineer** designing a multi-standard production line, the decision matrix is:

| Production Variable | Halal Constraint | Kosher Constraint | Conflict Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Slaughter Operator** | Must be Muslim | Must be Jewish Shochet | Dual hiring or sequential production |
| **Slaughter Tool** | Sharp knife | Halaf (no nicks) | Specialized sharpening protocol |
| **Sanitizer** | Alcohol-free | Permitted | Use non-alcoholic Kosher-compliant sanitizers |
| **Cross-Contamination Zone** | Physical separation | Pareve/equipment segregation | Air-gapped production lines |
| **Storage Zone** | Segregated from pork | Segregated from non-Kosher | Color-coded binning (e.g., blue for Halal, red for Kosher) |
| **Dairy Inclusion** | Permitted (if Halal) | Strictly separate from meat | Independent dairy and meat lines |
| **Ingredient Audit** | E-numbers checked for alcohol | E-numbers checked for animal source | Cross-referenced against a centralized **E-number database** |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Insight — Cost Engineering Extension:** For a food plant producing both Halal and Kosher certified products, the **incremental cost per unit** of dual certification can be modeled as:
>
> $$\text{C}_{\text{dual}} = \text{C}_{\text{base}} + \text{C}_{\text{Halal\_audit}} + \text{C}_{\text{Kosher\_audit}} + \text{C}_{\text{segregation}}$$
>
> Where $\text{C}_{\text{segregation}}$ includes **capital expenditure** on dedicated equipment and **operational expenditure** on parallel cleaning and quality control teams.

## 3.4 Statistical & Global Market Logic

According to recent industry data (knowledge-cutoff verified):

- **Halal food market**: ~**USD 2.5 trillion** globally, driven by ~**1.9 billion** Muslim consumers.
- **Kosher food market**: ~**USD 24 billion** in the U.S. alone, consumed by ~**12 million** Jewish Americans and ~**15 million** Kosher-observant non-Jewish consumers.
- **Overlap market (dual certified)**: products bearing **both Halal and Kosher symbols** are a growing niche, projected to grow at a **CAGR of ~6–8%** in EU and ASEAN markets.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering Implication:** This growth is driving **automation engineering** innovations such as **AI-driven ingredient scanners** that flag non-compliant E-numbers in real-time and **blockchain-based traceability** systems that immutably record every batch's compliance audit trail.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Set]
**Q: Define Halal. List any four classes of substances that are considered Haram in Islamic dietary law.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Definition (1 Mark):** Halal is an Arabic term meaning "permissible" or "lawful" and refers to food and beverages that are allowed for consumption under Islamic Shariah law, as derived from the Quran and Hadith.
2. **Four classes of Haram substances (2 Marks):**
   - **Pork and its derivatives** (e.g., gelatin, lard, porcine enzymes).
   - **Alcohol** and intoxicants (e.g., ethanol, wine, beer used as solvents).
   - **Blood and blood products** (in their raw or unprocessed forms).
   - **Carnivorous animals and birds of prey** (e.g., lions, eagles, wolves).
   - *Optional add-on: Animals not slaughtered according to Dhabihah.*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Set]
**Q: What is the significance of the "OU" symbol on a food product? How does it differ from the "JAKIM" symbol?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **OU Symbol (1.5 Marks):** "OU" stands for **Orthodox Union**, the largest Kosher certification agency in the world. A product bearing the OU symbol has been verified to comply with Jewish dietary laws (Kashrut), including ingredient sourcing, equipment segregation, and process supervision by a certified Mashgiach.
2. **JAKIM Symbol (1.5 Marks):** "JAKIM" refers to the **Department of Islamic Development Malaysia**, a Malaysian government body that certifies Halal products. It is widely recognized as a global benchmark for Halal compliance and signifies adherence to MS 1500:2019 standards, including Dhabihah slaughter, Najis avoidance, and alcohol-free processing.
3. **Key Difference:** OU is **Kosher** (Jewish), JAKIM is **Halal** (Islamic), and they have different slaughter requirements, certifying authorities, and audit protocols.
